title: [Inductor] Mismatch between eager and compiled outputs when converting fp32 → bf16 → fp32
labels: triage review, module: bfloat16, module: correctness (silent), oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ### Issue Description When compiling a function that performs a round-trip type conversion (fp32 → bfloat16 → fp32) using `torch.compile()`, the output differs from the eager mode execution. ### Reproduction Code ```python import torch class CastModule(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() def forward(self, x): bf16_t = x.to(torch.bfloat16) fp32_casted_t = bf16_t.to(torch.float32) return fp32_casted_t def func(compile): torch.manual_seed(1234) x = torch.randn(4, 4).cuda() cast_module = CastModule().cuda() if compile: fp32_casted_t = torch.compile(cast_module)(x) else: fp32_casted_t = cast_module(x) return fp32_casted_t def compare(): torch._inductor.config.force_disable_caches = True eager_out = func(compile=False) compiled_out = func(compile=True) assert torch.equal(eager_out, compiled_out), f"Mismatch between eager and compiled outputs: {eager_out} vs {compiled_out}" if __name__ == "__main__": compare() ``` The assertion fails with a mismatch between eager and compiled outputs. ### Generated Triton Kernel I tried to disable the registered replace pattern `pointless_convert` in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_inductor/fx_passes/joint_graph.py#L761, and get the following generated triton kernel: ```python @triton_heuristics.pointwise( size_hints={'x': 16}, filename=__file__, triton_meta={'signature': {'in_ptr0': '*fp32', 'out_ptr0': '*fp32', 'xnumel': 'i32', 'XBLOCK': 'constexpr'}, 'device': DeviceProperties(type='cuda', index=0, multi_processor_count=78, cc=90, major=9, regs_per_multiprocessor=65536, max_threads_per_multi_processor=2048, warp_size=32), 'constants': {}, 'configs': [{(0,): [['tt.divisibility', 16]], (1,): [['tt.divisibility', 16]], (2,): [['tt.divisibility', 16]]}]}, inductor_meta={'grid_type': 'Grid1D', 'autotune_hints': set(), 'kernel_name': 'triton_poi_fused__to_copy_0', 'mutated_arg_names': [], 'optimize_mem': True, 'no_x_dim': False, 'num_load': 1, 'num_reduction': 0, 'backend_hash': '03ABE04230B1E328310427D5399BB7600AAE91F41BEED9AB62FE9C801110B6A5', 'are_deterministic_algorithms_enabled': False, 'assert_indirect_indexing': True, 'autotune_local_cache': True, 'autotune_pointwise': True, 'autotune_remote_cache': None, 'force_disable_caches': True, 'dynamic_scale_rblock': True, 'max_autotune': False, 'max_autotune_pointwise': False, 'min_split_scan_rblock': 256, 'spill_threshold': 16, 'store_cubin': False, 'tiling_scores': {'x': 192}}, min_elem_per_thread=0 ) @triton.jit def triton_poi_fused__to_copy_0(in_ptr0, out_ptr0, xnumel, XBLOCK : tl.constexpr): xnumel = 16 xoffset = tl.program_id(0) * XBLOCK xindex = xoffset + tl.arange(0, XBLOCK)[:] xmask = xindex < xnumel x0 = xindex tmp0 = tl.load(in_ptr0 + (x0), xmask) tmp1 = tmp0.to(tl.float32) # It should be tmp0.to(tl.bfloat16) to make it align with eager run? tmp2 = tmp1.to(tl.float32) tl.store(out_ptr0 + (x0), tmp2, xmask) ``` The generated kernel completely skips the fp32 → bf16 → fp32 conversion chain. It directly loads fp32 values and stores them back as fp32, which is inconsistent with eager mode. While the Inductor's optimization might produce numerically better results, we have a use case that requires strict bitwise equivalence with eager mode. Is there a way to disable this specific optimization or enforce that type casts are preserved in the generated code? cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @yanboliang ### Versions torch 2.9.1

title: [Torch Inductor] cache key issue when compile and tensor parallel are both enabled
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2

title: vLLM tlparse half cut off
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, vllm-compile, module: vllm
body: @engineer_21_gh reported a vLLM tlparse that was half cut off. I was unable to repro. I have seen this elsewhere. We should figure out why these are half cut off because they hamper observability. cc @chauhang @penguinwu

title: export: `float8_e8m0fnu` fails to save
labels: good first issue, oncall: pt2, module: floatx (formerly float8), oncall: export, bot-triaged

= torch.ones(2, 3) ep = torch.export.export(M(), (x,), strict=False) torch.export.save(ep, "model.pt2") ``` Error logs ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File title: torch.compile does not correctly invoke property setter during object initialization
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug torch.compile produces incorrect results when a class property setter is triggered during object initialization. In eager execution, assigning to a property inside __init__ correctly invokes the property's setter and applies its logic. However, under torch.compile, the setter appears to be bypassed, leading to inconsistent behavior between eager and compiled execution. ```python import torch def test_property_setter_during_init(): class Clamped: def __init__(self, val): self.value = val @property def value(self): return self._value @value.setter def value(self, v): self._value = max(0, min(v, 100)) # clamp to [0, 100] def fn(x): c = Clamped(int(x.item())) return torch.tensor(float(c.value)) for val in [torch.tensor(50.0), torch.tensor(200.0), torch.tensor(-10.0)]: eager = fn(val) compiled = torch.compile(fn, backend="eager", fullgraph=False)(val) assert torch.equal(eager, compiled), \\ f"For {val}: expected {eager}, got {compiled}" test_property_setter_during_init() ``` ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... PyTorch version: 2.10.0.dev20251021+cu128 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.8 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 22.04.4 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1~22.04) 11.4.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: version 3.22.1 Libc version: glibc-2.35 Python version: 3.10.19 (main, Oct 21 2025, 16:43:05) [GCC 11.2.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-6.8.0-40-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.35 Is CUDA available: True CUDA runtime version: 11.8.89 CUDA_MODULE_LOADING set to: GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: NVIDIA RTX A6000 GPU 1: NVIDIA RTX A6000 Nvidia driver version: 535.183.01 cuDNN version: Could not collect Is XPU available: False HIP runtime version: N/A MIOpen runtime version: N/A Is XNNPACK available: True title: [dynamo] Property getter using `__dict__.get()` returns stale value inside `@torch.compiler.disable` in nightlies
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When a property getter reads an attribute via `self.__dict__.get("attr", default)` instead of `self.attr`, dynamo doesn't track the mutation. If the property is read from a `@torch.compiler.disable`-decorated method later in the same compiled frame, it returns the stale (pre-mutation) value. This is a regression — it passes on `2.11.0.dev20260116` and fails on `2.12.0.dev20260305`. ### Minimal repro ```python import torch from torch.compiler import disable class Container: def __init__(self): self._len_value = 0 @property def _len(self): # Using __dict__.get instead of self._len_value triggers the bug return self.__dict__.get("_len_value", 0) @_len.setter def _len(self, value): self._len_value = value def add(self, n): self._len = self._len + n @disable() def __len__(self): return self._len c = Container() @torch.compile(backend="eager") def f(x): c.add(x.shape[0]) # mutates c._len_value via property setter return len(c) # reads c._len via @disable -> property getter -> __dict__.get result = f(torch.randn(5)) assert result == 5, f"Expected 5, got {result}" ``` Note: The issue is specifically `__dict__.get()` in the property getter. Replacing it with direct attribute access (`return self._len_value`) or `getattr` makes the test pass. cc @chauhang @penguinwu @kurtamohler ### Versions - Passes: `torch 2.10.0`, `torch 2.11.0.dev20260116` - Fails: `torch 2.12.0.dev20260305`

title: [opaque objects] custom_op + Reference OpaqueObject is not CUDAGraphable.
labels: triaged, module: custom-operators, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2, module: pt2-dispatcher
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug My impression is that custom_op is by default assumed to be cudagraphable (and user can opt out of it by specifying [ cudagraph_unsafe tag](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/65762ca85745d786ab6b20e9cb060242b51e872d/aten/src/ATen/native/tags.yaml#L91-L95)). So, I would expect custom_op + reference opaque object to also be cudagraphable. Repro: ```python """ custom_op + reference-type OpaqueObject silently falls back to eager like kernel launch under mode="reduce-overhead" — CUDA graphs are NOT used. Reference-type opaque objects are stored as torchbind_constants and passed as FakeScriptObject inputs to the compiled partition. The cudagraph eligibility check in CompiledFxGraph.__init__ (output_code.py:596-601) rejects non-Tensor inputs: all(isinstance(t, (torch.Tensor, torch.SymInt, torch.Generator)) for t in example_inputs) The FakeScriptObject fails this check, producing fail reason "non-Tensor inputs". Then cudagraph_partition_post_compile (output_code.py:296-304) sees the fail reason and disables cudagraphs. """ import torch from torch._library.opaque_object import MemberType, OpaqueBase, register_opaque_type class State(OpaqueBase): def __init__(self, scale: float): self.scale = scale register_opaque_type( State, typ="reference", guard_fn=lambda obj: [obj.scale], ) @torch.library.custom_op("refop::scaled_add", mutates_args=[]) def scaled_add(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, state: State) -> torch.Tensor: return x + y * state.scale @scaled_add.register_fake def _(x, y, state): return torch.empty_like(x) def fn(x, y, state): return torch.ops.refop.scaled_add(x, y, state) + x.sin().cos() + y.exp() x = torch.randn(64, device="cuda") y = torch.randn(64, device="cuda") state = State(scale=0.5) # cudagraph_or_error does NOT catch this (seperate issue) torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraph_or_error = True compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, mode="reduce-overhead", fullgraph=True) for _ in range(3): compiled_fn(x, y, state) # verify: no cudaGraphLaunch in profile with torch.profiler.profile( activities=[torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CPU, torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CUDA], ) as prof: for _ in range(3): compiled_fn(x, y, state) cg = [e for e in prof.events() if "cudaGraphLaunch" in e.name] print(f"cudaGraphLaunch events: {len(cg)}") assert len(cg) == 0, "Expected 0 cudaGraphLaunch — reference-type opaque silently skips cudagraphs" print() print("Root cause:") print(" 1. FakeScriptObject in example_inputs fails isinstance(t, (Tensor, SymInt, Generator))") print(" -> cudagraph_fail_reasons = ['non-Tensor inputs'] (output_code.py:596-601)") print(" 2. cudagraph_partition_post_compile silently disables cudagraphs (output_code.py:296-304)") print(" -> no log_cudagraph_skip_and_bump_counter call -> no counter/error") ``` Value type OpaqueObject works fine <details> ```python """ custom_op + value-type OpaqueObject is correctly cudagraphed under mode="reduce-overhead". Value-type opaque objects are reconstructed inline in codegen via __fx_repr__ and never appear as graph inputs, so they pass the "all inputs are Tensor" check in CompiledFxGraph. """ import torch from torch._library.opaque_object import OpaqueBase, register_opaque_type class Config(OpaqueBase): def __init__(self, alpha: float): self.alpha = alpha def __eq__(self, other): return isinstance(other, Config) and self.alpha == other.alpha def __hash__(self): return hash(self.alpha) def __fx_repr__(self): return f"Config(alpha={self.alpha!r})", {"Config": Config} register_opaque_type(Config, typ="value") @torch.library.custom_op("valop::scaled_add", mutates_args=[]) def scaled_add(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor, cfg: Config) -> torch.Tensor: return x + y * cfg.alpha @scaled_add.register_fake def _(x, y, cfg): return torch.empty_like(x) def fn(x, y, cfg): return torch.ops.valop.scaled_add(x, y, cfg) + x.sin().cos() + y.exp() x = torch.randn(64, device="cuda") y = torch.randn(64, device="cuda") cfg = Config(alpha=0.5) compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, mode="reduce-overhead", fullgraph=True) # warmup + replay for _ in range(3): compiled_fn(x, y, cfg) # verify cudaGraphLaunch appears in profile with torch.profiler.profile( activities=[torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CPU, torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CUDA], ) as prof: for _ in range(3): compiled_fn(x, y, cfg) cg = [e for e in prof.events() if "cudaGraphLaunch" in e.name] print(f"cudaGraphLaunch events: {len(cg)}") assert len(cg) > 0, "Expected cudaGraphLaunch events — value-type opaque should be cudagraphed" print("PASS: value-type OpaqueObject is cudagraphed") ``` </details> ### Versions main cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: Security hardening: Add HMAC integrity verification and authentication support to torch.compile Redis remote cache
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, security, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch The torch.compile Redis remote cache (FxGraphCache, AOTAutogradCache, AutotuneCache, BundledAutotuneCache, DynamoPGOCache) uses pickle.loads() on data retrieved from Redis with no integrity verification, no authentication, and no encryption. This was discussed in a prior security report (GHSA-439w-rqx3-m624). The maintainers noted that distributed features assume trusted networks per SECURITY.md and suggested this hardening be discussed as a public feature request. The problem in practice: While the threat model assumes trusted networks, real-world distributed training deployments often run on shared infrastructure - Kubernetes clusters with multiple teams, cloud ML platforms with shared VPCs, university HPC clusters with hundreds of users on the same network. In these environments, the Redis cache becomes a single point of compromise. What an attacker with Redis network access can do: An attacker writes a malicious pickle payload to a cache key like pt2:fx-graph-v1::<hash>:c1. When any worker calls torch.compile() and hits that cache entry, codecache.py L1104 calls pickle.loads(content) - executing the attacker's code as the training process user. From there, the attacker can: - Steal training data - exfiltrate whatever datasets the training job has access to, including any sensitive or proprietary data - Poison model weights - silently modify the model during training so it produces attacker-chosen outputs for specific inputs, then the poisoned model gets deployed to production - Harvest cloud credentials - read AWS keys, GCP service account tokens, or Azure credentials from environment variables or instance metadata, pivoting from the training cluster to the broader cloud account - Persist across workers - the poisoned cache key stays in Redis. Every worker that compiles the same graph executes the payload. Killing one worker doesn't help - the next one picks up the same entry - Operate invisibly - torch.compile() doesn't log cache hits, Redis doesn't log reads, the pickle executes inside the existing Python process. Training completes normally. Metrics look fine. No alerts. This is confirmed with a working PoC - full RCE achieved against a Docker Redis instance in under 30 seconds from network access to code execution. This is a known vulnerability pattern in ML frameworks. The same issue led to CVEs in MLflow (CVE-2024-27132), Ray (CVE-2023-6019), and Hugging Face Datasets. Every ML framework that combines pickle deserialization with unauthenticated network caching has eventually needed to address this. Vulnerable code paths: - torch/_inductor/codecache.py L1104: pickle.loads(content) - no HMAC, no signature - torch/_inductor/remote_cache.py L264-266: Redis(host=..., port=...) - no auth, no SSL - 5 cache types affected, all using the same unauthenticated pattern Proposed hardening: - HMAC-SHA256 signing - sign payloads on cache write, verify before pickle.loads() on read. Key configured via TORCH_REMOTE_CACHE_HMAC_SECRET environment variable. Without the correct key, tampered cache entries are rejected. - Authentication and SSL support - expose password and ssl parameters in the RemoteCache constructor, configurable via TORCH_REMOTE_CACHE_PASSWORD and TORCH_REMOTE_CACHE_SSL environment variables. - Documentation update - add a security note to the distributed caching documentation explaining the risks of running Redis without authentication on shared networks and recommending the HMAC + auth configuration. ### Alternatives - Replace pickle with a safe serialization format - use safetensors, structured bytes, or JSON for metadata with raw compiled artifacts. Eliminates the deserialization attack surface entirely but requires more significant refactoring. - RestrictedUnpickler - implement a custom Unpickler subclass that only allows known-safe classes. Reduces risk but pickle's attack surface is broad enough that this isn't fully reliable. - Status quo with documentation - keep the current implementation but add explicit security warnings to the docs. Lowest effort but provides no technical mitigation. ### Additional context - Working PoC and full technical details: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/security/advisories/GHSA-439w-rqx3-m624 - Affected code: torch/_inductor/codecache.py L1104, torch/_inductor/remote_cache.py L264-266 - Similar CVEs in other ML frameworks: MLflow CVE-2024-27132, Ray CVE-2023-6019 cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [Inductor] `ForeachKernelSchedulerNode.reorder_loops_by_dep_pair` crashes with `AssertionError` when snodes contain nested `ForeachKernelSchedulerNode`
labels: high priority, module: crash, module: mta, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `ForeachKernelSchedulerNode.reorder_loops_by_dep_pair` unconditionally asserts `isinstance(snode, SchedulerNode)` when iterating over `self.snodes`. After fusion, `snodes` can contain `ForeachKernelSchedulerNode` instances, which are **not** subclasses of `SchedulerNode` (both are siblings under `BaseSchedulerNode`). This causes an `AssertionError` during the `loop_ordering_after_fusion` pass in the scheduler. The crash is triggered when a compiled function mixes `torch._foreach_*` operations with per-tensor reductions (e.g., `Tensor.sum()`) over tensors of **different shapes** on CUDA. A manual for loop works, and is what [HeavyBall](https://github.com/HomebrewML/HeavyBall) will now use instead. ## Minimal reproducer ```python import torch from typing import List from torch import Tensor @torch.compile(fullgraph=True) def repro( params: List[Tensor], state1: List[Tensor], state2: List[Tensor], grads: List[Tensor], ): g32 = [g.float() for g in grads] # foreach: lerp state1 s1 = [s.float() for s in state1] torch._foreach_lerp_(s1, g32, 0.1) for s, s_ in zip(state1, s1): s.copy_(s_) # foreach: lerp state2 s2 = [s.float() for s in state2] torch._foreach_lerp_(s2, g32, 0.001) for s, s_ in zip(state2, s2): s.copy_(s_) # foreach: combine updates = torch._foreach_div(s1, s2) # per-tensor with reduction — triggers the bug for p, u, g in zip(params, updates, g32): mask = g.signbit() ^ u.signbit() u = u.masked_fill(mask, 0) scale = mask.numel() / (mask.numel() - mask.sum()).clamp(min=1) p.add_(u * scale) torch.manual_seed(42) # Different shapes are required to trigger the bug params = [torch.randn(128, 128, device="cuda"), torch.randn(128, device="cuda")] state1 = [torch.ones_like(p) for p in params] state2 = [torch.ones_like(p) for p in params] grads = [torch.randn_like(p) for p in params] repro(params, state1, state2, grads) ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.12.0.dev20260304+cu130 Python version: 3.11.7 CUDA runtime version: 13.0.88 Is CUDA available: True OS: Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS (x86_64) cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @crcrpar @mcarilli @janeyx99 @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: RuntimeError: Event device type CPU does not match recording stream's device type
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ``` import torch event = torch.cuda.Event() @torch.compile def f(x): event.record() return x + 1 f(torch.tensor(1.0, device="cuda")) ``` The error disappears if you remove the `@torch.compile`. ### Error logs ``` Exit code 1 Traceback (most recent call last): File "/mnt/clusterstorage/workspace/kevin/ml-monorepo/basedformer/tests/test_cuda_event_compile.py", line 11, in <module> f(torch.tensor(1.0, device="cuda")) File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 953, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) File "/mnt/clusterstorage/workspace/kevin/ml-monorepo/basedformer/tests/test_cuda_event_compile.py", line 6, in f @torch.compile File "/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", title: rnumel not correctly parenthesized in torch/_inductor/codegen/simd.py line 1772
labels: high priority, triage review, triaged, module: codegen, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: Repro: ``` """ Repro for mix-order reduction operator precedence bug where rnumel is an additive symbolic expression (e.g. s0 + s1). The bug is in torch/_inductor/codegen/simd.py line 1772: stride_str = f"{nsplit} * {rnumel}" When rnumel is "s0 + s1", the generated wrapper code becomes: workspace[0 * 2048 * s0 + s1 : (0 + 1) * 2048 * s0 + s1].view(2048, s0 + s1) Due to Python operator precedence, this evaluates as: workspace[s1 : 2048*s0 + s1] => slice size = 2048*s0 But .view(2048, s0+s1) expects 2048*(s0+s1) = 2048*s0 + 2048*s1 elements. RuntimeError: shape '[2048, 640]' is invalid for input of size 786432 (2048*640=1310720 != 2048*384=786432) The fix is: stride_str = f"({nsplit}) * ({rnumel})" Run with: buck run fbcode//scripts/engineer_17:repro_mix_order_rnumel """ import sys import torch import torch._dynamo import torch._inductor.config as inductor_config from torch._inductor import metrics def main(): assert torch.cuda.is_available(), "This repro requires CUDA" # Force-enable mix-order reduction (may be off by default in some builds) inductor_config.triton.mix_order_reduction = True B = 32768 s0_val = 384 # use different sizes so compiler assigns distinct symbols s1_val = 256 D = s0_val + s1_val # 640 def f(x1, x2, w_pool, eps): # cat produces [B, s0+s1] -- additive symbolic last dim x = torch.cat([x1, x2], dim=1) # Slice w_pool to match dynamic dim. This avoids a guard that would # resolve s0+s1 to a concrete value (640), which is essential for # keeping rnumel symbolic in the generated code. w = w_pool[: x.shape[-1]] # [s0+s1], no concrete-equality guard orig_dtype = x.dtype x = x.float() # Manual rms_norm: backward produces two reductions with reversed # (numel, rnumel) that trigger mix-order reduction. # grad_w: reduces over B (dim=0) -> numel=s0+s1, rnumel=B # grad_input: reduces over D (dim=1) -> numel=B, rnumel=s0+s1 rsqrt = torch.rsqrt((x * x).sum(dim=-1) / x.shape[-1] + eps) y = (x * rsqrt[:, None] * w).to(dtype=orig_dtype) return y def fwd_bwd(compile_fn): x1.grad = None x2.grad = None w_pool.grad = None out = compile_fn(x1, x2, w_pool, eps) out.backward(dy) return x1.grad, x2.grad, w_pool.grad x1 = torch.randn( B, s0_val, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda", requires_grad=True ) x2 = torch.randn( B, s1_val, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda", requires_grad=True ) # w_pool is oversized; slicing inside the compiled fn avoids exact-size guard w_pool = torch.randn(1000, dtype=torch.float32, device="cuda", requires_grad=True) dy = torch.randn(B, D, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda") eps = 1e-5 # Mark dim-1 as dynamic so the compiler tracks separate symbols s0, s1 torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(x1, 1) torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(x2, 1) metrics.reset() ref = fwd_bwd(f) sys.stderr.write("Compiling with torch.compile ...\\n") opt_f = torch.compile(f) sys.stderr.write("Running compiled fwd+bwd ...\\n") try: act = fwd_bwd(opt_f) except RuntimeError as e: print(f"BUG REPRODUCED! RuntimeError: {e}") print( "\\nThis is the operator precedence bug in " "torch/_inductor/codegen/simd.py _codegen_mix_order_reduction" ) print( 'Fix: stride_str = f"({nsplit}) * ({rnumel})" ' "(parenthesize both nsplit and rnumel)" ) return print(f"Mix order reductions triggered: {metrics.codegen_mix_order_reduction}") if metrics.codegen_mix_order_reduction == 0: print("WARNING: mix-order reduction was NOT triggered.") else: from torch._dynamo.utils import same match_result = same(ref, act, tol=1e-2) print(f"Results match: {match_result}") if match_result: print("No crash and results match -- bug may already be fixed") if __name__ == "__main__": main() ``` cc @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @bhosmer @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @chauhang @penguinwu @aditvenk @laithsakka @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [torch.export] ExportedProgram.run_decompositions breaks backward for self attention

aligned (strideH)`. ```py import torch from torchvision.models import VisionTransformer device = "cuda" inner_model = VisionTransformer( image_size=224, patch_size=16, num_layers=12, num_heads=6, hidden_dim=384, mlp_dim=4 * 384, num_classes=1000, ).to(device) sample_args = (torch.rand([10, 3, 224, 224], device=device),) program = torch.export.export(inner_model, args=sample_args) program = title: Triton kernels with 2 or more tl.load() calls segfault at runtime on sm_120 (NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell). The kernels compile without errors but produce invalid code that crashes on execution.
labels: needs reproduction, module: crash, module: cuda, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, upstream triton, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Triton kernels with 2 or more tl.load() calls segfault at runtime on sm_120 (NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell). The kernels compile without errors but produce invalid code that crashes on execution. Reproduce: import torch import triton import triton.language as tl @triton.jit def _two_loads(X, Y, N: tl.constexpr): offs = tl.arange(0, N) x1 = tl.load(X + offs) x2 = tl.load(X + offs) # second load causes segfault tl.store(Y + offs, x1 + x2) x = torch.ones(128, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float32) y = torch.zeros(128, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float32) # This segfaults: _two_loads[(1,)](x, y, N=128) A single-load kernel works fine on the same GPU: @triton.jit def _one_load(X, Y, N: tl.constexpr): offs = tl.arange(0, N) x = tl.load(X + offs) tl.store(Y + offs, x * 2.0) # This works: _one_load[(1,)](x, y, N=128) Findings from bisection ┌───────────────────────────────────────┬──────────┐ │ Condition │ Result │ ├───────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┤ │ 1 tl.load, any num_warps, any BLOCK_N │ PASS │ ├───────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┤ │ 2 tl.load, num_warps=1, BLOCK_N=128 │ PASS │ ├───────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┤ │ 2 tl.load, num_warps=2, BLOCK_N=128 │ SEGFAULT │ ├───────────────────────────────────────┼──────────┤ │ 2 tl.load, num_warps=1, BLOCK_N=256 │ SEGFAULT │ └───────────────────────────────────────┴──────────┘ The issue appears to be in multi-warp code generation and/or register allocation for sm_120. With num_warps=1 the threshold is very low (BLOCK_N ≤ 128), making any real-world kernel unusable. Environment - GPU: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition (sm_120, 188 SMs, 96GB GDDR7) - Triton: 3.6.0 - PyTorch: 2.12.0.dev20260304+cu128 (also reproduced with 2.10.0+cu128) - CUDA toolkit: 12.8.0 - OS: Ubuntu 22.04 (container: nvidia/cuda:12.8.0-devel-ubuntu22.04) - Driver: supports sm_120 (single-load kernels run correctly) This blocks all non-trivial Triton kernel usage on sm_120 GPUs. Specifically, we were trying to run custom Triton inference kernels for Qwen3.5-27B that achieve 92.5 tok/s on sm_100 (B200). Every kernel requires 2+ loads (input + weights at minimum) and BLOCK_N ≥ 4096, so none can execute on sm_120. body: This is an issue surfaced during TorchInductor benchmark checklist for the upcoming 2.11 release in which `openai/whisper-tiny` regresses its accuracy check from pass to fail_accuracy on H100 * In 2.10, the check passed: ``` pip install torch==2.10.0 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/test/cu130 git checkout release/2.10 python benchmarks/dynamo/huggingface.py --accuracy title: torch.export.load() slow due to Python-side schema deserialization
labels: oncall: pt2, oncall: export, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ## 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch `torch.export.load()` is roughly 10x slower than `torch.jit.load()` for equivalent models. For a 24-layer transformer (~100M params), `torch.jit.load()` takes ~62ms while `torch.export.load()` takes ~650ms. Profiling shows the bottleneck is Python-side JSON parsing and recursive dataclass construction in `_dict_to_dataclass` / `_bytes_to_dataclass`, which accounts for ~53% of load time (JSON parsing ~8%, type introspection + dataclass construction ~45%). This is a problem for production serving where models are loaded frequently, and especially for multi-threaded loading where the GIL is held during the entire Python deserialization phase, preventing any parallelism. The C++ infrastructure to eliminate this bottleneck already exists: `generated_serialization_types.h` contains auto-generated C++ types with nlohmann::json support for every schema dataclass, and `torch._C._export.deserialize_exported_program()` already performs C++ JSON parsing and schema construction. However, the C++ types are registered as opaque pybind11 types with no field access from Python, so `GraphModuleDeserializer` can't use them. Exposing field access on the C++ schema types (via pybind11 property bindings) would allow the load path to use C++ deserialization, dropping schema construction from ~530ms to ~31ms and overall load time from ~650ms to ~366ms. **Benchmark (24-layer transformer, CPU):** | Scenario | jit.load | export.load (current) | export.load (C++ schema) | |----------|----------|-----------------------|--------------------------| | Single load | 0.062s | 0.650s | 0.366s | ## Alternatives - Caching `_dict_to_dataclass` type introspection results (already done, helps but still leaves ~45% of load time in Python deserialization) - Rewriting `GraphModuleDeserializer` in C++ (much larger scope, deeply coupled to `torch.fx` Python APIs) ## Additional context - Tested on PyTorch built from main (commit f61180da704) - Separate from GIL contention in tensor I/O during `torch.export.load()`, which is tracked independently - Related thread-safety issue: parallel `torch.export.load()` fails due to `_CURRENT_DESERIALIZER` global state in `GraphModuleDeserializer` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4

title: [inductor] cudagraph trees breaks with partitioned graphs with nested inputs
labels: module: crash, triaged, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: higher order operators, module: pt2-dispatcher, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile(mode="reduce-overhead")` crashes with `check_memory_pool` errors when the compiled function uses higher-order ops (`while_loop`, `scan`) that cause Inductor to partition the graph into subgraphs. ### To reproduce ```python import torch from torch._higher_order_ops.while_loop import while_loop torch._inductor.config.triton.slow_path_cudagraph_asserts = True w = torch.randn(16, 16, device="cuda") b = torch.randn(16, device="cuda") def f(x): y = x @ w def cond_fn(i, carry): return i < 3 def body_fn(i, carry): return i + 1, carry + b _, y = while_loop(cond_fn, body_fn, (torch.tensor(0, device="cuda"), y)) return y x = torch.randn(4, 16, device="cuda") compiled = torch.compile(f, mode="reduce-overhead", fullgraph=True) for i in range(3): result = compiled(x) ``` ### Error ``` RuntimeError: These storage data ptrs are not allocated in pool (0, 1) but should be: {<address>}. This could be a bug in inductor aliasing tracking or in a custom op's meta function. Please file an issue. ``` ### Root cause When `while_loop` (or `scan`) triggers graph partitioning, the Inductor-generated code passes the loop's carried state between partitions as a **Python list of tensors**, not as individual tensor arguments. For example, the generated `call` method does: ```python partition1_args = [buf2,] # buf2 is a list of tensors (buf4,) = self.partitions[1](partition1_args) ``` The CUDA graph tree infrastructure (`cudagraph_trees.py`) only inspects top-level elements of `new_inputs` via `isinstance(t, torch.Tensor)`. Since `buf2` is a list, all the tensors inside it are invisible to the warmup node's input tracking. When partition_1 passes one of those tensors through as an output, `add_ref` incorrectly tracks it as a pool allocation, and `check_memory_pool` rejects it. The same blind spot exists in `CUDAGraphNode._record` (`_allocate_and_copy_recording_inputs` asserts non-tensor inputs must be `int` or `Generator`, crashing on lists), and in the pre-record `check_memory_pool` call. ### Expected behavior `torch.compile(mode="reduce-overhead")` should work correctly with `while_loop` and other higher-order ops that trigger graph partitioning. cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @ydwu4 @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03 ### Versions - PyTorch: nightly (main) - GPU: NVIDIA H200 - CUDA: 12.x - Python: 3.12

title: Exporting a module with dynamic shape an example input the shape shape as the min boundry causes a failure
labels: oncall: pt2, oncall: export, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When trying to export a module with dynamic shape, the export will fail if the example input uses a shape of the lower bound: ```py import torch # Simple model class MLP(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x): return torch.relu(x @ x.T) model = MLP().eval().cuda() # Define inputs with dynamic batch dim inputs = [torch.randn(1, 128, device="cuda")] # An example input that is the same shape as min shape dynamic_shapes = ({0: torch.export.Dim("batch", min=1, max=32)},) # Explicitly set the dynamic shape for tracing # Export with dynamic shapes exp_program = torch.export.export(model, tuple(inputs), dynamic_shapes=dynamic_shapes) # This line fails ``` You get the following output ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/export/_trace.py", line 2031, in _export_to_aten_ir_make_fx produce_guards_callback(gm) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^ File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/export/_trace.py", line 2189, in _produce_guards_callback return produce_guards_and_solve_constraints( fake_mode=fake_mode, ...<3 lines>... original_signature=original_signature, ) File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/_export/non_strict_utils.py", line 615, in produce_guards_and_solve_constraints raise constraint_violation_error File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/_export/non_strict_utils.py", line 574, in produce_guards_and_solve_constraints shape_env.produce_guards( ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^ placeholders, ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ...<3 lines>... ignore_static=False, ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ) ^ File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py", line 5485, in produce_guards return self.produce_guards_verbose(*args, **kwargs, langs=("python",))[0].exprs ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/naren/pytorch_org/tensorrt/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/torch/fx/experimental/symbolic_shapes.py", line 6242, in produce_guards_verbose raise ConstraintViolationError( ...<3 lines>... ) torch.fx.experimental.symbolic_shapes.ConstraintViolationError: Constraints violated (batch)! If you're using mark_dynamic, either remove it or use maybe_mark_dynamic. If you're using Dim.DYNAMIC, replace it with either Dim.STATIC or Dim.AUTO. Suggested fixes: batch = 1 The error above occurred when calling torch.export.export. If you would like to view some more information about this error, and get a list of all other errors that may occur in your export call, you can replace your `export()` call with `draft_export()`. ``` Not fully sure its a bug or some misuse but it feels like something that should work Related issue: https://github.com/pytorch/TensorRT/issues/4103 ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: [inductor] CUDA graph outputs fed back as inputs crash with "overwritten by a subsequent run"
labels: module: cuda, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ### Description When CUDA graph outputs are fed back as inputs on the next call (the standard RL/simulation loop pattern `state = compiled_step(state)`), `mode="reduce-overhead"` crashes with: ``` RuntimeError: Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run. ``` There are two manifestations of this bug depending on whether the compiled function is partitioned: **Single partition (output->input feedback):** The crash occurs inside `CUDAGraphNode._record` or `_copy_inputs_and_remove_from_src` when `dealloc_current_path_weakrefs` frees pool storage that the new inputs reference. On the generation transition (triggered by `cudagraph_mark_step_begin`), the tree manager frees all pool outputs from the previous path and sets error messages on their storage. If the new call's inputs point to that storage (because they ARE the previous call's outputs), any subsequent access raises. **Multiple partitions (cross-partition invalidation):** When a compiled function is split into multiple CUDA graph partitions (e.g. by `while_loop`), the first partition's `dealloc_current_path_weakrefs` frees ALL pool storage from the previous generation. Later partitions' inputs — drawn from the top-level arg list — may still reference that freed storage. The crash occurs in `copy_misaligned_inputs` or `deferred_cudagraphify` when a later partition tries to access `.data_ptr()` on an invalidated tensor. ### Reproducer ```python import torch @torch.compile(mode="reduce-overhead") def step(x): return x * 0.99 + 0.01 x = torch.randn(100, device="cuda") for _ in range(5): torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin() x = step(x) torch.cuda.synchronize() ``` ### Error ``` RuntimeError: Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run. ``` cc @ptrblck @msaroufim @eqy @jerryzh168 @tinglvv @nWEIdia @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @engineer_02 ### Versions Local build of main branch

title: torch.compiler.is_exporting() returns True during torch.compile
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, oncall: export
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The API reference of `torch.compiler.is_exporting` explains that "_It’s stricter than is_compiling() flag, as it would **only be set to True when torch.export is used.**_". However, currently **torch.compiler.is_exporting() also returns true during `torch.compile`**. Was this intended? This is causing unexpected trouble when compiling my model whose forward method contains an if-else branch conditioned by `torch.compiler.is_exporting()`, which I expected to be executed only during torch.export but not during torch.compile. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/1b157c7aa2af29c7a50b54e9c1f73cc2ad4f5510/torch/_export/utils.py#L1421-L1431 ### Minimal Reproducible Example ```py import torch class ZerosLike(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, input: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: if torch.compiler.is_exporting(): return torch.zeros_like(input, dtype=torch.float32) return torch.zeros_like(input, dtype=torch.bfloat16) @torch.no_grad() def main(): model = ZerosLike() x = torch.randn(10, 10) output_raw = model(x) print(f"Output dtype (raw model): {output_raw.dtype}") assert output_raw.dtype == torch.bfloat16 ep = torch.export.export(model, (x,)) output_after_export = ep.module()(x) print(f"Output dtype (torch.export): {output_after_export.dtype}") assert output_after_export.dtype == torch.float32 output_after_compile = torch.compile(model)(x) print(f"Output dtype (torch.compile): {output_after_compile.dtype}") print() assert output_after_compile.dtype == torch.bfloat16, \\ f"Expected output dtype to be bfloat16 after torch.compile, but got {output_after_compile.dtype}" if __name__ == "__main__": main() ``` #### Output ``` Output dtype (raw model): torch.bfloat16 Output dtype (torch.export): torch.float32 Output dtype (torch.compile): torch.float32 Traceback (most recent call last): File ".../main.py", line 33, in main() File ".../python3.10/site-packages/torch/utils/_contextlib.py", line 120, in decorate_context return func(*args, **kwargs) File ".../main.py", line 28, in main assert output_after_compile.dtype == torch.bfloat16, \\ AssertionError: Expected output dtype to be bfloat16 after torch.compile, but got torch.float32 ``` ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions ``` PyTorch version: 2.9.1+cpu Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: None ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS (aarch64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1~22.04.3) 11.4.0 Clang version: 14.0.0-1ubuntu1.1 CMake version: version 3.22.1 Libc version: glibc-2.35 Python version: 3.10.16 (main, Mar 18 2025, 12:09:19) [GCC 11.4.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-5.15.153.1-microsoft-standard-WSL2-aarch64-with-glibc2.35 Is CUDA available: False CUDA runtime version: No CUDA CUDA_MODULE_LOADING set to: N/A GPU models and configuration: No CUDA Nvidia driver version: No CUDA cuDNN version: No CUDA Is XPU available: False HIP runtime version: N/A MIOpen runtime version: N/A Is XNNPACK available: True Caching allocator config: N/A CPU: Architecture: title: empty AssertionError when use_deterministic_algorithms is toggled inside forward
labels: triaged, module: determinism, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged, bot-mislabeled
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Calling torch.use_deterministic_algorithms() inside a module's forward method causes torch.compile to raise a BackendCompilerFailed with an empty AssertionError (no message) Maybe this is not a proper usage, but i think more information should be provided to diagnose BTW, crashes only happens under torch.compile. Eager mode runs succcessfully ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class M(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) try: out = F.linear(x, torch.ones(4, 4)) marker = torch.tensor(1.0 if torch.are_deterministic_algorithms_enabled() else -1.0) finally: torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(False) return out + marker x = torch.randn(2, 4) torch.compile(M())(x) ``` ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: cpp_wrapper crashes with `CudaKernelParamCache not populated` in scaled_dot_product_attention
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile` with `options={"cpp_wrapper": True}` crashes during the backward pass when the compiled function contains `F.scaled_dot_product_attention`. ```python3 import torch import torch.nn.functional as F @torch.compile(options={"cpp_wrapper": True}) def fn(x): return F.scaled_dot_product_attention(x, x, x) x = torch.randn(2, 4, 16, 64, device="cuda", requires_grad=True) fn(x).sum().backward() ``` ### Ablation | Setting | Forward | Backward | |---|---|---| | `cpp_wrapper=False` | ✅ | ✅ | | `cpp_wrapper=True` | ✅ | ❌ crash | | `cpp_wrapper=True`, without backward | ✅ | - | | `cpp_wrapper=True`, simple op (x*2) | ✅ | ✅ | ### Error logs ```python3 Traceback (most recent call last): File "/tmp/reproduce.py", line 15, in <module> fn(x).sum().backward() ... File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 2533, in _compile_to_module self.codegen_with_cpp_wrapper() if self.cpp_wrapper else self.codegen() ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 2445, in codegen_with_cpp_wrapper return self.codegen() File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 2476, in codegen result = self.wrapper_code.generate(self.is_inference) File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/cpp_wrapper_gpu.py", line 559, in generate return super().generate(is_inference) File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/cpp_wrapper_cpu.py", line 1043, in generate return super().generate(is_inference) File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/wrapper.py", line 1787, in generate return self._generate(is_inference) File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/wrapper.py", line 1875, in _generate self.finalize_prefix() File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/cpp_wrapper_gpu.py", line 571, in finalize_prefix kernel.generate(self) File ".../torch/_inductor/codegen/cpp_wrapper_gpu.py", line 162, in generate assert params, f"CudaKernelParamCache not populated for {self.kernel_name}" ^^^^^^ torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: AssertionError: CudaKernelParamCache not populated for triton_poi_fused_add_0 ``` ### Versions <details> <summary>PyTorch version: 2.12.0.dev20260302+cu130</summary> title: export runtime error: Tensor specified that the operator does not mutate the argument, but this seems to be empirically wrong.
labels: oncall: pt2, export-triaged, oncall: export, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: When I export model, I used fbgemm op in model，I encountered this error. The error message is as follows. `RuntimeError: fbgemm::int_nbit_split_embedding_codegen_lookup_function: for argument 'indice_weights': the operator's schema fbgemm::int_nbit_split_embedding_codegen_lookup_function(Tensor dev_weights, Tensor uvm_weights, Tensor weights_placements, Tensor weights_offsets, Tensor weights_tys, Tensor D_offsets, SymInt total_D, int max_int2_D, int max_int4_D, int max_int8_D, int max_float16_D, int max_float32_D, Tensor indices, Tensor offsets, int pooling_mode, Tensor? indice_weights, int output_dtype=1, Tensor? lxu_cache_weights=None, Tensor? lxu_cache_locations=None, int? row_alignment=None, int? max_float8_D=0, int? fp8_exponent_bits=-1, int? fp8_exponent_bias=-1) -> Tensor specified that the operator does not mutate the argument, but this seems to be empirically wrong. Please make the schema and operator behavior consistent. You can specify that an operator mutates a Tensor by e.g. changing its schema type from 'Tensor name' to 'Tensor(a!) name'(use different identifiers (a, b, c, ...) for different Tensors)` cc @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4

title: TypeError in guard when working with list of tensors
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: guards
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug recompilation_reason_for_no_tensor_aliasing_guard in torch/_dynamo/guards.py crashes with TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable when list of tensors is changed to None ### To Reproduce ```python3 import torch @torch.compile def fn(a, b, data): x = a + b if data is not None: x = x + data[0] + data[1] return x t1, t2 = torch.randn(4), torch.randn(4) fn(t1, t2, [torch.randn(4), torch.randn(4)]) t = torch.randn(4) fn(t, t, None) # crashes in guard recompilation ``` ### Error logs ```python Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module> File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 953, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 2202, in __call__ result = self._torchdynamo_orig_backend( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1945, in __call__ result = self._inner_convert( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 707, in __call__ result = _compile( ^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1627, in _compile reasons = get_and_maybe_log_recompilation_reasons( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 4476, in get_and_maybe_log_recompilation_reasons reason = get_guard_fail_reason( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 4443, in get_guard_fail_reason reason_str = get_guard_fail_reason_helper( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 4404, in get_guard_fail_reason_helper reasons = recompilation_reason_for_no_tensor_aliasing_guard( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/home/user/.conda/envs/main_12/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 4317, in recompilation_reason_for_no_tensor_aliasing_guard tensor_id = id(eval(tensor_source, global_scope, scope)) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "<string>", line 1, in <module> TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not subscriptable ``` ### Versions <details> <summary>PyTorch version: 2.12.0.dev20260302+cu130</summary> ``` PyTorch version: 2.12.0.dev20260302+cu130 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 13.0 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 22.04.5 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 11.4.0-1ubuntu1~22.04.2) 11.4.0 Clang version: 14.0.0-1ubuntu1.1 CMake version: version 3.22.1 Libc version: glibc-2.35 Python version: 3.12.12 | packaged by Anaconda, Inc. | title: StreamContextVariable's create_proxy logic removed in Dynamo post-2.9, breaking custom backends relying on set_stream nodes
labels: triaged, module: regression, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged, bot-mislabeled, module: compiled streams
body: I observed that in PyTorch 2.10, the enter and exit methods of StreamContextVariablein the Dynamo component have removed the logic for creating proxies via tx.output.create_proxy. In contrast, PyTorch 2.9 retains this logic: ``` def enter(self, tx): # stream generated inside the traced function if self.target_values[0].as_proxy() is not None: tx.output.create_proxy( "call_function", self.set_stream, (self.target_values[0].as_proxy(),), {}, ) ``` This change causes the set_stream nodes to disappear from FX graphs in PyTorch 2.10+, whereas they were present in 2.9. related pr maybe: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/166472 **Example** ``` import torch def my_backend(gm, example_inputs): print(gm.graph) return gm @torch.compile(backend=my_backend) def cus_func(a): s = torch.cuda.Stream() with torch.cuda.stream(s): torch.add(a, 1) a = torch.ones(2, 2).cuda() cus_func(a) ``` In torch2.9, FX graph will be: ``` graph(): %l_a_ : torch.Tensor [num_users=1] = placeholder[target=L_a_] %stream : [num_users=1] = call_function[target=torch.cuda.streams.Stream](args = (), kwargs = {}) %current_stream : [num_users=1] = call_function[target=torch.cuda.current_stream](args = (None,), kwargs = {}) %set_stream : [num_users=0] = call_function[target=torch.cuda.set_stream](args = (%stream,), kwargs = {}) %add : [num_users=0] = call_function[target=torch.add](args = (%l_a_, 1), kwargs = {}) %set_stream_1 : [num_users=0] = call_function[target=torch.cuda.set_stream](args = (%current_stream,), kwargs = {}) return () ``` **Impact:** My custom torch.compilebackend relies on these set_stream nodes in the FX graph to perform stream-related operations before AOTAutograd. The removal of these nodes breaks my workflow, as the necessary stream configuration is no longer captured in the graph. **Questions:** * What motivated the removal of create_proxyin StreamContextVariable? * Are there alternative approaches to retain the original behavior (e.g., explicit stream management) in newer Dynamo versions? cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo @engineer_10

title: autograd version error for custom context manager with in-place mutations
labels: module: autograd, triaged, oncall: pt2, topic: fuzzer, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug A custom context manager modifies a tensor in-place in `__enter__` and restores it in `__exit__` ```python import torch from torch import nn class SaveRestoreCtx: def __init__(self, tensor): self.tensor = tensor def __enter__(self): self.saved = self.tensor.clone() return self.tensor.add_(1) def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb): self.tensor.copy_(self.saved) class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): with SaveRestoreCtx(x): y = torch.sin(x) return y * x x = torch.randn(4, requires_grad=True) m = module() output1 = m(x.clone()) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x.clone()) # crash print(output1) print(output2) ``` Eager mode runs successfully (no .backward() called, or context manager restores state before gradient computation). Compiled mode crashes with autograd version mismatch. ``` /home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/autograd/graph.py:857: UserWarning: Error detected in SinBackward0. Traceback of forward call that caused the error: File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v4_gpt-5-codex/128059-1.py", line 21, in forward y = torch.sin(x) (Triggered internally title: [CuTeDSL] grouped_mm illegal address from TMA descriptor init/update shape mismatch
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile(torch.nn.functional.grouped_mm, backend="inductor")` with the CuTeDSL backend can fail with illegal address for some grouped GEMM shapes. The issue appears to be in Inductor’s CuTeDSL grouped GEMM template: it passes `initial_c` with the shape of the entire concatenated output across all groups `(sumM, N)` (and similarly for `initial_a`) into `GroupedGemmKernel`, instead of passing a tensor shaped like a **single group output** `(M, N)`. `GroupedGemmKernel` uses `initial_c` when creating the initial TMA store descriptor. If that descriptor is initialized from `(sumM, N)` (for example `sumM=4096`) but runtime updates are per-group `(M, N)` (for example `M=16`), descriptor updates can become invalid for some `N`, leading to `CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_ADDRESS`. Repro: ```python import torch import torch.nn.functional as F from torch._inductor import config as inductor_config patches = { "max_autotune": True, "max_autotune_gemm_backends": "CUTEDSL", "test_configs.autotune_choice_name_regex": "cutedsl", "autotune_fallback_to_aten": False, "cpp_wrapper": False, } G, M, K, N = 256, 16, 3072, 1024 A = torch.randn(G * M, K, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda") * 0.1 B = torch.randn(G, K, N, dtype=torch.bfloat16, device="cuda") * 0.01 offs = torch.arange(1, G + 1, dtype=torch.int32, device="cuda") * M with inductor_config.patch(patches): fn = torch.compile(F.grouped_mm, backend="inductor", dynamic=False) out = fn(A, B, offs=offs) torch.cuda.synchronize() print(out.shape) ``` ### Error logs ``` File "/tmp/torchinductor_root/3d/c3drrspgiy7dtk5cbyksf7er6jbckqtad45go7vj7t66geag6inv.py", line 428, in call cutedsl_fused__grouped_mm_85be668f.run(arg0_1, arg1_1, arg2_1, buf0, stream=stream0) File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_inductor/codegen/cutedsl/cutedsl_kernel.py", line 65, in run return self.kernel_fn(*args, stream=stream, **kwargs) File "/tmp/torchinductor_root/vz/cvzjywbaubyz53rgge6sroywejnx3kj5z6xcduibl42wb7mou66p.py", line 345, in cutedsl_fused__grouped_mm_85be668f_main gemm_executor( File "/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/nvidia_cutlass_dsl/python_packages/cutlass/base_dsl/jit_executor.py", line 799, in run_compiled_program raise DSLCudaRuntimeError(error_code, error_name) cutlass.base_dsl.common.DSLCudaRuntimeError: DSLCudaRuntimeError: CUDA_ERROR_ILLEGAL_ADDRESS (error code: 700) ``` ### Versions - 2.12.0a0+git902c0e0 - GB200 - `nvidia-cutlass-dsl`: 4.4.1 cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: `test_update_user_managed_buffer` is flaky on a unified-memory platform due to memory measurement
labels: module: cuda, module: tests, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: [`AOTInductorTestABICompatibleGpu.test_update_user_managed_buffer`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/42dd401eec004550e0765eeefe568d765aec2a8a/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py#L6544-L6548) uses `torch.cuda.mem_get_info()` to verify that `update_constant_buffer` allocates GPU memory. This is unreliable because the constant blob is allocated via raw `cudaMalloc` (see below), and `cudaMemGetInfo` does not always reflect `cudaMalloc` allocations. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/42dd401eec004550e0765eeefe568d765aec2a8a/torch/csrc/inductor/aoti_runtime/model_base.h#L339-L343 On an NVIDIA IGX Orin, `cudaMemGetInfo` reports zero delta after e.g. 64MB `cudaMalloc`. Possibly, on other unified-memory platforms too. This makes the memory measurement in test `AOTInductorTestABICompatibleGpu.test_update_user_managed_buffer` flaky. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/42dd401eec004550e0765eeefe568d765aec2a8a/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py#L6544-L6548 ## Repro Apply the following patch: ```diff diff --git a/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py b/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py index b07c80f1e41..75d526507c3 100644 --- a/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py +++ b/test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py @@ -6495,7 +6495,9 @@ class AOTInductorTestsTemplate: runner.free_inactive_constant_buffer() - def test_update_user_managed_buffer(self): + @requires_gpu + @parametrize(n, range(20)) + def test_update_user_managed_buffer(self, n): if self.device not in ["cuda", "xpu"]: raise unittest.SkipTest("requires CUDA/XPU") ``` and run: ``` python test/inductor/test_aot_inductor.py -k AOTInductorTestABICompatibleGpu.test_update_user_managed_buffer ``` **Independent script to test `mem_get_info` and `cudaMalloc`**: ```python import torch import ctypes torch.randn(1, device="cuda") torch.cuda.synchronize() torch.cuda.empty_cache() free_before, _ = torch.cuda.mem_get_info("cuda") libcudart = ctypes.CDLL("libcudart.so") ptr = ctypes.c_void_p() libcudart.cudaMalloc(ctypes.byref(ptr), ctypes.c_size_t(64 * 1024 * 1024)) torch.cuda.synchronize() free_after, _ = torch.cuda.mem_get_info("cuda") libcudart.cudaFree(ptr) print(f"cudaMalloc 64MB -> mem_get_info delta: {free_before - free_after} bytes") # Output on IGX Orin: # cudaMalloc 64MB -> mem_get_info delta: 0 bytes ``` ## Fix Compile the test model with `aot_inductor.weight_use_caching_allocator=True` so the blob goes through PyTorch's caching allocator, then measure with `memory_allocated()` which reliably tracks it. I'm working on this, along with verification on various platforms. ## Environment **Relevant output of `collect_env.py`** ``` Python version: 3.12.3 (main, Jan 22 2026, 20:57:42) [GCC 13.3.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-5.15.0-1019-nvidia-tegra-igx-aarch64-with-glibc2.39 Is CUDA available: N/A CUDA runtime version: 12.9.86 CUDA_MODULE_LOADING set to: N/A GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: Orin (nvgpu) Nvidia driver version: 540.4.0 ``` cc @ptrblck @msaroufim @eqy @jerryzh168 @tinglvv @nWEIdia @mruberry @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: cpp_wrapper + CUTLASS backend leads to C++ compile error
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When `cpp_wrapper=True` (or `TORCHINDUCTOR_CPP_WRAPPER=1`) is combined with CUTLASS for GEMM autotune backend that is incompatible with C++ wrapper codegen, the compilation fails with a cryptic C++ error rather than a clear diagnostic. The generated C++ wrapper emits `kernels.<kernel_name>(...)` but the `kernels` object (`AOTInductorModelKernels`) is only declared in AOT mode. This produces `error: 'kernels' was not declared in this scope`. #### Repro ```python import os os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_CPP_WRAPPER"] = "1" os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_CUDAGRAPHS"] = "0" os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_MAX_AUTOTUNE_GEMM_BACKENDS"] = "CUTLASS" import torch import torch._inductor.config as inductor_config inductor_config.cpp_wrapper = True inductor_config.max_autotune_gemm = True inductor_config.max_autotune_gemm_backends = "CUTLASS" inductor_config.cuda.cutlass_epilogue_fusion_enabled = True device = torch.device("cuda") dtype = torch.bfloat16 seqlen, hidden = 512, 4096 x = torch.randn((1, seqlen, hidden), device=device, dtype=dtype) w_rms = torch.randn((hidden,), device=device, dtype=dtype) w = torch.randn((hidden, hidden), device=device, dtype=dtype) class M(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x, w_rms, w): xf = x.to(torch.float32) rstd = torch.rsqrt(xf.pow(2).mean(dim=-1, keepdim=True) + 1e-5) y = (xf * rstd) * w_rms y = y.to(torch.bfloat16) y2 = y.view(seqlen, hidden) wt = w.permute(1, 0) return y2 @ wt m = M().to(device=device) cm = torch.compile(m, backend="inductor", dynamic=False) with torch.no_grad(): cm(x, w_rms, w) ``` [torch_trace.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25648933/torch_trace.log) ### Error logs ``` torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: CppCompileError: C++ compile error ... error: 'kernels' was not declared in this scope 141 | kernels.cutlass_fused__to_copy_add_mean_mm_mul_permute_pow_rsqrt_view_986383f9(...); | ^~~~~~~ ``` ### Expected behavior Clearer error message or, turning off CUTLASS when cpp wrapper is enabled, similar to cuda graphs are disabled -- https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/45dfce33fa9f475cc715e528f64a45f6ed0cb150/torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py#L2169-L2185 ### Versions - PyTorch: 2.12.0a0+git8a65c3e - CUTLASS: The repo's submodule - Tested on H100 and GB200 cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: `torch.vmap(torch.compile(jvp_fn, fullgraph=True))` fails with `Unsupported functorch tracing attempt`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When attempting to vectorize a compiled function that uses `torch.func.jvp` via `torch.vmap`, PyTorch Dynamo fails with an `Unsupported functorch tracing attempt` error. The error message suggests: - Calling `torch.func.vmap(compiled_fn)` from eager mode is not supported. - Or `torch.func.vmap(fn)` requires the function to be inlined by dynamo. ### Ablation - **Mode**: - `fullgraph=True`: Fails with `torch._dynamo.exc.Unsupported: Unsupported functorch tracing attempt`. - `fullgraph=False`: Script completes but with significant graph breaks, effectively falling back to eager mode for the transformed portions. - **Goal**: Enable `torch.vmap(torch.compile(jvp_fn, fullgraph=True))` to work without falling back to eager mode. ### Minimal Reproducer ```python import torch from torch import nn from torch.func import functional_call, jvp, vmap # Simple linear model model = nn.Linear(5, 1) params = dict(model.named_parameters()) param_numels = tuple(p.numel() for p in params.values()) param_named_shapes = tuple((m, p.shape) for m, p in params.items()) x = torch.randn(4, 5) def model_fn(p): return functional_call(model, p, x) def compute_jvp(tangent): v_split = tangent.split(param_numels) v_dict = { k: m.reshape(shape) for m, (k, shape) in zip(v_split, param_named_shapes) } _, out_jvp = jvp(model_fn, (params,), (v_dict,)) return out_jvp n_params = sum(param_numels) tangents = torch.randn(4, n_params) # 1. Eager vmap works correctly res_eager = vmap(compute_jvp)(tangents) print("Eager Success, shape:", res_eager.shape) # 2. vmap on compiled function fails compiled_jvp = torch.compile(compute_jvp, fullgraph=True) try: res_compiled = vmap(compiled_jvp)(tangents) print("Compiled Success, shape:", res_compiled.shape) except Exception as e: print("\\n--- FAILS HERE ---") print(e) ``` ### Error logs ```text Traceback (most recent call last): File "<frozen runpy>", line 198, in _run_module_as_main File "<frozen runpy>", line 88, in _run_code File "/Users/tristancinquin/Documents/PhD/lm-laplace/src/lm_laplace/laplace_approx/test_jvp_vmap.py", line 33, in <module> res_crash = vmap(torch.compile(compute_jvp, fullgraph=True))(tangents) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/tristancinquin/anaconda3/envs/lm-laplace-py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_functorch/apis.py", line 208, in wrapped return vmap_impl( ^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/tristancinquin/anaconda3/envs/lm-laplace-py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_functorch/vmap.py", line 282, in vmap_impl return _flat_vmap( ^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/tristancinquin/anaconda3/envs/lm-laplace-py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_functorch/vmap.py", line 432, in _flat_vmap batched_outputs = func(*batched_inputs, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/tristancinquin/anaconda3/envs/lm-laplace-py312/lib/python3.12/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 832, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, title: a.new_tensor([num]) should not introduce a graph break
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: dynamo, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug For this simple case, f2 has a break while f1 doesn't: ```python import torch def f1(a): num = a.nonzero().squeeze(-1).numel() return torch.tensor([num]) # no break def f2(a): num = a.nonzero().squeeze(-1).numel() return a.new_tensor([num]) # break a = torch.tensor([1, 0]) torch.compile(f1, fullgraph=True)(a) torch.compile(f2, fullgraph=True)(a) ``` ### Error logs ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "test.py", line 13, in <module> torch.compile(f2, fullgraph=True)(a) File "pytorch/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 1030, in compile_wrapper raise e.with_traceback(None) from e.__cause__ # User compiler error torch._dynamo.exc.UserError: Could not extract specialized integer from data-dependent expression u0 (unhinted: u0). (Size-like symbols: u0) Caused by: return a.new_tensor([num]) # break # test.py:9 in f2 (_dynamo/utils.py:3906 in run_node) For more information, run with TORCH_LOGS="dynamic" For extended logs when we create symbols, also add TORCHDYNAMO_EXTENDED_DEBUG_CREATE_SYMBOL="u0" If you suspect the guard was triggered from C++, add TORCHDYNAMO_EXTENDED_DEBUG_CPP=1 For more debugging help, see https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HSuTTVvYH1pTew89Rtpeu84Ht3nQEFTYhAX3Ypa_xJs/edit?usp=sharing User Stack (most recent call last): (snipped, see stack below for prefix) File "test.py", line 9, in f2 return a.new_tensor([num]) # break For C++ stack trace, run with TORCHDYNAMO_EXTENDED_DEBUG_CPP=1 For more information about this error, see: https://pytorch.org/docs/main/generated/exportdb/index.html#constrain-as-size-example from user code: File "test.py", line 9, in f2 return a.new_tensor([num]) # break ``` [versions.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25620731/versions.log) ### Versions To my understanding, if `torch.tensor([num])` could be compiled without break, `a.new_tensor([num])` should also pass? cc @chauhang @penguinwu @engineer_17 @engineer_15_gh @aditvenk @laithsakka @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: amp + flex_attention_backward results in NoValidChoicesError
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: flex attention
body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile` of `flex_attention_backward` may crash inside `torch.autocast('cuda', torch.bfloat16)` context: ``` torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError ``` The forward pass succeeds. More specifically, this behavior depends on the `BLOCK_SIZE` and query/key dimensions `C` used. The reproducer below loops over different settings. Note that I need to set tile sizes in `FlexKernelOptions` to be divisible by `BLOCK_SIZE`. Queries/keys are autocasted to `float32` while values are `bfloat16`. ```python import torch from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import flex_attention, create_block_mask, FlexKernelOptions amp_enabled = True # amp_enabled = False amp_dtype = torch.bfloat16 device = 'cuda' torch.set_default_device(device) B,H,N = 1,8,234 def mask_mod(b, h, q, kv): return q > kv for BLOCK_SIZE in (32, 64, 128): block_mask = torch.compile(create_block_mask)( mask_mod, B=None, H=None, Q_LEN=N, KV_LEN=N, device=device, BLOCK_SIZE=BLOCK_SIZE ) # Set tile sizes to BLOCK_SIZE to prevent # torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: LoweringException: ValueError: # Q and KV block size must be divisible by BLOCK_M and BLOCK_N. kernel_options: FlexKernelOptions = { 'BLOCK_M': BLOCK_SIZE, 'BLOCK_M1': BLOCK_SIZE, 'BLOCK_M2': BLOCK_SIZE, 'BLOCK_N': BLOCK_SIZE, 'BLOCK_N1': BLOCK_SIZE, 'BLOCK_N2': BLOCK_SIZE, } for C in (16, 32, 64, 128): x = torch.randn(B,H,N,C, requires_grad=True, dtype=torch.float32) lin = torch.nn.Linear(C, 3*C) # for triggering autocast to bfloat16 torch._dynamo.reset() # @torch.compile(mode="max-autotune-no-cudagraphs") @torch.compile def forward(x, block_mask): with torch.autocast(device, dtype=amp_dtype, enabled=amp_enabled): # amp + linear -> q,k,v in bfloat16 x = lin(x) q,k,v = torch.chunk(x, chunks=3, dim=-1) # amp + exp -> q,k in float32, v remains in bfloat16 q = torch.exp(q) k = torch.exp(k) # Explicit casting back to bfloat16 doesn't change anything # q = q.to(dtype=torch.bfloat16) # k = k.to(dtype=torch.bfloat16) # The forward pass always succeeds out = flex_attention(q, k, v, block_mask=block_mask, kernel_options=kernel_options) loss = out.mean() return loss loss = forward(x, block_mask) # Depending on the shapes (BLOCK_SIZE and C), the backward pass results in an error: # LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward try: loss.backward() print(f'BLOCK_SIZE={BLOCK_SIZE:3d}, C={C:3d}: success') except Exception as exc: err = str(exc)[:38] + ' [...] ' + str(exc)[248:279] print(f'BLOCK_SIZE={BLOCK_SIZE:3d}, C={C:3d}: ' + err) print() ``` Here is the output, showing which settings succeed/fail: ``` BLOCK_SIZE= 32, C= 16: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 32, C= 32: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 32, C= 64: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 32, C=128: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 64, C= 16: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 64, C= 32: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError [...] target: flex_attention_backward BLOCK_SIZE= 64, C= 64: success BLOCK_SIZE= 64, C=128: success BLOCK_SIZE=128, C= 16: success BLOCK_SIZE=128, C= 32: success BLOCK_SIZE=128, C= 64: success BLOCK_SIZE=128, C=128: success ``` And the full error message for one of the settings that fail: ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File ".../reproducer.py", line 79, in loss.backward() ~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^ File ".../torch/_tensor.py", line 622, in backward return handle_torch_function( Tensor.backward, ...<5 lines>... inputs=inputs, ) File ".../torch/overrides.py", line 1742, in handle_torch_function result = mode.__torch_function__(public_api, types, args, kwargs) File ".../torch/utils/_device.py", line 115, in __torch_function__ return func(*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_tensor.py", line 631, in backward torch.autograd.backward( ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^ self, gradient, retain_graph, create_graph, inputs=inputs ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ) ^ File ".../torch/autograd/__init__.py", line 381, in backward _engine_run_backward( ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^ tensors, ^^^^^^^^ ...<5 lines>... accumulate_grad=True, ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ) ^ File ".../torch/autograd/graph.py", line 879, in _engine_run_backward return Variable._execution_engine.run_backward( # Calls into the C++ engine to run the backward pass ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ t_outputs, *args, **kwargs ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ) # Calls into the C++ engine to run the backward pass ^ File ".../torch/autograd/function.py", line 317, in apply return user_fn(self, *args) File ".../torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 2849, in backward return impl_fn() File ".../torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 2835, in impl_fn out = CompiledFunction._backward_impl(ctx, all_args) File ".../torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line ".../torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/schemas.py", line 1396, in __call__ output_code = self.compiler_fn(gm, example_inputs) File ".../torch/_dynamo/backends/common.py", line 83, in _wrapped_bw_compiler disable( ~~~~~~~~ bw_compiler_fn, reason="do not trace backward compiler function" ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~ )(*args, **kwargs), # type: ignore[misc] ~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File ".../torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 1258, in _fn return fn(*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_utils_internal.py", line 96, in wrapper_function return function(*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 2757, in bw_compiler return compile_fx_backward( gm, ...<2 lines>... inner_compile=inner_compile, ) File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 2436, in compile_fx_backward return inner_compile( gm, ...<5 lines>... boxed_forward_device_index=compiler_config_extra.forward_device, ) File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 826, in **kwargs, ^^^^^^^^^ ) ^ File ".../torch/_dynamo/repro/after_aot.py", line 273, in debug_wrapper inner_compiled_fn = compiler_fn(gm, example_inputs) File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 1053, in _compile_fx_inner raise InductorError(e, currentframe()).with_traceback( e.__traceback__ ) from None File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 1037, in _compile_fx_inner mb_compiled_graph = fx_codegen_and_compile( gm, example_inputs, inputs_to_check, **graph_kwargs ) File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 1798, in fx_codegen_and_compile return scheme.codegen_and_compile(gm, example_inputs, inputs_to_check, graph_kwargs) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File ".../torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py", line 1485, in codegen_and_compile graph.run(*example_inputs) ~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 1027, in run return super().run(*args) ~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^^ File ".../torch/fx/interpreter.py", line 200, in run self.env[node] = self.run_node(node) ~~~~~~~~~~~~~^^^^^^ File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 1834, in run_node result = super().run_node(n) File ".../torch/fx/interpreter.py", line 297, target, args, kwargs, stack_trace=stack_trace ).with_traceback(e.__traceback__) from None File ".../torch/_inductor/graph.py", line 1419, in call_function out = lowerings[target](*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_inductor/lowering.py", line 519, in wrapped out = decomp_fn(*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_inductor/kernel/flex/flex_attention.py", line 1015, in flex_attention_backward broadcasted_grad_key = autotune_select_algorithm( "flex_attention_backward", ...<3 lines>... input_gen_fns=input_gen_fns, ) # [Bq, Hkv, seq_len_kv, k_head_dim] File ".../torch/_inductor/select_algorithm.py", line 4833, in autotune_select_algorithm return cache(*args, **kwargs) File ".../torch/_inductor/select_algorithm.py", line 3077, in __call__ raise self.create_no_valid_choices(name, "No choices exist for backend.") torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: LoweringException: NoValidChoicesError: No choices to select. stride=[8, 8, 1])) )), TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='primals_7', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.int32, size=[1, 1, 8, 8], stride=[64, 64, 8, 1])) )), TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='primals_8', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.int32, size=[1, 1, 8], stride=[8, 8, 1])) )), TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='primals_9', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.int32, size=[1, 1, 8, 8], stride=[64, 64, 8, 1])) )), TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='primals_10', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.int32, size=[1, 1, 8], stride=[8, 8, 1])) )), TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='primals_11', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.int32, size=[1, 1, 8, 8], stride=[64, 64, 8, 1])) )), 32, 32, Subgraph(name='mask_graph0', graph_module=GraphModule(), graph=None)) args[10]: 0.25 args[11]: {'BLOCK_M': 32, 'BLOCK_M1': 32, 'BLOCK_M2': 32, 'BLOCK_N': 32, 'BLOCK_N1': 32, 'BLOCK_N2': 32, 'BACKEND': 'AUTO', 'PRESCALE_QK': False, 'ROWS_GUARANTEED_SAFE': False, 'BLOCKS_ARE_CONTIGUOUS': False, 'WRITE_DQ': True, 'OUTPUT_LOGSUMEXP': True, 'OUTPUT_MAX': False} args[12]: () args[13]: () Found from : File ".../reproducer.py", line 73, in forward out = flex_attention(q, k, v, block_mask=block_mask, kernel_options=kernel_options) File ".../torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py", line 1692, in flex_attention out, lse, max_scores = flex_attention_hop( File ".../torch/utils/_device.py", line 115, in __torch_function__ return func(*args, **kwargs) ``` Some observations that might be helpful - Disabling `torch.autocast` works around the issue but let's `flex_attention` run in `float32` precision. - Similarly, one may wrap `flex_attention` into `@torch.amp.custom_fwd(device_type='cuda', cast_inputs=torch.float32)`, such that at least the linear layer runs in `bfloat16`. - Explicitly casting queries/keys to `bfloat16` prior to `flex_attention` does not help. Issues https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/165468 and https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/153042 are related to `flex_attention` and amp but but they don't seem relevant. Another issue suggested to compile with `mode="max-autotune-no-cudagraphs"`, which fixes the error but leads to long compilation times. Is this the recommended fix or are you planning to fix this without requiring autotuning? https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/146260#issuecomment-2643708917
