title: Inductor C++ codegen: undeclared variable in transpose_mxn on ARM/NEON
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ## 🐛 Describe the bug Inductor C++ codegen on ARM/NEON generates code where a variable is used before it is declared, causing a compile error. ## Generated code (condensed) ```cpp // tmp5 used here — NOT YET DECLARED transpose_mxn<double, 3LL, 3LL, false>( in_ptr5 + (x0 + 3LL*x1 + 9LL*tmp5), 3LL, tmp9, 3LL); for (long x0_inner = 0; x0_inner < 3LL; x0_inner++) { auto tmp0 = in_ptr0[0LL]; auto tmp3 = int64_t(tmp0 + 17LL); auto tmp4 = tmp0 < 0; auto tmp5 = tmp4 ? tmp3 : tmp0; // declared HERE, too late } ``` The `transpose_mxn` call is hoisted above the loop that defines `tmp5`. ## Error ``` error: use of undeclared identifier 'tmp5' ``` Compile flags include `-D CPU_CAPABILITY_NEON` (ARM vectorization path). ## Environment - macOS 15.4 / Apple M3 Max (ARM64) - Python 3.12, torch built from source (main, commit `74b00562f5d`) - Default inductor backend

title: `torch.set_default_device` are ignored by `torch.compile`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When `torch.set_default_device("cuda")` is set globally, `torch.tensor(...)` correctly creates tensors on CUDA in eager mode. However, under torch.compile, Dynamo's `FakeTensor` tracing ignores the global default device and creates the tensor on CPU instead, causing a device mismatch error that never occurs in eager mode. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn torch.set_default_device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu") class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): bias = torch.tensor([1.0, -1.0], requires_grad=False) return (x + bias).tanh() m = module() x = torch.randn(2) output1 = m(x) print("eager:", output1) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) print("compiled:", output2) ``` Eager mode prints correct result: ``` eager: tensor([-0.2151, -0.7581], device='cuda:0') ``` torch.compile error log: ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File title: [opaque object] Running the compiled function with opaque object for the second time fails.
labels: triaged, module: custom-operators, tensor subclass, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: pt2-dispatcher, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ```python """ Repro: reference-type opaque object is None inside a custom_op kernel at runtime when loading from cache. Setup: - A user-facing custom_op (custom_op) takes a tensor subclass (ScaledTensor). - Its register_torch_dispatch rule creates a reference-type opaque object (SubclassMeta) mid-trace and passes it to a base custom_op (custom_op_base). - Inductor stores the opaque object in torchbind_constants and references it as _opaque_obj0 in the generated wrapper code. Bug: On the first process run, compilation succeeds and the opaque object is correctly wired into the generated code. On the second process run, with the cached artifact, running the compiled function fails to restore the opaque object -- _opaque_obj0 is None at runtime. To reproduce, run this script twice in separate processes: $ python opaque_obj_bug.py # first run: PASS $ python opaque_obj_bug.py # second run: AssertionError """ import torch from torch._library.opaque_object import OpaqueBase, register_opaque_type class SubclassMeta(OpaqueBase): pass register_opaque_type(SubclassMeta, typ="reference") @torch.library.custom_op("nonerepro_base::custom_op_base", mutates_args=[]) def custom_op_base(data: torch.Tensor, meta: SubclassMeta) -> torch.Tensor: print("custom_op_base", meta) assert meta is not None, "opaque object is None at runtime" return data * 2 @custom_op_base.register_fake def _(data: torch.Tensor, meta: SubclassMeta) -> torch.Tensor: return torch.empty_like(data) custom_op_base.register_autograd( lambda ctx, grad_output: (grad_output * 2, None), setup_context=lambda ctx, inputs, output: None, ) @torch.library.custom_op("nonerepro::custom_op", mutates_args=[]) def custom_op_(x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: return x * 2 @custom_op_.register_fake def _(x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: return torch.empty_like(x) custom_op_.register_autograd( lambda ctx, grad_output: (grad_output * 2,), setup_context=lambda ctx, inputs, output: None, ) class ScaledTensor(torch.Tensor): @staticmethod def __new__(cls, data, scale): return torch.Tensor._make_wrapper_subclass( cls, data.shape, dtype=data.dtype, device=data.device, requires_grad=data.requires_grad, ) def __init__(self, data, scale): self._data = data self._scale = scale def __repr__(self): return f"ScaledTensor(shape={tuple(self.shape)}, scale={self._scale})" def __tensor_flatten__(self): return ["_data"], {"scale": self._scale} @staticmethod def __tensor_unflatten__(inner_tensors, meta, outer_size, outer_stride): return ScaledTensor(inner_tensors["_data"], meta["scale"]) @classmethod def __torch_dispatch__(cls, func, types, args=(), kwargs=None): def unwrap(t): return t._data if isinstance(t, ScaledTensor) else t scale = args[0]._scale out = func( *torch.utils._pytree.tree_map(unwrap, args), **torch.utils._pytree.tree_map(unwrap, kwargs or {}), ) return torch.utils._pytree.tree_map( lambda t: ScaledTensor(t, scale) if isinstance(t, ScaledTensor) else t, out, ) @custom_op_.register_torch_dispatch(ScaledTensor) def _(mode, func, types, args, kwargs): x = args[0] meta = SubclassMeta() out_data = torch.ops.nonerepro_base.custom_op_base(x._data, meta) return ScaledTensor(out_data, x._scale) def fn(x): return torch.ops.nonerepro.custom_op(x) def test_opaque_obj_not_none_on_cache_hit(): x = ScaledTensor(torch.randn(4, device="cuda", requires_grad=True), scale=0.5) compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True) with torch._inductor.utils.fresh_cache(): compiled_fn(x) # Clear in-memory caches torch._dynamo.reset() compiled_fn(x) test_opaque_obj_not_none_on_cache_hit() ``` Output on first run ```python custom_op_base <__main__.SubclassMeta object at 0x7deefcd6fa70> custom_op_base <__main__.SubclassMeta object at 0x7deef6bf23c0> ``` Output on second run ```python custom_op_base <__main__.SubclassMeta object at 0x730d1e831130> custom_op_base None ``` ### Versions main cc @engineer_17 @albanD @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: torch.compile fails with `AttributeError` when namedtuple subclass overrides `__add__`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, topic: fuzzer, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When a namedtuple subclass overrides __add__ to return an instance of itself, `torch.compile` incorrectly degrades the return type to plain tuple? causing AttributeError on named field access. Program works well in eager mode Minimal Reproducible Example: ```python import torch from collections import namedtuple class FancyPair(namedtuple("FancyPair", ["a", "b"])): __slots__ = () def __add__(self, other): return FancyPair(self.a + other.a, self.b + other.b) class module(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x): base = FancyPair(x.sin(), x.cos()) delta = FancyPair(x.relu(), x.tanh()) combined = base + delta return combined.a * 0.5 - combined.b.exp() if __name__ == "__main__": torch.manual_seed(0) x = torch.randn(3, requires_grad=True) m = module() output1 = m(x) print("eager:", output1) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) print("compiled:", output2) ``` Error log: ``` eager: tensor([-1.2950, -2.1027, -0.6235], grad_fn=<SubBackward0>) Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/133762-0-submit.py", line 27, in <module> output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 433, in __call__ return super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 912, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/133762-0-submit.py", line 17, in forward return combined.a * 0.5 - combined.b.exp() AttributeError: 'tuple' object has no attribute 'a' ``` ### Versions ``` PyTorch version: 2.10.0.dev20251021+cu128 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.8 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 22.04.4 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: title: [RFC] Unify decomposition table usage
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: # 🚀 Thread-Safe Custom Decomposition Tables for Inductor cc: @engineer_17 @engineer_14 @voznesenskym @EikanWang @Chillee @thoangtrvn ## Summary This RFC proposes making the `decompositions` kwarg in `compile_fx()` consistently respected throughout the Inductor compilation pipeline. Currently, custom decompositions are accepted but ignored during pattern matching and other compilation phases, forcing backends to use fragile global state patching. ## Motivation PyTorch's Inductor compiler accepts custom decomposition tables via the `decompositions` kwarg: ```python from torch._inductor.compile_fx import compile_fx custom_decomps = { torch.ops.aten.some_op.default: custom_decomposition_fn, # ... more custom decompositions } compiled = compile_fx(gm, example_inputs, decompositions=custom_decomps) ``` However, these custom decompositions are only used during AOT Autograd. Pattern matching and other compilation phases fall back to the global `torch._inductor.decomposition.decompositions` table via `select_decomp_table()`. **Why this matters:** Any out-of-tree hardware backend (like [torch-spyre](https://github.com/torch-spyre/torch-spyre) for IBM's Spyre AI accelerator) likely needs to: - Add hardware-specific decompositions - Remove incompatible decompositions from the default table - Support concurrent compilations (thread safety) **Current workaround:** Monkey-patch the global `decompositions` dict using proxy objects and `contextvars`. This works but is fragile and requires patching multiple import locations. ## Current Behavior - Examples The decomposition kwarg gets lost in the compilation pipeline: 1. ✅ `compile_fx()` receives `decompositions` kwarg ([`compile_fx.py:2664`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py#L2664)) 2. ✅ Passed to `aot_autograd()` ([`compile_fx.py:2813`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py#L2813)) 3. ❌ Not passed to `fw_compiler_base()` → `compile_fx_forward()` 4. ❌ Pattern matching uses global table ([`pattern_matcher.py:2187-2189`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/main/torch/_inductor/pattern_matcher.py#L2187-L2189)) **Example from `pattern_matcher.py`:** ```python def fwd_only( fn: Callable[..., Any], args: Sequence[Any], *, get_decomp_fn: Optional[Callable[..., Any]] = None, # Parameter exists but unused ) -> torch.fx.GraphModule: with enable_python_dispatcher(), preserve_node_meta(): decompositions = ( get_decomp_fn() if get_decomp_fn is not None else select_decomp_table() # Always falls back ) ``` **Other locations using `select_decomp_table()` directly:** - `torch/_inductor/codegen/subgraph.py:323` - `torch/_inductor/kernel/mm.py:234, 271` - `torch/_inductor/pattern_matcher.py:2220` (in `joint_fwd_bwd`) ## Proposed Solutions ### Solution: Thread Decomposition Dict Parameter Thread the `decompositions` dict directly through the compilation pipeline, falling back to `select_decomp_table()` when `None`. For example, in `compile_fx.py` do a simple pass through: **`compile_fx.py`**: Pass decompositions to `fw_compiler_base()`: ```python def fw_compiler_base( gm: GraphModule, example_inputs: Sequence[InputType], is_inference: bool, decompositions: Optional[Dict[Any, Callable]] = None, # Add parameter ) -> OutputCode: return compile_fx_forward( gm, example_inputs, decompositions=decompositions, # Pass through # ... other params ) ``` while in the `pattern_matcher.py`, where the dictionary is actually accessed, one can eventually use the `select_decomp_table()`. For example: ```python def fwd_only( fn: Callable[..., Any], args: Sequence[Any], *, decompositions = None, ) -> torch.fx.GraphModule: decompositions = ( decompositions if decompositions is not None else select_decomp_table() ) # ... rest of function ``` **Benefits:** - Thread-safe: each compilation has its own decomposition table - Backward compatible: all new parameters optional with `None` defaults - Explicit data flow - Minimal changes to existing code ### Alternatives ### Centralize Through `select_decomp_table()` Make all code paths use `select_decomp_table()` consistently, allowing backends to monkey-patch a single function: **Changes needed:** 1. Remove direct imports of `decompositions` dict (e.g., `torch/_inductor/lowering.py:61`) 2. Replace all direct `decompositions` access with `select_decomp_table()` calls 3. Document `select_decomp_table()` as the official extension point **Benefits:** - Single function to patch for custom backends - Less invasive changes - Maintains current architecture **Drawbacks:** - Still relies on global state - Requires monkey-patching for thread safety - Less explicit data flow **Usage example for backends:** ```python from torch._inductor import decomposition import contextvars _custom_decomps = contextvars.ContextVar('custom_decomps', default=None) original_select = decomposition.select_decomp_table def patched_select_decomp_table(): custom = _custom_decomps.get() if custom is not None: return custom return original_select() decomposition.select_decomp_table = patched_select_decomp_table ``` ### Additional context _No response_ cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Silently incorrect results for torch.arange + Inductor
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, pt2: ubn, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Hi! Reproducer below shows that when `torch.compile` is applied to `kek` function it outputs different (and incorrect) results than in eager ``` import torch def kek(x): return torch.arange( 0, 11, device=x.device, dtype=torch.int64 ) * torch.tensor([int(1e9)], dtype=torch.int64, device=x.device) x = torch.zeros(1, device='cuda') print(kek(x)) # output: # tensor([ 0, 1000000000, 2000000000, 3000000000, 4000000000, # 5000000000, 6000000000, 7000000000, 8000000000, 9000000000, # 10000000000], device='cuda:0') print(torch.compile(kek, backend="aot_eager_decomp_partition")(x)) # output: # tensor([ 0, 1000000000, 2000000000, 3000000000, 4000000000, # 5000000000, 6000000000, 7000000000, 8000000000, 9000000000, # 10000000000], device='cuda:0') print(torch.compile(kek, backend="inductor")(x)) # output: # tensor([ 0, 1000000000, 2000000000, -1294967296, -294967296, # 705032704, 1705032704, -1589934592, -589934592, 410065408, # 1410065408], device='cuda:0') ``` Generated Triton code looks like this: ``` import triton import triton.language as tl from torch._inductor.runtime import triton_helpers, triton_heuristics from torch._inductor.runtime.triton_helpers import libdevice, math as tl_math from torch._inductor.runtime.hints import AutotuneHint, ReductionHint, TileHint, DeviceProperties triton_helpers.set_driver_to_gpu() @triton_heuristics.pointwise( size_hints={'x': 16}, filename=__file__, triton_meta={'signature': {'out_ptr0': '*i64', 'xnumel': 'i32', 'XBLOCK': 'constexpr'}, 'device': DeviceProperties(type='cuda', index=0, multi_processor_count=132, cc=90, major=9, regs_per_multiprocessor=65536, max_threads_per_multi_processor=2048, max_threads_per_block=1024, warp_size=32), 'constants': {}, 'native_matmul': False, 'configs': [{(0,): [['tt.divisibility', 16]]}], 'enable_fp_fusion': True}, inductor_meta={'grid_type': 'Grid1D', 'autotune_hints': set(), 'kernel_name': 'triton_poi_fused_arange_lift_fresh_mul_0', 'mutated_arg_names': [], 'optimize_mem': True, 'no_x_dim': False, 'atomic_add_found': False, 'num_load': 0, 'num_store': 1, 'num_reduction': 0, 'backend_hash': '91D1D1166449D9E2EB66EB9770C9E2C3B0FEB787341F27FEB4D3F0338DA50336', 'assert_indirect_indexing': True, 'autotune_local_cache': True, 'autotune_pointwise': True, 'autotune_remote_cache': None, 'force_disable_caches': False, 'dynamic_scale_rblock': True, 'max_autotune': False, 'max_autotune_pointwise': False, 'min_split_scan_rblock': 256, 'spill_threshold': 16, 'store_cubin': False, 'deterministic': False, 'force_filter_reduction_configs': False, 'are_deterministic_algorithms_enabled': False, 'tiling_scores': {'x': 176}}, min_elem_per_thread=0 ) @triton.jit def triton_poi_fused_arange_lift_fresh_mul_0(out_ptr0, xnumel, XBLOCK : tl.constexpr): xnumel = 11 xoffset = tl.program_id(0) * XBLOCK xindex = xoffset + tl.arange(0, XBLOCK)[:] xmask = xindex < xnumel x0 = xindex tmp0 = 1000000000*x0 tl.store(out_ptr0 + (x0), tmp0, xmask) ``` It seems to me that overflow happens because `xindex` and `x0` are both `int32` (despite the fact that I specified `dtype=torch.int64` in `torch.arange`) ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: [torch.compile] Massive numerical discrepancy (~0.05 Max Abs Error) in Conv+BN+SiLU fusion with INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM=1
labels: high priority, module: numerical-stability, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile with the Inductor backend and enabling INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM=1, there is a significant numerical divergence compared to Eager mode. In a standalone reproduction involving the first three blocks of a YOLOv8-like architecture (Conv + BN + SiLU), the Max Absolute Error reaches ~0.05, and the Max Relative Error exceeds 10^5. This suggests an unstable numerical approximation within the fused kernels. Observed Results: Comparing Mode A (Eager Execution) vs Mode B (Compiled with INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM=1): Max Absolute Error: 5.4588e-02 Max Relative Error: 1.0962e+05 Rel L2 Error (Global): 3.8443e-04 Eager output range: [-0.2785, 97.1013] Compiled output range: [-0.2785, 97.0889] Why this matters: While a 0.05 absolute error might seem small in isolation, this discrepancy is roughly 4 orders of magnitude higher than the expected numerical noise for FP32 fusion (typically < 1e-6). In full-scale YOLOv8 models, I have observed this error accumulating across layers, eventually reaching a Max Absolute Error of 0.4 to 0.6, which is large enough to flip classification labels and compromise model reliability. Reproduction Script: This script is truly standalone and does not require any external weights or data files. It manually initializes the weights to ensure intermediate activations reach a magnitude that exposes the numerical instability. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn import os import contextlib SEED = 0 torch.manual_seed(SEED) torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(SEED) @contextlib.contextmanager def set_inductor_env_vars(opt_str): original_env = os.environ.copy() try: s = (opt_str or "0000")[:4].ljust(4, "0") os.environ["INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM"] = "1" if s[0] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION"] = "1" if s[1] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING"] = "1" if s[2] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_DYNAMIC"] = "1" if s[3] == "1" else "0" yield finally: os.environ.clear() os.environ.update(original_env) class Conv(nn.Module): def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size=3, stride=1, padding=1): super().__init__() self.conv = nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size, stride, padding, bias=False) self.bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels, eps=0.001, momentum=0.03) self.act = nn.SiLU() def forward(self, x): return self.act(self.bn(self.conv(x))) class Bottleneck(nn.Module): def __init__(self, c1, c2): super().__init__() self.cv1 = Conv(c1, c2, 3, 1, 1) self.cv2 = Conv(c2, c2, 3, 1, 1) def forward(self, x): return self.cv2(self.cv1(x)) + x class C2f(nn.Module): def __init__(self, c1, c2, n=1, e=0.5): super().__init__() self.c = int(c2 * e) self.cv1 = Conv(c1, self.c, 1, 1, 0) self.cv2 = Conv((n + 1) * self.c, c2, 1, 1, 0) self.m = nn.ModuleList([Bottleneck(self.c, self.c) for _ in range(n)]) def forward(self, x): y = [self.cv1(x)] for module in self.m: y.append(module(y[-1])) return self.cv2(torch.cat(y, 1)) class YOLOv8FirstThreeBlocks(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.block0 = Conv(3, 16, 3, 2, 1) self.block1 = Conv(16, 32, 3, 2, 1) self.block2 = C2f(32, 32, n=1) def forward(self, x): x = self.block0(x) x = self.block1(x) x = self.block2(x) return x def main(): device = torch.device('cuda') torch.manual_seed(SEED) model = YOLOv8FirstThreeBlocks().to(device).eval() for name, param in model.named_parameters(): if 'weight' in name and 'bn' not in name: nn.init.uniform_(param, -0.5, 0.5) elif 'bn' in name and 'weight' in name: nn.init.ones_(param) elif 'bn' in name and 'bias' in name: nn.init.zeros_(param) torch.manual_seed(SEED) x_in = torch.randn(64, 3, 640, 640).to(device) print("\\n[Mode A] Eager Execution...") with torch.no_grad(): out_eager = model(x_in) print("Eager mode completed") print("\\n[Mode B] Compiled Execution (INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM=1)...") with set_inductor_env_vars('1000'): compiled = torch.compile(model) with torch.no_grad(): _ = compiled(x_in) out_compiled = compiled(x_in) print("Compiled mode completed") max_abs_err = torch.abs(out_eager - out_compiled).max().item() rel_l2_err = (torch.norm(out_eager - out_compiled) / (torch.norm(out_eager) + 1e-12)).item() max_rel_err = torch.abs((out_eager - out_compiled) / (torch.abs(out_eager) + 1e-12)).max().item() print("\\n" + "="*100) print("Numerical Difference Analysis (Truly Standalone):") print(f" Max Absolute Error: {max_abs_err:.4e}") print(f" Max Relative Error: {max_rel_err:.4e}") print(f" Rel L2 Error (Global): {rel_l2_err:.4e}") print(f" Eager output range: [{out_eager.min().item():.4f}, {out_eager.max().item():.4f}]") print(f" Compiled output range: [{out_compiled.min().item():.4f}, {out_compiled.max().item():.4f}]") print("="*100) if __name__ == "__main__": main() ``` ``` [Mode A] Eager Execution... Eager mode completed [Mode B] Compiled Execution (INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM=1)... Compiled mode completed ==================================================================================================== Numerical Difference Analysis (Truly Standalone): Max Absolute Error: 5.4588e-02 Max Relative Error: 1.0962e+05 Rel L2 Error (Global): 3.8443e-04 Eager output range: [-0.2785, 97.1013] Compiled output range: [-0.2785, 97.0889] ==================================================================================================== ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: title: [RFC] torch.switch
labels: feature, triaged, module: cuda graphs, topic: new features, oncall: pt2, module: higher order operators, module: pt2-dispatcher, bot-triaged
body:
### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch ### The feature PyTorch recently gained support for [CUDA Graph compatible `torch.cond`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168912). This feature request is a generalization of `torch.cond` to support N-way branching during stream capture. Just like `torch.cond`, `torch.switch` maps nicely to [switch type CUDA graph conditional nodes](https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/dynamic-control-flow-in-cuda-graphs-with-conditional-nodes/#conditional_switch_nodes). The API would be identical to [jax.lax.switch](https://docs.jax.dev/en/latest/_autosummary/jax.lax.switch.html). ### Motivation [PyTorch docs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/torch.compiler_cudagraph_trees.html#cudagraph-trees-integration) demonstrate how a graph tree is constructed in the presence of a conditional node. For a function containing 4 subgraphs that selects between graph 2 and 3 based on a condition, we would instantiate a graph tree containing two copies of graph 4. Graph 4 needs to be recorded twice because depending on which path was taken, the input memory addresses can change. ```mermaid flowchart LR; code["
def func(x):
 y = graph_1(x)
 if pred(y):
 z = graph_2(y)
 else:
 z = graph_3(y)
 return graph_4(y)
"]; subgraph L["Python Control Flow"] 1 --> 2; 1 --> 3; 2 --> 4; 3 --> 4; end subgraph R["CUDA Graph Tree"] _1[1]; _2[2]; _3[3]; _4_1[4.1]; _4_2[4.2]; _1 --> _2 --> _4_1; _1 --> _3 --> _4_2; end code ~~~ L ~~~ R; ``` If we were to have control flow with N-way branching, the downstream graph will be instantiated N times. Adding more control flow in subgraphs will increase the branching multiplicatively. Fortunately, just like `torch.cond` can avoid this by using CUDA Graph conditional nodes, switch can do the same in CUDA >= 12.8. ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context This has previously been requested here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/62530 cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang @ydwu4 @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: torch.compile crashes with `InternalTorchDynamoError (RecursionError)`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, dynamo-variable-tracker, dynamo-torch-function
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug torch.compile crashes with InternalTorchDynamoError (RecursionError). I have minimized the program and it seems to do with overriding `__torch_function__` of Tensor subclass. Eager execution works correctly, but compiled execution fails internally instead of producing the same result or graph-breaking gracefully. Minimal reproducer: ```python import torch class QuirkyTensor(torch.Tensor): @classmethod def __torch_function__(cls, func, types, args=(), kwargs=None): args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] return func(*args) def fn(x): return torch.sin(x) x = torch.randn(4).as_subclass(QuirkyTensor) output1 = fn(x) print("eager:", output1) compiled = torch.compile(fn) output2 = compiled(x) print("compiled:", output2) ``` ### Error logs Full error log is too long, i have minimized it: ``` ...... torch._dynamo.exc.InternalTorchDynamoError: RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object from user code: File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 11, in fn return torch.sin(x) File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in __torch_function__ args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in <listcomp> args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in __torch_function__ args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in <listcomp> args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in __torch_function__ args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in <listcomp> args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/150265-0-submit.py", line 7, in __torch_function__ args = [a.as_subclass(torch.Tensor) for a in args] (repeated) ``` ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: [Inductor] Incorrect results in `index_put_` when `randperm` views are not injective
labels: high priority, triaged, module: advanced indexing, module: correctness (silent), oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ```python import torch from torch.nn import Module class Model(Module): def forward(self, x): p = torch.randperm(5) # Create a non-injective (overlapping) view of randperm idx = torch.as_strided(p, (5,), (0,)) y = x[idx] + 1.0 x.index_put_((idx,), y, accumulate=False) return x model = Model().eval() compiled_model = torch.compile(model, fullgraph=True) torch.manual_seed(42) input_uncompiled = torch.zeros(5) output_uncompiled = model(input_uncompiled) torch.manual_seed(42) input_compiled = torch.zeros(5) output_compiled = compiled_model(input_compiled) print("Uncompiled model output:\\n", output_uncompiled) print("Compiled model output:\\n", output_compiled) torch.testing.assert_close(output_uncompiled, output_compiled, equal_nan=True) ``` Output: ``` Uncompiled model output: tensor([0., 0., 1., 0., 0.]) Compiled model output: tensor([0., 0., 2., 0., 0.]) AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 1 / 5 (20.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 1.0 at index (2,) (up to 1e-05 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 0.5 at index (2,) (up to 1.3e-06 allowed) ``` Root cause: The compiler incorrectly assumes any view of `randperm` is injective and skips the `.realize()` read-after-write safeguard. ### Versions [pip3] numpy==2.4.2 [pip3] optree==0.19.0 [pip3] torch==2.12.0a0+git93b243e [conda] numpy 2.4.2 py311h4bb6f22_0 [conda] numpy-base 2.4.2 py311h23175f9_0 [conda] optree 0.19.0 pypi_0 pypi [conda] torch 2.12.0a0+git93b243e pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [Numerical Consistency] Significant drift between Eager and torch.compile triggered by INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING=1 on Conv2d(1x1)
labels: module: numerical-stability, module: convolution, triaged, module: correctness (silent), oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I observed a significant numerical discrepancy ($> 10^{-3}$ Max Absolute Error) between PyTorch Eager mode and torch.compile when testing a standard 1x1 Convolution. The issue is specifically triggered or amplified when the optimization flag INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING=1 is enabled. Why this matters: This level of error ($10^{-3}$) is unacceptable for a single layer in a deep network. In architectures like MobileNet or ResNet, such numerical drift in early layers can accumulate and propagate through dozens of subsequent layers, eventually leading to inconsistent Top-1 classification results or training divergence. The promise of torch.compile is bit-wise or near bit-wise consistency, which is broken here. ```python import torch, os import torch.nn as nn torch.manual_seed(0) layer = nn.Conv2d(32, 64, 1, bias=False).eval().cuda() x = torch.randn(64, 32, 112, 112).cuda() print(f"Target Op: {layer}") print(f"Input - NaN: {torch.isnan(x).any()}, Inf: {torch.isinf(x).any()}") print(f"Input - Min: {x.min():.4f}, Max: {x.max():.4f}, Mean: {x.mean():.4f}") def get_out(opt): mode = "cuda float32 eager" if opt == "0000" else "cuda float compile" print(f"\\nRunning Mode: {mode}") for i, key in enumerate(["EPILOGUE_FUSION", "FALLBACK_RANDOM", "SHAPE_PADDING", "DYNAMIC"]): os.environ[f"INDUCTOR_{key}"] = opt[i] with torch.no_grad(): if opt == "0000": return layer(x) return torch.compile(layer)(x) out_eager = get_out("0000") out_comp = get_out("0010") diff = (out_eager.detach().cpu().float() - out_comp.detach().cpu().float()).abs() rel_l2 = (torch.norm(diff) / torch.norm(out_eager.detach().cpu().float()).clamp(min=1e-8)).item() print(f"\\nMax Abs Error: {diff.max().item():.6e}") print(f"Rel L2 Error: {rel_l2:.6e}") print(f"Final Status: {'FAILED (Exceeds threshold)' if rel_l2 > 1e-4 else 'PASSED'}") ``` ``` Target Op: Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=(1, 1), stride=(1, 1), bias=False) Input - NaN: False, Inf: False Input - Min: -5.2046, Max: 5.2977, Mean: -0.0002 Running Mode: cuda float32 eager Running Mode: cuda float compile Max Abs Error: 1.208246e-03 Rel L2 Error: 3.003954e-04 Final Status: FAILED (Exceeds threshold) ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.6 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: Could not collect Libc version: glibc-2.39 Python version: 3.10.19 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Jan 26 2026, 23:45:08) [GCC 14.3.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-6.8.0-90-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.39 Is CUDA available: True CUDA runtime version: 12.6.20 CUDA_MODULE_LOADING set to: GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU 1: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Nvidia driver version: 560.35.03 cuDNN version: Could not collect Is XPU available: False HIP runtime version: N/A MIOpen runtime version: N/A Is XNNPACK available: True Caching allocator config: N/A CPU: Architecture: x86_64 CPU op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit Address sizes: 40 bits physical, 48 bits virtual Byte Order: Little Endian CPU(s): 48 On-line CPU(s) list: 0-47 Vendor ID: GenuineIntel Model name: QEMU Virtual CPU version 2.5+ CPU family: 15 Model: 107 Thread(s) per core: 1 Core(s) per socket: 48 Socket(s): 1 Stepping: 1 BogoMIPS: 4190.15 Flags: fpu de pse tsc msr pae mce cx8 apic sep mtrr pge mca cmov pat pse36 clflush mmx fxsr sse sse2 ht syscall nx lm constant_tsc nopl xtopology cpuid tsc_known_freq pni ssse3 fma cx16 sse4_1 sse4_2 x2apic movbe popcnt aes xsave avx f16c hypervisor lahf_lm abm cpuid_fault pti bmi1 avx2 bmi2 avx512f avx512dq avx512cd avx512bw avx512vl Hypervisor vendor: KVM Virtualization type: full L1d cache: 1.5 MiB (48 instances) L1i cache: 1.5 MiB (48 instances) L2 cache: 192 MiB (48 instances) L3 cache: 16 MiB (1 instance) NUMA node(s): 1 NUMA node0 CPU(s): 0-47 Vulnerability Gather data sampling: Not affected title: torch.compile crashes with `NotImplementedError` when passing user-defined class to torch op inside try/except
labels: triaged, module: fx, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, topic: fuzzer, module: compile ux, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When a user-defined class (e.g., `class MyClass: pass`) is passed as an argument to a PyTorch operator (like `new_full`, which expects a shape), TorchDynamo crashes with an `InternalTorchDynamoError` during graph construction. In eager mode, this operation correctly raises a `TypeError`, which can be caught and handled by a `try/except` block. Interestingly, passing a built-in type like `int` (which is also invalid for `new_full`) works correctly ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn class MyClass: pass class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): try: y = x.new_full(MyClass, 3.14) # y = x.new_full(int, 3.14) # not crash, TyprError captured except TypeError: print("TyprError captured") y = x + 1.0 return y m = module() x = torch.ones(4) output = torch.compile(m)(x) # crashes ``` Error log: ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/129651-1-submit.py", line 18, in <module> output = torch.compile(m)(x) # crashes File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 433, in __call__ return **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File title: `torch.compile` crashes with "Guard failed on same frame" when user audit hook monitors `exec`
labels: module: crash, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, topic: fuzzer, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When a user registers a `sys.addaudithook` that modifies global state (e.g., increments a counter) upon receiving an `exec` event, `torch.compile` crashes with an `AssertionError: Guard failed on the same frame it was created` ```python import torch import sys audit_state = {"count": 0} def audit_hook(event, args): if event == "exec": audit_state["count"] += 1 sys.addaudithook(audit_hook) class module(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x): exec("pass") if audit_state["count"] % 2 == 0: return x + 1 return x + 2 m = module() x = torch.ones(3) print("eager:", m(x)) print("compiled:", torch.compile(m)(x)) ``` Error log: ``` eager: tensor([3., 3., 3.]) Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/133185-0-submit.py", line 25, in <module> output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 433, in __call__ return super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 912, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/133185-0-submit.py", line 14, in forward exec("pass") # triggers Python auditing hook implicitly File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 2034, in __call__ result = self._torchdynamo_orig_backend( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1784, in __call__ result = self._inner_convert( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 701, in __call__ result = _compile( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1591, in _compile guarded_code, tracer_output = compile_inner(code, one_graph, hooks) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_utils_internal.py", line 98, in wrapper_function return function(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1273, in compile_inner return _compile_inner(code, one_graph, hooks) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1407, in _compile_inner check_fn = dynamo_output.build_guards( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 873, in build_guards return CheckFunctionManager( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/guards.py", line 3617, in __init__ raise AssertionError( AssertionError: Guard failed on the same frame it was created. This is a bug - please create an issue.Guard fail reason: 1/0: G['audit_state']['count'] == 3902 # if audit_state["count"] % 2 == 0: # torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/133185-0-submit.py:15 in torch_dynamo_resume_in_forward_at_14 ``` ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: Numerical discrepancy in nn.Linear between eager and compile modes
labels: module: numerical-stability, oncall: pt2, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile() on nn.Linear layer, the output differs from eager mode even with identical device (cuda) and dtype (float32). The relative L2 error is 1.7180e-04, which exceeds the 1e-4 threshold. Key observations: Same configuration: Both modes use cuda device and float32 dtype, only the optimization mode differs (eager vs compile) All compile modes produce same error: All optimization modes (0001, 0010, 0100, 1000) produce identical relative L2 error of 1.7180e-04, suggesting this is a general torch.compile() issue rather than a specific optimization option problem Data validation: All inputs and outputs are valid (no NaN/Inf), with normal value ranges Error magnitude: Relative L2 error of 1.7180e-04 exceeds the 1e-4 threshold Impact: This numerical discrepancy may accumulate in deep models, potentially affecting final predictions Comparison results: Device switch (cpu vs cuda): OK (1.1980e-07 < 1e-4) - acceptable device difference Dtype switch (float16 vs float32): Exceeds threshold (3.3306e-04) - expected due to precision loss All compile modes (0001, 0010, 0100, 1000): All exceed threshold (1.7180e-04) - this is the main issue Expected behavior: Eager and compile modes should produce identical (or within acceptable tolerance < 1e-4) numerical results when device and dtype are identical. Actual behavior: Any compile mode produces a relative L2 error of 1.7180e-04 compared to eager mode, indicating a numerical inconsistency in the compilation process that should not occur. Reproduction: The issue can be reproduced using the provided self-contained code with fixed random seed (42), ensuring reproducibility across different environments. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn import os import contextlib torch.manual_seed(42) torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42) @contextlib.contextmanager def set_inductor_env_vars(env_str: str): original_env = {} env_keys = ["INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM", "INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION", "INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING", "INDUCTOR_DYNAMIC"] for key in env_keys: original_env[key] = os.environ.get(key) try: s = (env_str or "0000")[:4].ljust(4, "0") os.environ["INDUCTOR_FALLBACK_RANDOM"] = "1" if s[0] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION"] = "1" if s[1] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING"] = "1" if s[2] == "1" else "0" os.environ["INDUCTOR_DYNAMIC"] = "1" if s[3] == "1" else "0" yield finally: for key, value in original_env.items(): if value is None: os.environ.pop(key, None) else: os.environ[key] = value def compare(t_a, t_b): a, b = t_a.detach().cpu().float(), t_b.detach().cpu().float() max_abs = torch.abs(a - b).max().item() l2_a, l2_b = torch.norm(a), torch.norm(b) rel_l2 = (torch.norm(a - b) / torch.maximum(l2_a, l2_b).clamp(min=1e-8)).item() return max_abs, rel_l2 def run_mode(dense_layer, x_in, dev, dtype, opt): dense = dense_layer.to(dev).to(dtype).eval() if opt != "0000": with set_inductor_env_vars(opt): dense = torch.compile(dense) with torch.no_grad(): return dense(x_in.to(dev).to(dtype)) def run(): x_in = torch.randn(64, 50) dense_layer = nn.Linear(50, 1) print("Input: shape={}".format(x_in.shape)) print("Input - min: {:.6f}, max: {:.6f}, mean: {:.6f}".format(x_in.min().item(), x_in.max().item(), x_in.mean().item())) print("Input - has NaN: {}, has Inf: {}".format(torch.isnan(x_in).any().item(), torch.isinf(x_in).any().item())) print("=" * 60) modes = [ ("Mode A (cuda/f32/0000)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0000'), ("Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000)", 'cpu', torch.float32, '0000'), ("Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000)", 'cuda', torch.float16, '0000'), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0001)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0001'), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0010'), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0100)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0100'), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/1000)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '1000') ] outputs = {} for label, dev, dtype, opt in modes: print(f"\\n>>> Running {label} ...") outputs[label] = run_mode(dense_layer, x_in, dev, dtype, opt) has_nan = torch.isnan(outputs[label]).any().item() has_inf = torch.isinf(outputs[label]).any().item() print(f" ✓ {label} executed successfully") print(f" Output - min: {outputs[label].min().item():.6f}, max: {outputs[label].max().item():.6f}, mean: {outputs[label].mean().item():.6f}") print(f" Output - has NaN: {has_nan}, has Inf: {has_inf}") ma_name = modes[0][0] for switch_idx in range(1, len(modes)): mb_name = modes[switch_idx][0] max_d, max_rd = compare(outputs[ma_name], outputs[mb_name]) exceeds = "Yes" if max_rd > 1e-4 else "No" status = "DIFF > 1e-4" if max_rd > 1e-4 else "OK" print("\\n" + "="*120) print(f"Comparison: {ma_name} vs {mb_name}") print("="*120) print(f"{'Layer Name':<40} | {'Max Abs Error':<15} | {'Rel L2 Error':<15} | {'> 1e-4':<8} | {'Status'}") print("-" * 120) print(f"{'dense':<40} | {max_d:.4e} | {max_rd:.4e} | {exceeds:<8} | {status}") print("="*120) if __name__ == "__main__": run() ``` ``` Input: shape=torch.Size([64, 50]) Input - min: -3.832532, max: 3.445625, mean: 0.004185 Input - has NaN: False, has Inf: False ============================================================ >>> Running Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) ... ✓ Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559245, max: 1.641414, mean: 0.001701 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) ... ✓ Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559245, max: 1.641414, mean: 0.001701 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) ... ✓ Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559570, max: 1.641602, mean: 0.001665 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0001) ... ✓ Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0001) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559244, max: 1.641361, mean: 0.001691 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) ... ✓ Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559244, max: 1.641361, mean: 0.001691 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0100) ... ✓ Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0100) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559244, max: 1.641361, mean: 0.001691 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/1000) ... ✓ Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/1000) executed successfully Output - min: -1.559244, max: 1.641361, mean: 0.001691 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 2.3842e-07 | 1.1980e-07 | No | OK ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 6.7222e-04 | 3.3306e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0001) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 3.3081e-04 | 1.7180e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 3.3081e-04 | 1.7180e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0100) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 3.3081e-04 | 1.7180e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/1000) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ dense | 3.3081e-04 | 1.7180e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.6 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: Could not title: torch.compile yields incorrect results when iterating over a list
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When iterating over a list while simultaneously appending to it inside the loop, Python's standard iterator dynamically reflects these changes (i.e., it visits the newly appended elements). However, `torch.compile` ignores the elements appended during iteration and leads to silent correctness errors. The output diverges from eager execution. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): vals = [x.sum()] def grow_and_read(): for item in vals: if len(vals) < 3: vals.append(item + 1) yield item seq = list(grow_and_read()) return torch.stack(seq).sum() m = module() x = torch.ones(1) output1 = m(x) print("eager:", output1) # tensor(6.) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) print("compiled:", output2) # tensor(1.) ``` ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: RFC: `torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic_dict` — skip key-set guards on dict mutations
labels: feature, triaged, function request, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile-time, module: compile ux, bot-triaged
body: ## The problem Dynamo installs a `DICT_KEYS_MATCH` guard on almost every dict operation it traces, including `__setitem__`. That guard records the exact set of keys at trace time and invalidates the compiled frame whenever the set changes. This is fine for dicts that have a fixed schema, but it makes life miserable for any code that *adds* a key to an input dict inside a compiled region: ```python @torch.compile def step(state): state["loss"] = loss_fn(state["obs"]) return state ``` First call: `state` has `{"obs"}`. Dynamo traces, records `DICT_KEYS_MATCH({"obs"})`, replays the `__setitem__` as a side-effect. Now `state` has `{"obs", "loss"}`. Second call: the guard checks the key set against `{"obs"}` — fails — Dynamo retraces. This time the guard records `{"obs", "loss"}`. From the third call onward it stabilises, but you've paid for an unnecessary recompilation that can easily snowball with more keys or nested structures. The concrete case that brought this up is tensordict, where `TensorDict` wraps a plain `dict[str, Tensor]`. Any `__setitem__` that introduces a new key triggers the problem above. The current workaround in tensordict (pytorch/tensordict#1605, already merged) is to wrap the mutation in `@torch.compiler.disable`, which eliminates the recompilation but introduces a graph break at every `__setitem__`. That kills operator fusion across the mutation boundary and makes `fullgraph=True` impossible for any code path that writes to the dict. The same pattern shows up anywhere a dict is used as an open-ended container for tensors — parameter stores, batched output dicts, metric accumulators, named-tensor wrappers, etc. ## Why `__setitem__` doesn't need the full key-set guard Looking at what `ConstDictVariable.__setitem__` actually does: ```python elif name == "__setitem__" and self.is_mutable(): self.install_dict_keys_match_guard() # ← over-guarding tx.output.side_effects.mutation(self) self.items[Hashable(args[0])] = args[1] ``` The mutation is replayed at runtime via `dict.update({key: value})`. That call works correctly regardless of what other keys exist in the dict — it either adds or overwrites the target key. My understanding is that the guard isn't protecting the mutation; it's protecting other operations (like iteration) that happen to share the same `install_dict_keys_match_guard` call path. For `__getitem__`, Dynamo already skips the key-set guard: ```python elif name == "__getitem__": # Key guarding - Nothing to do. LazyVT for value will take care. return self.getitem_const_raise_exception_if_absent(tx, args[0]) ``` Each value accessed via `__getitem__` gets its own source (`DictGetItemSource`) and its own value-level guard (`TENSOR_MATCH`, etc.). If the key disappears at runtime, the guard on the value fails and Dynamo recompiles — no silent wrong answers. So the asymmetry today is: `__getitem__` is guarded per-key (cheap, correct), but `__setitem__` pulls in the entire key-set guard (expensive, unnecessary). ## Proposed API I opened a PR with a prototype of these changes [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/175624). ```python d = {"obs": obs_tensor} torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic_dict(d) ``` Analogous to `mark_dynamic` for tensor shapes. It tells Dynamo "this dict's key set may change between calls; don't guard the whole set on every mutation." ### What it does Internally, the marked dict gets tracked as a `DynamicKeysConstDictVariable` (subclass of `ConstDictVariable`) where `install_dict_keys_match_guard` is a no-op. The effect: | Operation | Guard installed | Recompiles on key-set change? | |-----------|----------------|-------------------------------| | `d[key]` (getitem) | Per-value (via source) | Only if that specific value changes | | `d[key] = val` (setitem) | None | No | | `del d[key]` | None | No | | `d.get(key)` / `key in d` | `DICT_CONTAINS` for that key | Only if that key's presence changes | | `d.items()` / `d.keys()` / `d.values()` | `DICT_KEYS_MATCH` (forced) | Yes | | `len(d)` / `d.copy()` | `DICT_KEYS_MATCH` (forced) | Yes | Operations that genuinely observe the full key set (`items`, `keys`, `values`, `len`, `copy`) still install the guard. I think that's the right call — if you're iterating over all keys, the traced code is specialised to that set, and you *should* recompile when it changes. The win is that plain getitem/setitem — which is the hot path for tensordict and similar — doesn't drag in the full guard. ## Open questions 1. **Should this be the default for all dicts?** I don't think so — most dict usage in compiled code has a fixed schema, and the key-set guard is cheap insurance. But maybe there's an argument for only guarding operations that actually read the full key set, even without an explicit opt-in. That would mean changing `ConstDictVariable.__setitem__` directly to not call `install_dict_keys_match_guard`. I'd like to understand whether there's a correctness reason it's there today beyond conservatism. 2. **Tracking by `id()`.** Plain `dict` objects don't support arbitrary attributes, so we'd need to track marked dicts in a global `set[int]` keyed by `id()`, cleaned up on `torch._dynamo.reset()`. `id()` reuse after GC is theoretically possible but not a practical concern since the dict must be alive from `mark_dynamic_dict(d)` through to the compiled call. Still, a `weakref`-based approach would be cleaner if we can find one that works for plain dicts (they're not weakly referenceable by default). 3. **Interaction with `__delitem__` / `pop` / `clear`.** The current prototype makes `install_dict_keys_match_guard` a no-op for all of these. For `__delitem__` specifically, the side-effects replay uses `dict.clear()` + `dict.update()` when `should_reconstruct_all` is set, which should be correct regardless of extra keys. Worth double-checking edge cases. 4. **Dict subclass contract.** An alternative to `mark_dynamic_dict` is a class-level opt-in (e.g. `__dynamo_dynamic_keys__ = True` on a dict subclass). That might be more ergonomic for libraries like tensordict that always want the behaviour on their internal dict. The two aren't mutually exclusive. ## References - #175624 - draft PR implementing this RFC - pytorch/tensordict#1606 — original bug report with recompilation logs - pytorch/tensordict#1605 — merged workaround using `@torch.compiler.disable` - `NNModuleHooksDictVariable` in `torch/_dynamo/variables/dicts.py` - existing precedent for skipping dict guards (hooks dicts don't guard at all) ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_ cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo @oulgen @jamesjwu @engineer_03 @engineer_21_gh @laithsakka @masnesral @coconutruben @aditvenk

title: Duplicate SDPA Input Constraints
labels: module: performance, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: sdpa, bot-triaged
body: When applying constraints to SDPA, if K==V, it's doing duplicate copy. Small repro where you see 2 identical empty_strided_cuda: ``` import torch import torch._inductor.config as inductor_config from torch._inductor.utils import run_and_get_code def fn(q, kv): kv_act = torch.relu(kv) return torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, kv_act, kv_act) q = torch.randn(2, 2, 16, 32, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float16) kv = torch.randn(2, 2, 16, 32, device="cuda", dtype=torch.float16) _, (code,) = run_and_get_code(torch.compile(fn), q, kv) print(code) ``` cc @jerryzh168 @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [inductor] volo_d1_224 will run into torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded
labels: oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug ```python benchmarks/dynamo/timm_models.py --inference --amp --amp-dtype float16 -d cuda -n10 --accuracy --disable-cudagraphs --only volo_d1_224 --cold-start-latency title: [dynamo] Tracking issue for itertools polyfills
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: This issue lists the `itertools` members, whether they have a polyfill or are supported in `IterToolsVariable.call_function` directly, and the status of some tests. More specifically, it lists if the CPython tests in `test_itertools.py` named after this member (using `-k membername`) complete successfully when run with `PYTORCH_TEST_WITH_DYNAMO=1` after disabling the `dynamo_expected_failures` mechanism. Note that some of these tests have been edited, by disabling test cases that don't really make sense in the dynamo setting, or by wrapping some cases in `with torch._dynamo.error_on_graph_break(False)`. | member | has polyfill? | supported in `IterToolsVariable`? | CPython tests succeed? | | :---- | :----: | :-----: | :-----: | | `accumulate` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `batched` | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | | `chain` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `chain.from_iterable` | ✔ | ✕ | ✔ | | `compress` | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | | `count` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `cycle` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `dropwhile` | ✔ | ✕ | ✔ | | `filterfalse` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `groupby` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `islice` | ✔ | ✕ | (1) | | `pairwise` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `repeat` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `starmap` | in progress | ✕ | in progress | | `takewhile` | ✔ | ✕ | ✔ | | `tee` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `zip_longest` | ✔ | ✕ | ✕ | | `product` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `permutations` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `combinations` | ✕ | ✔ | ✕ | | `combinations_with_replacement` | ✕ | ✕ | ✕ | (1): The test `TestPurePythonRoughEquivalents.test_islice_recipe` is disabled with `@skipIfTorchDynamo`, but that decorator is itself not currently supported, so we can't easily make this test pass. (@guilhermeleobas suggested I file this.) cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: torch.export.load holds GIL during tensor deserialization, preventing parallel loading
labels: oncall: pt2, export-triaged, oncall: export, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.export.load()` holds the GIL for ~62% of wall time during model loading, preventing effective multi-threaded parallelism. This is due to the `PT2ArchiveReader.read_bytes()` path using `PyTorchFileReader.get_record()` which holds the GIL during both the zip file read and Python `bytes` object construction. In contrast, `torch.load()` (state_dict path) achieves near-linear thread scaling (7.16x with 8 threads) because it uses `get_storage_from_record` which returns a tensor directly. ### Root cause The `torch.export.load` deserialization path goes through `_build_file_map` → `read_bytes` → `get_record`: **`get_record` binding (torch/csrc/jit/python/init.cpp:1600):** ```cpp "get_record", [](PyTorchStreamReader& self, const std::string& key) { auto [data, size] = self.getRecord(key); // GIL held: reads from zip return py::bytes(reinterpret_cast<const char*>(data.get()), size); // GIL held: copies to py::bytes }) ``` No `pybind11::gil_scoped_release`. The GIL is held for the entire zip read + Python bytes construction per tensor. The data is then copied *again* through `torch.frombuffer()` + `.to(device)` in `_create_flat_tensor_from_bytes`. **Compare with `torch.load`** which uses `get_storage_from_record` (init.cpp:1611) — returns a `Tensor` directly, no Python bytes intermediate, and the per-tensor granularity allows GIL switching between calls. ### Profiling data Profiled with a ~1.4B parameter MLP model on torch 2.10.0, loading with `ThreadPoolExecutor`: ``` Total Samples 500 GIL: 62.37%, Active: 100.00%, Threads: 33 %Own %Total OwnTime TotalTime Function (filename) 66.67% 66.67% 3.46s 3.46s read_bytes (torch/export/pt2_archive/_package.py) 32.26% 32.26% 2.12s 2.13s _create_flat_tensor_from_bytes (torch/export/pt2_archive/_package.py) ``` ### Benchmark: `torch.jit.load` vs `torch.load` (state_dict) parallel scaling Tested with a 4.8B parameter transformer model, PyTorch 2.12.0a0+gitacd5a39: | Path | Threads | Sequential (s) | Parallel (s) | Speedup | |------|---------|---------------|--------------|---------| | `torch.jit.load` | 2 | 23.0 | 19.5 | 1.18x | | `torch.jit.load` | 4 | 46.0 | 39.1 | 1.18x | | `torch.jit.load` | 8 | 91.9 | 78.2 | 1.18x | | `torch.load` | 2 | 23.0 | 10.5 | 2.19x | | `torch.load` | 4 | 46.0 | 11.1 | 4.13x | | `torch.load` | 8 | 92.6 | 12.9 | 7.16x | `torch.jit.load` is also GIL-bound (99.7% of time in `torch.import_ir_module` which has no `gil_scoped_release` in its pybind binding in `script_init.cpp`), but that path is being deprecated. ### Suggested fix Change `_build_file_map` in `torch/export/pt2_archive/_package.py` to use `get_storage_from_record` instead of `get_record`. The tensor metadata (`dtype`, `sizes`) is already available in `payload_meta.tensor_meta` at that call site, so the switch should be straightforward. This would: 1. Eliminate the GIL-held `py::bytes` construction 2. Remove the double data copy (file → C++ buffer → Python bytes → tensor) 3. Match the `torch.load` path which already achieves near-linear parallel scaling ### Versions PyTorch 2.10.0 (profiling data), PyTorch 2.12.0a0+gitacd5a39 (benchmark data) Reproduced on Linux x86_64, CPU-only (`map_location='cpu'`) cc @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4

title: [Inductor] Out-of-Bounds Memory Read via `resize` on Overlapping Views
labels: module: crash, triaged, module: memory format, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, pt2: ubn, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using `torch.compile` (Inductor), calling `torch.ops.aten.resize` (or `Tensor.resize_`) on an overlapping view (e.g., created via `as_strided` with stride 0) incorrectly unwraps the view and reads from its base storage with a sequential stride of 1. If the view's size is larger than the base storage's allocation, this results in a deterministic out-of-bounds (OOB) memory read. In eager mode, the correct tensor is materialized without reading past the original allocation of the base tensor. In compiled mode, arbitrary out-of-bounds memory can be read and returned to the user, potentially leaking sensitive data or causing segmentation faults. ## Reproducing the Bug The following minimal script demonstrates the vulnerability: ```python import torch def fn(x): # Base tensor `x` has size 10. # We create a valid overlapping view of size 100 pointing entirely inside `x` view = torch.as_strided(x, (100,), (0,)) # In Inductor, resizing this view unwraps it to the 10-element base, # but wrongly accesses it with stride 1 for the first 50 elements, # resulting in reading 40 elements of out-of-bounds memory. return torch.ops.aten.resize(view, (50,)) if __name__ == "__main__": x = torch.ones(10) compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True) eager_res = fn(x) compiled_res = compiled_fn(x.clone()) print("Eager expected range:", eager_res.min().item(), "to", eager_res.max().item()) print("Compile actual range:", compiled_res.min().item(), "to", compiled_res.max().item()) if compiled_res.max().item() > 1.0 or compiled_res.min().item() < 1.0: print("\\n[BUG CONFIRMED] Compiled tensor contains garbage/out-of-bound memory:") print(compiled_res) ``` ## Outputs **Eager Mode (Expected):** ``` Eager expected range: 1.0 to 1.0 ``` **Compiled Mode (Bug):** ``` Compile actual range: 0.0 to 1.0 [BUG CONFIRMED] Compiled tensor contains garbage/out-of-bound memory: tensor([1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 1.0000e+00, 0.0000e+00, 0.0000e+00, 9.8950e-08, 1.4013e-45, 0.0000e+00, 0.0000e+00, 1.4013e-45, 0.0000e+00, ... ]) ``` Notice that the first 10 elements are correctly `1.0` (from `x`), but elements 11-50 are garbage data from adjacent memory locations since they are read using stride 1 outside the 10-element bounds of `x`. ## Root Cause Analysis The vulnerability exists in how PyTorch Inductor lowers the `aten.resize` operation. When it encounters a view, it determines `old_numel` based on the view's size. It unwraps the view to the base storage (which may be significantly smaller, as in extreme overlapping cases with stride 0) and creates an unrolled tensor spanning up to `old_numel` elements using `stride=1`. This accesses memory beyond the allocated bounds of the base storage. ### Versions [pip3] numpy==2.4.2 [pip3] optree==0.19.0 [pip3] torch==2.12.0a0+git93b243e [conda] numpy 2.4.2 py311h4bb6f22_0 [conda] numpy-base 2.4.2 py311h23175f9_0 [conda] optree 0.19.0 pypi_0 pypi [conda] torch 2.12.0a0+git93b243e pypi_0 pypi cc @jamesr66a @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Numerical discrepancy in nn.LSTM between eager and compile modes with INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING
labels: module: numerical-stability, module: nn, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile() with INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING enabled on nn.LSTM, the output differs from eager mode even with identical device (cuda) and dtype (float32). The relative L2 error is 2.4009e-04, which exceeds the 1e-4 threshold. Details: Same configuration: Both modes use cuda device and float32 dtype, only the optimization mode differs (eager vs compile with INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING) Data validation: All inputs and outputs are valid (no NaN/Inf), with normal value ranges Error magnitude: Relative L2 error of 2.4009e-04 exceeds the 1e-4 threshold Impact: This numerical discrepancy may accumulate in deep LSTM models, potentially affecting final predictions Comparison results: Device switch (cpu vs cuda): OK (5.4181e-06 < 1e-4) Dtype switch (float16 vs float32): Exceeds threshold (4.1319e-04) - expected due to precision loss Optimization switch (compile vs eager): Exceeds threshold (2.4009e-04) - this is the main issue Expected behavior: Eager and compile modes should produce identical (or within acceptable tolerance < 1e-4) numerical results when device and dtype are identical. Actual behavior: Compile mode with INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING produces a relative L2 error of 2.4009e-04 compared to eager mode, indicating a numerical inconsistency that should not occur. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn import os import contextlib torch.manual_seed(42) torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42) @contextlib.contextmanager def set_inductor_env_vars(): original = os.environ.get("INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING") try: os.environ["INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING"] = "1" yield finally: if original is None: os.environ.pop("INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING", None) else: os.environ["INDUCTOR_SHAPE_PADDING"] = original def compare(t_a, t_b): a, b = t_a.detach().cpu().float(), t_b.detach().cpu().float() max_abs = torch.abs(a - b).max().item() l2_a, l2_b = torch.norm(a), torch.norm(b) rel_l2 = (torch.norm(a - b) / torch.maximum(l2_a, l2_b).clamp(min=1e-8)).item() return max_abs, rel_l2 def run_mode(lstm_layer, x_in, dev, dtype, opt): lstm = lstm_layer.to(dev).to(dtype).eval() if opt != "0000": with set_inductor_env_vars(): lstm = torch.compile(lstm) with torch.no_grad(): return lstm(x_in.to(dev).to(dtype))[0] def run(): x_in = torch.randn(64, 10, 50) lstm_layer = nn.LSTM(input_size=50, hidden_size=50, num_layers=1, batch_first=True, bidirectional=False) print("Input: shape={}".format(x_in.shape)) print("Input - min: {:.6f}, max: {:.6f}, mean: {:.6f}".format(x_in.min().item(), x_in.max().item(), x_in.mean().item())) print("Input - has NaN: {}, has Inf: {}".format(torch.isnan(x_in).any().item(), torch.isinf(x_in).any().item())) print("=" * 60) modes = [ ("Mode A (cuda/f32/0000)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0000'), ("Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000)", 'cpu', torch.float32, '0000'), ("Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000)", 'cuda', torch.float16, '0000'), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010)", 'cuda', torch.float32, '0010') ] outputs = {} for label, dev, dtype, opt in modes: print(f"\\n>>> Running {label} ...") outputs[label] = run_mode(lstm_layer, x_in, dev, dtype, opt) has_nan = torch.isnan(outputs[label]).any().item() has_inf = torch.isinf(outputs[label]).any().item() print(f" ✓ {label} executed successfully") print(f" Output - min: {outputs[label].min().item():.6f}, max: {outputs[label].max().item():.6f}, mean: {outputs[label].mean().item():.6f}") print(f" Output - has NaN: {has_nan}, has Inf: {has_inf}") ma_name = modes[0][0] for switch_idx in range(1, len(modes)): mb_name = modes[switch_idx][0] max_d, max_rd = compare(outputs[ma_name], outputs[mb_name]) exceeds = "Yes" if max_rd > 1e-4 else "No" status = "DIFF > 1e-4" if max_rd > 1e-4 else "OK" print("\\n" + "="*120) print(f"Comparison: {ma_name} vs {mb_name}") print("="*120) print(f"{'Layer Name':<40} | {'Max Abs Error':<15} | {'Rel L2 Error':<15} | {'> 1e-4':<8} | {'Status'}") print("-" * 120) print(f"{'lstm1':<40} | {max_d:.4e} | {max_rd:.4e} | {exceeds:<8} | {status}") print("="*120) if __name__ == "__main__": run() ``` ``` Input: shape=torch.Size([64, 10, 50]) Input - min: -4.590487, max: 4.220885, mean: 0.002711 Input - has NaN: False, has Inf: False ============================================================ >>> Running Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) ... ✓ Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -0.581809, max: 0.548356, mean: -0.007435 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) ... ✓ Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -0.581811, max: 0.548357, mean: -0.007435 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) ... ✓ Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) executed successfully Output - min: -0.582031, max: 0.548340, mean: -0.007435 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False >>> Running Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) ... ✓ Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) executed successfully Output - min: -0.581794, max: 0.548316, mean: -0.007436 Output - has NaN: False, has Inf: False ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Device (cpu/f32/0000) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ lstm1 | 7.0035e-06 | 5.4181e-06 | No | OK ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/0000) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ lstm1 | 3.3201e-04 | 4.1319e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ======================================================================================================================== Comparison: Mode A (cuda/f32/0000) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/0010) ======================================================================================================================== Layer Name | Max Abs Error | Rel L2 Error | > 1e-4 | Status ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ lstm1 | 2.1362e-04 | 2.4009e-04 | Yes | DIFF > 1e-4 ======================================================================================================================== ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.6 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: Could not collect Libc version: glibc-2.39 Python version: 3.10.19 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Jan 26 2026, 23:45:08) [GCC 14.3.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: title: [XPU][INDUCTOR] TestInductorOpInfoXPU::test_comprehensive_ tests failing for random ops with Exception: The values for attribute 'stride()' do not match!
labels: module: tests, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: xpu, bot-triaged
body: ### Problem Description I spotted that some tests from: > test/inductor/test_torchinductor_opinfo.py::TestInductorOpInfoXPU::test_comprehensive are randomly failing. Here are several examples of mentioned test failing for different ops: [xpu · pytorch/pytorch@eaa0221](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/actions/runs/22332254587/job/64619048419#step:14:16405) [xpu · pytorch/pytorch@dc580b1](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/actions/runs/22233238997/job/64321322640#step:14:4701) [xpu · pytorch/pytorch@60dc00d](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/actions/runs/22207166979/job/64235489878#step:14:6367) All of them have the same error message: > Exception: The values for attribute 'stride()' do not match! ### Considerations and findings My findings are that the stride comparison (which now fails) was disabled until month ago, when in was enabled in this commit: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/10ca1e95eaf390f11139ec6226e2ab0ae2ba01e3 with skip list for operators that were failing after enabling. Then about two weeks ago, the fix for most failing operators was introduced and most of operators were removed from the skip list: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/27f3af2feacb6f2e0936bca89d9d95718305339a Between those two above changes, there was a XPU-specific skip list added to this test function: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/4cc755e80980d94f44a91567da695f8b48c6c6df The reasoning was that: > On XPU, Inductor may apply additional layout optimizations compared to CUDA, which can legitimately change tensor strides relative to eager mode. As a result, enforcing exact stride equality with eager is not always appropriate on XPU, and the exact stride checks need to be relaxed for certain ops. ### Potential Solution I suppose that it may be needed to add more ops to the skip list for XPU or maybe even all exact stride checks should be disabled on XPU if indeed XPU inductor may introduce additional layout optimizations relative to eager mode. cc @mruberry @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @gujinghui @fengyuan14 @guangyey
