title: Dtensor Shard->Replicate redistribution corrupts symbolic shapes under torch.compile
labels: oncall: distributed, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: dtensor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When a DTensor with Shard(dim) placement on a dynamically-sized dimension is redistributed to Replicate, the gathered dimension's symbolic shape becomes n*ceil(s/n) instead of s. This corrupted expression propagates through all downstream operations (view, transpose, reshape, to_local), causing shape mismatches in compilation pipelines. Root cause: Shard._to_replicate_tensor in placement_types.py 1. torch.chunk uses ceiling division: local_size = (s + n - 1) // n 2. all_gather concatenates n chunks: gathered = n * ((s + n - 1) // n) 3. _maybe_unpad_tensor checks "s % n != 0" — evaluates False for even s, so no unpadding occurs 4. Result has symbolic size n*ceil(s/n) ≠ s (SymPy cannot simplify) ### Reproduction Run: `torchrun --nproc_per_node=2 repro_redistribute_bug.py` With the following ode as `repro_redistribute_bug.py` ```python import torch import torch.distributed as dist import torch.nn as nn from torch.distributed.device_mesh import init_device_mesh from torch.distributed.tensor import DTensor, Replicate, Shard from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import ( ColwiseParallel, parallelize_module, PrepareModuleInput, RowwiseParallel, SequenceParallel, ) class AttentionBlock(nn.Module): def __init__(self, dim, n_heads, head_dim): super().__init__() self.wq = nn.Linear(dim, n_heads * head_dim, bias=False) self.n_heads = n_heads self.head_dim = head_dim def forward(self, x): q = self.wq(x) # (batch, seqlen, n_heads * head_dim) bs = q.shape[0] q = q.view(bs, -1, self.n_heads, self.head_dim) q = q.transpose(1, 2) # (batch, n_heads, seqlen, head_dim) return q class TransformerLayer(nn.Module): def __init__(self, dim, n_heads, head_dim): super().__init__() self.tok_embeddings = nn.Embedding(1000, dim) self.norm = nn.RMSNorm(dim) self.attention = AttentionBlock(dim, n_heads, head_dim) def forward(self, input_ids): h = self.tok_embeddings(input_ids) h = self.norm(h) q = self.attention(h) # DTensor (batch, n_heads, seqlen, head_dim) Shard(1) if isinstance(q, DTensor): return q.to_local() return q def run(): dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl") rank = dist.get_rank() world_size = dist.get_world_size() device = torch.device(f"cuda:{rank}") torch.cuda.set_device(device) mesh = init_device_mesh("cuda", (world_size,), mesh_dim_names=("tp",)) model = TransformerLayer(dim=64, n_heads=8, head_dim=8).to(device) # TP plan: SequenceParallel shards seqlen, PrepareModuleInput gathers it back parallelize_module( model, mesh, { "tok_embeddings": RowwiseParallel( input_layouts=Replicate(), output_layouts=Shard(1), use_local_output=False, ), "norm": SequenceParallel(use_local_output=False), "attention": PrepareModuleInput( input_layouts=(Shard(1),), desired_input_layouts=(Replicate(),), ), "attention.wq": ColwiseParallel(use_local_output=False), }, ) # Custom backend to inspect symbolic shapes in the Dynamo FX graph def debug_backend(gm, example_inputs): if rank == 0: # Use print_readable to get annotated shapes import io, sys old_stdout = sys.stdout sys.stdout = buf = io.StringIO() try: gm.print_readable() finally: sys.stdout = old_stdout code = buf.getvalue() for line in code.split('\\n'): if 'prim_to_local' in line and ':' in line and '=' in line: print(f" Actual: {line.strip()}") from torch._inductor.compile_fx import compile_fx return compile_fx(gm, example_inputs) import copy compiled = torch.compile(copy.deepcopy(model), backend=debug_backend, dynamic=True) input_ids = torch.randint(0, 1000, (1, 100), device=device) torch._dynamo.mark_dynamic(input_ids, 1) # seqlen is dynamic if rank == 0: print("Compiling with dynamic seqlen...") print("Expected: prim_to_local shape should be [1, 4, s_N, 8]") print("Bug: prim_to_local shape is [1, 4, 2*(((s_N + 1)//2)), 8]") print() out = compiled(input_ids) if rank == 0: print(f"\\n Runtime output shape: {out.shape}") # Verify runtime correctness with different seqlen out2 = compiled(torch.randint(0, 1000, (1, 200), device=device)) if rank == 0: assert out.shape == torch.Size([1, 4, 100, 8]) assert out2.shape == torch.Size([1, 4, 200, 8]) print(f" Reuse with seqlen=200: {out2.shape}") print(" Runtime shapes are correct (bug only affects symbolic shapes)") dist.destroy_process_group() if __name__ == "__main__": run() ``` ### Versions Collecting environment information... PyTorch version: 2.11.0a0+git1c3d76e Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.9 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: CentOS Stream 9 (x86_64) GCC version: (GCC) 11.5.0 20240719 (Red Hat title: Numerical discrepancy in nn.Embedding between eager and compile modes
labels: triage review, module: numerical-stability, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile() on nn.Embedding layer, the output differs from eager mode even with identical device (cuda) and dtype (float32). The relative L2 error is 2.0980e-04, which exceeds the 1e-4 threshold. **Key observations:** - Same device (cuda) and dtype (float32), only optimization mode differs - Weight range: [-0.0920, 0.0921] (normal, no extreme values) - All outputs are valid (no NaN/Inf) - This discrepancy could accumulate in deep models, potentially affecting downstream predictions **Expected behavior:** Eager and compile modes should produce identical (or within acceptable tolerance) numerical results for the same inputs. **Actual behavior:** Compile mode produces relative L2 error of 2.0980e-04 compared to eager mode. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn import os torch.manual_seed(42) torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(42) def compare(t_a, t_b): t_a_f32 = t_a.detach().cpu().float() t_b_f32 = t_b.detach().cpu().float() abs_diff = torch.abs(t_a_f32 - t_b_f32) max_abs_error = abs_diff.max().item() l2_norm_a = torch.norm(t_a_f32) l2_norm_b = torch.norm(t_b_f32) l2_norm_diff = torch.norm(t_a_f32 - t_b_f32) denom_l2 = torch.maximum(l2_norm_a, l2_norm_b).clamp(min=1e-8) rel_l2_error = (l2_norm_diff / denom_l2).item() return max_abs_error, rel_l2_error def run_mode(wpe_layer, position_ids, dev, dtype, opt): wpe = wpe_layer.to(dev).to(dtype).eval() if opt == "compile": os.environ["INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION"] = "1" wpe = torch.compile(wpe) elif "INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION" in os.environ: del os.environ["INDUCTOR_EPILOGUE_FUSION"] with torch.no_grad(): return wpe(position_ids.to(dev)) wpe_layer = nn.Embedding(1024, 768) torch.nn.init.normal_(wpe_layer.weight, mean=0.0, std=0.02) position_ids = torch.arange(16, dtype=torch.long).unsqueeze(0) assert not torch.isnan(wpe_layer.weight).any() and not torch.isinf(wpe_layer.weight).any() assert position_ids.min() >= 0 and position_ids.max() < 1024 weight_min, weight_max = wpe_layer.weight.min().item(), wpe_layer.weight.max().item() assert abs(weight_min) < 1e6 and abs(weight_max) < 1e6 print(f"Input: weight[{weight_min:.4f}, {weight_max:.4f}], no NaN/Inf") modes = [ ("Mode A (cuda/f32/eager)", "cuda", torch.float32, "eager"), ("Switch Device (cpu/f32/eager)", "cpu", torch.float32, "eager"), ("Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/eager)", "cuda", torch.float16, "eager"), ("Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/compile)", "cuda", torch.float32, "compile") ] outputs = {label: run_mode(wpe_layer, position_ids, dev, dtype, opt) for label, dev, dtype, opt in modes} for label, output in outputs.items(): assert not torch.isnan(output).any() and not torch.isinf(output).any() out_min, out_max = output.min().item(), output.max().item() assert abs(out_min) < 1e6 and abs(out_max) < 1e6 print("Output: all valid, no NaN/Inf") threshold = 1e-4 for i in range(1, len(modes)): max_abs, rel_l2 = compare(outputs[modes[0][0]], outputs[modes[i][0]]) print(f"\\n{modes[0][0]} vs {modes[i][0]}") print(f"Max Absolute Error: {max_abs:.4e}") print(f"Relative L2 Error: {rel_l2:.4e}") print(f"Exceeds 1e-4 threshold: {'Yes' if rel_l2 > threshold else 'No'}") ``` ``` Input: weight[-0.0920, 0.0921], no NaN/Inf Output: all valid, no NaN/Inf Mode A (cuda/f32/eager) vs Switch Device (cpu/f32/eager) Max Absolute Error: 0.0000e+00 Relative L2 Error: 0.0000e+00 Exceeds 1e-4 threshold: No Mode A (cuda/f32/eager) vs Switch Dtype (cuda/f16/eager) Max Absolute Error: 3.0376e-05 Relative L2 Error: 2.0980e-04 Exceeds 1e-4 threshold: Yes Mode A (cuda/f32/eager) vs Switch Optimization (cuda/f32/compile) Max Absolute Error: 3.0376e-05 Relative L2 Error: 2.0980e-04 Exceeds 1e-4 threshold: Yes ``` ### Versions PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu126 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.6 ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Ubuntu 24.04.3 LTS (x86_64) GCC version: (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04) 13.3.0 Clang version: Could not collect CMake version: Could not collect Libc version: glibc-2.39 Python version: 3.10.19 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Jan 26 2026, 23:45:08) [GCC 14.3.0] (64-bit runtime) Python platform: Linux-6.8.0-90-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.39 Is CUDA available: True CUDA runtime version: 12.6.20 CUDA_MODULE_LOADING set to: GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU 1: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Nvidia driver version: 560.35.03 cuDNN version: Could not collect Is XPU available: False HIP runtime version: N/A MIOpen runtime version: N/A Is XNNPACK available: True Caching allocator config: N/A CPU: Architecture: x86_64 CPU op-mode(s): 32-bit, 64-bit Address sizes: 40 bits physical, 48 bits virtual Byte Order: Little Endian CPU(s): 48 On-line CPU(s) list: 0-47 Vendor ID: GenuineIntel Model name: QEMU Virtual CPU version 2.5+ CPU family: 15 Model: 107 Thread(s) per core: 1 Core(s) per socket: 48 Socket(s): 1 Stepping: 1 BogoMIPS: 4190.15 Flags: fpu de pse tsc msr pae mce cx8 apic sep mtrr pge mca cmov pat pse36 clflush mmx fxsr sse sse2 ht syscall nx lm constant_tsc nopl xtopology cpuid tsc_known_freq pni Virtualization type: full L1d cache: 1.5 MiB (48 instances) L1i cache: 1.5 MiB (48 instances) L2 cache: 192 MiB (48 instances) L3 cache: 16 MiB (1 instance) NUMA node(s): 1 NUMA node0 CPU(s): 0-47 title: [inductor] dynamic=True produces unvectorized Triton loads for contiguous tensors
labels: module: performance, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: With `dynamic=True`, Inductor-generated Triton kernels use scalar memory access (`vec=1`) for contiguous tensors, while `dynamic=False` produces vectorized access (`vec=8`). On H100, this causes a ~27% regression on memory-bound kernels. The stride argument (`ks0`) does not receive a `tt.divisibility` hint, so Triton's coalescing pass picks `sizePerThread=[1,1]` instead of a vectorized layout. Adding `torch._check(x.shape[-1] % 16 == 0)` does not help -- the constraint is present in `ShapeEnv.axioms` but is not picked up by `statically_known_multiple_of`. **Repro:** ```python import torch import torch.utils.benchmark as benchmark def naive_rmsnorm(x, eps=1e-4): # A manual guard that should propagate down to codegen torch._check(x.shape[-1] % 16 == 0) mean_sq = torch.mean(x * x, dim=-1, dtype=torch.float32) scale = torch.rsqrt(mean_sq + eps).unsqueeze(-1) return (x.to(torch.float32) * scale).to(x.dtype), scale x = torch.randn(256*1024, 16*1024, dtype=torch.float16, device="cuda") for dynamic in [False, True]: fn = torch.compile(naive_rmsnorm, dynamic=dynamic) fn(x, 1e-4) torch.cuda.synchronize() @torch.inference_mode() def run(): fn(x, 1e-4) torch.set_num_threads(1) for _ in range(5): run() t = benchmark.Timer(stmt="run()", globals={"run": run}, num_threads=1) m = t.blocked_autorange(min_run_time=1.0) print(f"dynamic={str(dynamic):5s}: {m.median * 1e3:.4f} ms") ``` **Observed:** On H100 ``` dynamic=False: 5.5867 ms dynamic=True : 7.0567 ms ``` **Expected:** `dynamic=True` should match `dynamic=False` when the tensor is contiguous and the stride is divisible by 16. cc @jerryzh168 @chauhang @penguinwu @engineer_17 @engineer_15_gh @aditvenk @laithsakka @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [inductor] Incorrect output when compiling model with expand+broadcasted index put
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When compiling a function containing Tensor.expand() followed by a broadcasted index put (e.g., expanded[0, 1] = 1), torch.compile produces inconsistent output compared to eager. This issue disappears if the index put is not broadcasted (i.e., `expanded[0, 1, 1]=1`). To reproduce: ```python import torch torch.manual_seed(0) def fn(x, y): z = x @ y expanded = x.expand(1, 2, 4) expanded[0, 1] = 1 return z cfunc = torch.compile(fn, backend='inductor') x1 = torch.randn(1, 2, 4, dtype=torch.float32) y1 = torch.randn(1, 4, 4, dtype=torch.float32) x2 = x1.clone() y2 = y1.clone() out1 = fn(x1, y1) out2 = cfunc(x2, y2) torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` It seems that, the broadcasted index put is incorrectly applied to `z` under torch.compile. Maybe it is an issue during functionalization? Note that backend `eager` and `aot_eager` do to have this issue. ### Error logs AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 4 / 8 (50.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 7.357644081115723 at index (0, 1, 1) (up to 1e-05 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 3.269789218902588 at index (0, 1, 1) (up to 1.3e-06 allowed) ### Versions [pip3] numpy==2.4.2 [pip3] torch==2.12.0.dev20260223+cpu [pip3] torchvision==0.26.0.dev20260223+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] numpy 2.4.2 pypi_0 pypi [conda] torch 2.12.0.dev20260223+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] torchvision 0.26.0.dev20260223+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: `torch.compile` crashes with `InternalTorchDynamoError: AttributeError` when tracing generator iteration over tensor subclass
labels: oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When compiling a module with `torch.compile`, the code crashes with an internal error when iterating over a Python generator produced by a method on a tensor subclass. Eager execution works. ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn class YieldTensor(torch.Tensor): @staticmethod def wrap(t): return torch.Tensor._make_subclass(YieldTensor, t, require_grad=t.requires_grad) def iter_vals(self): flat = torch.Tensor.reshape(self, -1) for chunk in flat.split(1): yield from chunk class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): y = YieldTensor.wrap(x * 2) total = torch.zeros((), dtype=y.dtype, device=y.device) for value in y.iter_vals(): total = total + value return total m = module() x = torch.arange(8.0, requires_grad=True) output1 = m(x) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) ``` ### Error logs The failure is an `InternalTorchDynamoError` caused by an `AttributeError 'function' object has no attribute '__objclass__'`, which looks like an internal modeling/binding issue rather than a clean "unsupported" error or a graph break fallback. ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/117026-1-submit.py", line 26, in <module> output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 433, in __call__ return super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 912, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/117026-1-submit.py", line 17, in forward y = YieldTensor.wrap(x * 2) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 2034, in __call__ result = self._torchdynamo_orig_backend( File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/convert_frame.py", line 1784, in __call__ result = self._inner_convert( File title: torch.compile fails to trace try-except semantic and throw `RuntimeError`
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Applying `torch.linalg.inv(x)` on a non-square `x` is mathematically invalid and will raise a `RuntimeError`. Under eager mode, `RuntimeError` is correctly caught by the `except` block. In contrast, when using `torch.compile`, the program crashes immediately. Minimal reproducer: ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn class module(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): try: with torch.no_grad(): torch.linalg.inv(x) except RuntimeError: return x + 1 return x m = module() x = torch.randn(2, 3) output1 = m(x) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) # crash ``` ### Error logs ``` File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/152012-2-submit.py", title: torch.compile produces stale read with generator over mutated list
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug torch.compile produces behavior inconsistent with eager mode when a generator iterates over a list that is mutated after generator creation In eager mode, the generator correctly observes the updated list element. Under torch.compile, the generator appears to read a stale value, leading to inconsistent result. Minimal reproducer: ```python class M(nn.Module): def forward(self, x): lst = [torch.tensor(-1.)] g = (v for v in lst) # generator captures list reference lst[0] = x.mean() + 1 # mutation after generator creation val = next(g) # deferred read # if val < 0: # raise RuntimeError("stale read") return val m = M() x = torch.randn(4) print("eager:", m(x)) cm = torch.compile(m) print("compiled:", cm(x)) ``` ### Error logs The compiled version incorrectly reads the original -1. value instead of the updated value. ``` eager: tensor(1.3012) compiled: tensor(-1.) ``` ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: torch.compile does not support operator override for user-defined tensor subclass
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Magic method overriding for `torch.Tensor` subclasses may trigger internal tracing failures. In this case, overriding `__mul__` in a tensor subclass and explicitly converting the output back to the subclass using `as_subclass` can cause an assertion error during compilation. Eager execution works correctly Minimal reproducer: ```python import torch class VerboseTensor(torch.Tensor): @staticmethod def __new__(cls, base): return torch.Tensor._make_subclass(cls, base, base.requires_grad) def __mul__(self, other): return super().__mul__(other).as_subclass(VerboseTensor) class module(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x): vt = VerboseTensor(x) scaled = vt.__mul__(3.0) # invoking magic method on a tensor subclass return scaled + 1 if __name__ == "__main__": x = torch.randn(2) m = module() output1 = m(x) output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) ``` ### Error logs ``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/shawn/torchfuzz/validate_strategies/generated_cases_v3/128961-2-submit.py", line 21, in <module> output2 = torch.compile(m)(x) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 433, in __call__ return super().__call__(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1780, in _wrapped_call_impl return self._call_impl(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/shawn/anaconda3/envs/torch2.10/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 1791, in _call_impl return forward_call(*args, **kwargs) File title: [inductor] torch.compile produces inconsistent output for attention-like computation when only returning the final output tensor
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When compiling an attention-like function, the output is inconsistent between torch.compile and eager when q/k/v's dims is 4. Interestingly, if the function additionally returns intermediate tensors (attn, v), the inconsistency disappears. This issue disappears when backend is set to `aot_eager` or `eager` Here is the code to reproduce: ```python import torch torch.manual_seed(0) def foo(q, k, v, a): qk = q @ k.transpose(-2, -1) / 1.0 qk = qk + a attn = torch.softmax(qk, dim=-1) output = attn @ v return output # return output, attn, v # no inconsistency if also return attn and value compiled_foo = torch.compile(foo, backend="inductor") q = torch.randn(1, 1, 64, 64) k = torch.randn(1, 1, 64, 64) v = torch.randn(1, 1, 64, 64) a = torch.randn(64, 64) res1 = foo(q, k, v, a) res2 = compiled_foo(q, k, v, a) torch.testing.assert_close(res1, res2, equal_nan=True) ``` ### Error logs ``` AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 4096 / 4096 (100.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 3.2043964862823486 at index (0, 0, 5, 38) (up to 1e-05 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 25769.873046875 at index (0, 0, 39, 49) (up to 1.3e-06 allowed) ``` _No response_ ### Versions [pip3] numpy==2.4.2 [pip3] torch==2.12.0.dev20260223+cpu [pip3] torchvision==0.26.0.dev20260223+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] numpy 2.4.2 pypi_0 pypi [conda] torch 2.12.0.dev20260223+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] torchvision 0.26.0.dev20260223+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [Inductor][PrivateUse1]: Stride mismatch in constant_pad_nd between privateuse1 and native aten implementation
labels: triaged, module: padding, module: meta tensors, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: PrivateUse1, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I'm encountering a stride mismatch error when running a compiled model with constant_pad_nd operation. The error occurs during the execution of the generated inductor code: ``` W0224 09:29:19.873000 1093123 pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/debug_utils.py:591] no save_dir specified, will generate random data 33.3%W0224 09:29:19.874000 1093123 pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/debug_utils.py:620] could not load None, generating random data instead 66.7%W0224 09:29:19.899000 1093123 pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/debug_utils.py:620] could not load None, generating random data instead 100.0%W0224 09:29:19.900000 1093123 pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/debug_utils.py:620] could not load None, generating random data instead Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/mashaobin/fx.py", line 82, in <module> run_repro(mod, load_args, accuracy=False, command='run', save_dir=None, tracing_mode='real', check_str=None) File "/home/mashaobin/pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch_mlu/_dynamo/repro/after_aot.py", line 443, in run_repro return COMMAND_FNS[options.command](options, mod, load_args) File "/home/mashaobin/pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/repro/after_aot.py", line 1003, in repro_run compiled(list(args)) File "/home/mashaobin/pytorch_29/venv/pytorch/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/_inductor/output_code.py", line 613, in __call__ return self.current_callable(inputs) File "/tmp/torchinductor_mashaobin/ik/cikrprno54pcousodxdvqf4mlxc7a7nb4ejoconkujghqbebclrh.py", line 133, in call assert_size_stride(buf6, (1, 64, 640, 958), (39239680, 1, 61312, 64), 'torch.ops.aten.constant_pad_nd.default') AssertionError: expected size 64==64, stride 613120==1 at dim=1; expected size 640==640, stride 958==61312 at dim=2; expected size 958==958, stride 1==64 at dim=3 Error in op: torch.ops.aten.constant_pad_nd.default This error most often comes from a incorrect fake (aka meta) kernel for a custom op. Use torch.library.opcheck to test your custom op. See https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/library.html#torch.library.opcheck ``` fx_graph_runnable.py as follows: ``` import os import torch import torch_mlu from torch import tensor, device import torch.fx as fx from torch._dynamo.testing import rand_strided from math import inf import torch._inductor.inductor_prims device_str="mlu" import torch._dynamo.config import torch._inductor.config import load_args._version = 0 mod = Repro() if __name__ == '__main__': from torch._dynamo.repro.after_aot import run_repro with torch.no_grad(): run_repro(mod, load_args, accuracy=False, command='run', save_dir=None, tracing_mode='real', check_str=None) # To run it separately, do # mod, args = run_repro(mod, load_args, accuracy=False, command='get_args', save_dir=None, tracing_mode='real', check_str=None) # mod(*args) ``` This issue will not be reproduced in the CUDA environment because we have separate logic for allocating the output of the aten.constant_pad_nd in privateuse1. I've always thought that the function register in torch/_meta_registrations.py determines the output size/stride in the inductor. I modified constant_pad_nd in torch/_meta_registrations.py as follows, and the logic of this function in allocating output is consistent with the logic in aten.constant_pad_nd in privateuse1. However, the process_kernel of ir.ExternKernel does not use this function, but instead uses constant_pad_nd in aten/src/ATen/native/PadNd.cpp, which causes the size/stride to be inconsistent with the size/stride actually allocated by privateuse1. the aten.op logic (C++) isn't easy to patch. ``` @out_wrapper() def _constant_pad_nd_meta(input, pad, value=0): # same checks as decomposition in torch/_refs/__init__.py:constant_pad_nd() torch._check( len(pad) % 2 == 0, lambda: f"Length of pad must be even but instead it equals {len(pad)}", ) input_sizes = input.shape l_inp = len(input_sizes) l_pad = len(pad) // 2 l_diff = l_inp - l_pad torch._check( l_inp >= l_pad, lambda: "Length of pad should be no more than twice the number of " f"dimensions of the input. Pad length is {len(pad)} while the input has " f"{l_inp} dimensions.", ) # Modify by CAMBRICON memory_format = utils.suggest_memory_format(input) input_contiguous = input.contiguous(memory_format=memory_format) all_pads_is_zero = not any(p != 0 for p in pad) if all_pads_is_zero: return input_contiguous.clone() # if all(isinstance(p, utils.IntWithoutSymInt) and p <= 0 for p in pad): # c_input = input # for i in range(l_diff, l_inp): # pad_idx = 2 * (l_inp - i - 1) # if pad[pad_idx] < 0: # c_input = c_input.narrow( # i, -pad[pad_idx], c_input.shape[i] + pad[pad_idx] # ) # if pad[pad_idx + 1] < 0: # c_input = c_input.narrow(i, 0, c_input.shape[i] + pad[pad_idx + 1]) # return c_input.clone() # end Modify by CAMBRICON new_shape = list(input_sizes[:l_diff]) for i in range(l_pad): pad_idx = len(pad) - ((i + 1) * 2) new_dim = input_sizes[l_diff + i] + pad[pad_idx] + pad[pad_idx + 1] torch._check( new_dim >= 0, lambda: f"The input size {input_sizes[l_diff + i]}, plus negative padding " f"{pad[pad_idx]} and {pad[pad_idx + 1]} resulted in a negative output size, " f"which is invalid. Check dimension {l_diff + i} of your input.", ) new_shape.append(new_dim) return torch.empty( new_shape, dtype=input.dtype, device=input.device, requires_grad=input.requires_grad, memory_format=suggest_memory_format(input), ) ``` I have a few questions: 1. Should the output tensor from the meta function in _meta_registrations.py exactly match the strides produced by the native aten.constant_pad_nd implementation? If not, what invariants should be preserved? 2. For privateuse1 backends, should we consider enabling python_dispatch in ir.ExternalKernel to allow custom devices to override the meta behavior? 3. Are there any suggestions? ### Versions ``` Collecting environment information... title: [Inductor][CUDA][test] test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper fails with XPASS on CUDA.
labels: module: cuda, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: The test `test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper` in `inductor/test_gpu_cpp_wrapper.py` fails with [XPASS(strict)] on CUDA because of the xfail. **Test Class**: inductor/test_gpu_cpp_wrapper.py::TestGpuWrapper::test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper **Platform**: CUDA **Error**: XPASS(strict) - test was expected to fail but passes **Steps to Reproduce** 1. Build PyTorch with CUDA support. 2. Run: ` python test/run_test.py -i inductor/test_gpu_cpp_wrapper -k test_mm_plus_mm3` 4. Observe `TestGpuWrapper.test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper` failing with [XPASS(strict)] because it passes but is marked as expected to fail. **Root Cause** PR [#172780 ](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/172780) added an xfail on `test_mm_plus_mm3` in `test_select_algorithm.py` with the reason "C++ wrapper dynamic shapes fails on CUDA, fixed on ROCm." On CUDA, the test `TestGpuWrapper.test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper` actually passes, so it fails with `[XPASS(strict)]` error. Only the dynamic shapes variant `DynamicShapesGpuWrapperGpuTests.test_mm_plus_mm3_dynamic_shapes_gpu_wrapper` fails on CUDA. So the xfail is too broad: it marks both tests as expected to fail when only the dynamic shapes run fails. **Proposed fix:** Remove the xfail from `test_mm_plus_mm3` and skip the dynamic shapes variant on CUDA in `test_failures_gpu_wrapper` (similar to test_mm_plus_mm2_dynamic_shapes). So that The `TestGpuWrapper.test_mm_plus_mm3_gpu_wrapper` test runs and passes on CUDA. The dynamic shapes test is skipped on CUDA (where it fails) and runs on ROCm (where it passes). Hence, for `test_mm_plus_mm3`, if we remove the xfail on the test and use a skip in `test_failures_gpu_wrapper` the dynamic variant which is known to fail on CUDA is skipped and the XPASS issue will be resolved. I have a fix for this and if this is a good approach I can go ahead with this. **Code References** **Base test**: test/inductor/test_select_algorithm.py:299 (test_mm_plus_mm3) **GPU wrapper config**: test/inductor/test_gpu_cpp_wrapper.py:107-125 (test_failures_gpu_wrapper) CC: @engineer_02 @Chillee @engineer_18_gh @engineer_14 @jgong5 @engineer_20 @engineer_17 @ptrblck @csarofeen @ajtulloch @zheng-xq @morrison-turnansky @stmcgovern @cleonard530 @jewelkm89 @adabeyta @groenenboomj cc @ptrblck @msaroufim @eqy @jerryzh168 @tinglvv @nWEIdia @chauhang @penguinwu @engineer_17 @engineer_15_gh @aditvenk @laithsakka @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [Inductor] Need a CustomIRPass Abstraction for Loop‑Level IR Transformations
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug **Summary** TorchInductor currently lacks a proper abstraction for custom passes that operate on Loop‑level IR (List[BaseSchedulerNode]). This results in incorrect cache classification and forces IR‑level passes such as _pre_fusion_custom_pass to inherit from CustomGraphPass, which is designed only for FX graph transformations. This issue proposes introducing a new base class, CustomIRPass, to correctly represent and register Loop‑level IR passes and restore expected cache hit/miss behavior. **Background & Motivation** 1. Abstraction mismatch - _pre_fusion_custom_pass operates directly on Loop‑level IR scheduler nodes. - However, due to lack of an appropriate base class, it is currently forced to subclass CustomGraphPass, which applies to torch.fx.Graph transformations. - This causes conceptual inconsistency. 2. Incorrect cache classification - Inductor’s caching logic recognizes only CustomGraphPass. - As a result, IR‑level passes implemented as subclasses of CustomGraphPass are treated as unsupported callables, making Inductor skip caching entirely. - This led to avoidable cache misses during Intel PerfCore IPEX analysis. 3. Preserving uuid‑based caching guarantees - Custom passes contribute to deterministic cache keys via uuid(). - IR‑level passes must implement this without depending on FX‑specific base class. **Proposed Solution** **Introduce a new abstraction: CustomIRPass** A dedicated base class for Loop‑level IR transformations that will: - Provide the correct abstraction for Loop‑level IR changes - Register IR‑passes cleanly within Inductor - Ensure cache logic properly recognizes IR‑level custom passes - Maintain deterministic uuid() logic - Clearly separate FX‑level and IR‑level transformation flows Update _pre_fusion_custom_pass - Modify it to inherit from the newly introduced CustomIRPass. **Benefits** - Restores correct TorchInductor cache hit/miss behavior - Improves maintainability for custom IR passes - Reduces unnecessary recompilation in Intel PerfCore IPEX workflows and similar environments ### Versions Versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] intel_perfcore_for_pytorch==2.11.0 [pip3] numpy==2.3.2 [pip3] torch==2.11.0a0+gitafa9e35 [pip3] torchaudio==2.1.0+6ea1133 [pip3] torchvision==0.16.0+fbb4cc5 [pip3] triton==3.5.0+gita43f054c cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @marpioch @gujinghui @Jian-Zhang @vivek5-ai @draghan

title: `__getattribute__` tracing
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch torch.compile graph-breaks on every attribute access when nn.Module defines `__getattribute__`. If an `nn.Module` subclass defines `__getattribute__`, Dynamo inserts a graph break on every attribute access, even for attributes that don't hit the custom logic. With enough accesses, Dynamo skips the entire frame. Dynamo already traces `__getattribute__` successfully on plain user-defined objects (`UserDefinedObjectVariable`), but the `NNModuleVariable` path unconditionally calls `unimplemented()`. ```python class MyModule(torch.nn.Module): def __getattribute__(self, name): if name == "alias": return super().__getattribute__("real_attr") return super().__getattribute__(name) ``` This forces users to replace `__getattribute__` with per-field `@property` descriptors, which isn't always possible when the set of affected attributes isn't known at class definition time. ### Feature implementation In `_custom_getattr_fallback` (torch/_dynamo/variables/nn_module.py), replace the unconditional `unimplemented()` call with a `try` block that builds a `UserMethodVariable` for the class's `__getattribute__` and traces through it — the same approach `UserDefinedObjectVariable` already uses. On `ObservedAttributeError` it falls through to `__getattr__`; on `Unsupported` it graph-breaks as before. In `has_key_in_generic_dict`, remove the guard entirely since it accesses `__dict__` via `object.__getattribute__`, which bypasses any custom override. cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @chauhang @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: torch.compile fails during backward for ConvNeXt LayerNorm2d (permute + layer_norm)
labels: high priority, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile` fails on a minimal model using `torchvision.models.convnext_tiny` on CPU. The same script runs correctly in eager mode. I happens on other models with the LayerNorm2d pattern of: ```python class LayerNorm2d(nn.LayerNorm): def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: x = x.permute(0, 2, 3, 1) x = F.layer_norm(x, self.normalized_shape, self.weight, self.bias, eps=self.eps) x = x.permute(0, 3, 1, 2) return x ``` Below is a minimal reproducible example that fails when the model is compiled. Removing torch.compile makes the script run successfully. ```python import torch from torch import nn from torchvision.models.convnext import convnext_tiny class ReproModel(nn.Module): def __init__(self) -> None: super().__init__() self.backbone = convnext_tiny() def forward(self, x1: torch.Tensor, x2: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: e1 = self.backbone(x1) e2 = self.backbone(x2) return e1.float().pow(2).mean() + e2.float().pow(2).mean() def main() -> None: device = torch.device("cpu") model = ReproModel().to(device) model = torch.compile(model) x1 = torch.randn(4, 3, 64, 64, device=device) x2 = torch.randn(4, 3, 64, 64, device=device) loss = model(x1, x2) loss.backward() if __name__ == "__main__": main() ``` More complex variants of this workload also fail when running on CUDA. ### Error logs Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/user/Programming/birder/repro_torch_compile_dino_v2_backward.py", line 30, in <module> main() File "/home/user/Programming/birder/repro_torch_compile_dino_v2_backward.py", line 26, in main loss.backward() File "/home/user/Programming/birder/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/torch/_tensor.py", line 630, in backward torch.autograd.backward( File "/home/user/Programming/birder/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/torch/autograd/__init__.py", line 364, in backward _engine_run_backward( File "/home/user/Programming/birder/.venv/lib/python3.11/site-packages/torch/autograd/graph.py", line 865, in _engine_run_backward return Variable._execution_engine.run_backward( # title: torch.compile crashes during autoregressive decode with growing KV cache in cudagraph_trees
labels: triaged, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `torch.compile` with inductor crashes during autoregressive decode when the KV cache grows via `torch.cat` each step. The crash comes from `dealloc_current_path_weakrefs()` in `cudagraph_trees.py` — it frees storages that are still needed as inputs to subsequent warmup nodes. This is the same issue as #154824. Crashes at step 1 with `mode="reduce-overhead"`, reproduces on PyTorch 2.8 and 2.10. I also found a secondary issue on the same codepath: line 2614 has a hard assert on `len(tensor_weakrefs) == len(stack_traces)` but these can diverge for the same reason noted in the comment at lines 2624-2627 for `outputs_weakrefs`. Fix for that in #175562. ### Repro ```python import torch class KVCacheModel(torch.nn.Module): def __init__(self, d=64): super().__init__() self.proj = torch.nn.Linear(d, d) def forward(self, x, cache=None): out = self.proj(x) if cache is not None: cache = torch.cat([cache, out], dim=1) else: cache = out return out, cache model = KVCacheModel().cuda().eval() compiled = torch.compile(model, mode="reduce-overhead") cache = None for step in range(10): torch.compiler.cudagraph_mark_step_begin() x = torch.randn(1, 1, 64, device="cuda") with torch.no_grad(): out, cache = compiled(x, cache) print(f"step {step}: cache shape {cache.shape}") ``` ``` step 0: cache shape torch.Size([1, 1, 64]) RuntimeError: Error: accessing tensor output of CUDAGraphs that has been overwritten by a subsequent run. ``` ### Versions - PyTorch 2.8.0a0+34c6371d24.nv25.08 (CUDA 13.0, Triton 3.3.1) - PyTorch 2.10.0a0+a36e1d39eb.nv26.01 (CUDA 13.1, Triton 3.6.0) - GPU: RTX 4080 Laptop (Ada Lovelace) - OS: Linux (Docker/WSL2) cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang

title: FlexAttention's `BlockMask._adjust` does not update the sequence lengths
labels: module: nn, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: flex attention, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using the `BlockMask._adjust` method, the output sequence lengths are not updated. For example: ```python import torch from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import create_block_mask, flex_attention def causal_mask_mod(b, h, q_idx, kv_idx): return q_idx >= kv_idx if __name__ == "__main__": device = "cuda" dtype = torch.float16 Q_LEN, KV_LEN = 32, 32 ADJ_Q_LEN, ADJ_KV_LEN = 16, 16 block_mask = create_block_mask( causal_mask_mod, B=None, H=None, Q_LEN=Q_LEN, KV_LEN=KV_LEN, ) # prints (32, 32) print("Block mask sequence lengths:", block_mask.seq_lengths) block_mask_adj = block_mask._adjust(ADJ_Q_LEN, ADJ_KV_LEN) # ⚠️ still prints (32, 32). I would expect (16, 16). print("Block mask sequence lengths after adjust:", block_mask_adj.seq_lengths) ``` This then causes errors if using the block mask on the expected adjusted shapes: ```python q = torch.randn((1, 1, ADJ_Q_LEN, 32), device=device, dtype=dtype) k = torch.randn((1, 1, ADJ_KV_LEN, 32), device=device, dtype=dtype) v = torch.randn((1, 1, ADJ_KV_LEN, 32), device=device, dtype=dtype) flex_attention(q, k, v, block_mask=block_mask_adj) ``` which raises ``` ValueError: block_mask was created for block_mask.shape=(1, 1, 128, 128) but got q_len=16 and kv_len=16. As the block mask was created for a larger length than you're using it for, you can either 1. create a new block mask with the correct length, or 2. 'adjust' the existing block mask to the correct length by calling block_mask._adjust(q_len, kv_len). This essentially 'crops' the block mask to the upper left corner, which does not work for all mask_mods! ``` The reason why I started using the otherwise private method `BlockMask._adjust` is exactly due to this suggestion, which kind of exposes it as public. ### Suggested fix Simply adding `seq_lengths=(new_q_len, new_kv_len)` here should do: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/449b1768410104d3ed79d3bcfe4ba1d65c7f22c0/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py#L812-L819 ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: while_loop vmap compatibility
labels: feature, triaged, module: vmap, oncall: pt2, module: functorch, module: higher order operators, module: pt2-dispatcher, bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch `torch.while_loop` has no `vmap` batching rule. It raises `KeyError(TransformType.Vmap)` when called inside `torch.vmap`. Other higher-order ops (`cond`, `scan`) have `vmap` rules but `while_loop` does not. ```python def fn(x): return torch.while_loop(lambda x: x.sum() < 10, lambda x: (x + 1,), (x,)) torch.vmap(fn)(torch.randn(4, 3)) # KeyError ``` This forces users to fall back to fixed-iteration for loops under vmap, running `max_iter` steps even when most batch elements converge in 3–5. For physics solvers this is a massive burden. ### Feature implementation Register a `TransformType.Vmap` implementation on `while_loop_op`, following the same pattern as `cond` and `scan`. The rule unwraps batched inputs, expands any unbatched carried inputs to the batch size (with `.contiguous()` to match body output strides), then runs the loop with two wrappers: `wrapped_cond_fn` calls `restore_vmap` on the user's `cond_fn` and reduces the per-element booleans with `.any()`; `wrapped_body_fn` calls `restore_vmap` on `body_fn` and uses `torch.where(active_mask, new_val, old_val)` to freeze converged elements. The loop runs until all batch elements are done. cc @zou3519 @chauhang @penguinwu @Chillee @samdow @kshitij12345 @ydwu4 @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: [inductor] model with torch.compile outputs inconsistent result when return value includes detached tensor
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, topic: fuzzer, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When compiling a function containing `div_`, `softmax` and returning x.detach(), the output of the compiled function is inconsistent with eager. Specifically, the function returns two elements (`z` and `x.detach()`), the inconsistency is observed on `z`. Interestingly, if we don't return `x.detach()` (only return `z`), there is no inconsistency. Also note that `TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES=1` is necessary to reproduce the issue. To reproduce: ```python import os os.environ['TORCHINDUCTOR_FORCE_DISABLE_CACHES'] = '1' # The inconsistency will occasionally disappear if remove this. import torch def fn(x, y): x.div_(y) z = torch.nn.functional.softmax(x, dim=-1) return z, x.detach() # The x.detach() part cannot be removed or changed to z, x, even though the inconsistency part is output[0]. torch.manual_seed(0) x1 = torch.randn(2, 4, 4, 4, dtype=torch.float64) y1 = torch.randn(4, 1, 1, dtype=torch.float64) # print(y1) x2 = x1.clone() y2 = y1.clone() cfunc = torch.compile(fn) output1 = fn(x1, y1) output2 = cfunc(x2, y2) torch.testing.assert_close(output1[0], output2[0], equal_nan=True) ``` ### Versions [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Unable to disable warning about tf32 precision
labels: triaged, enhancement, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: compile ux, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug This message is repeated once per GPU in all my runs: ``` /usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py:321: UserWarning: TensorFloat32 tensor cores for float32 matrix multiplication available but not enabled. Consider setting `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high')` for better performance. warnings.warn( ``` I want my fp32 matrix multiplications to be in fp32, not tf32. But there is no avenue to suppressing this warning besides filtering logs. `torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("highest")` does not provide a way to distinguish the default "highest" from a user-set "highest". So maybe we could add a flag `matmul_precision_set_manually` that `_warn_tf32_disabled()` would check. ### Versions ``` PyTorch version: 2.10.0 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.9 title: make_fx symbolic tracing produces wrong 2nd-order gradients through torch.sqrt
labels: module: autograd, triaged, module: fx, oncall: pt2, module: decompositions, bot-triaged, bot-mislabeled
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug `make_fx` with `tracing_mode="symbolic"` produces wrong 2nd-order gradients when the traced function involves `torch.sqrt` and `torch.autograd.grad(..., create_graph=True)`. The eager and traced results diverge by a relative error of ~40x, indicating that the decomposition of `sqrt` during tracing breaks the higher-order gradient computation. ## Reproduction ```python import torch import torch.nn as nn from torch.fx.experimental.proxy_tensor import make_fx torch.manual_seed(42) weight = torch.randn(16, 3, requires_grad=True) x = torch.randn(5, 3) target_energy = torch.randn(()) target_force = torch.randn(5, 3) def fn(x, weight): x = x.detach().requires_grad_(True) h = x @ weight.T # [N, D] # LayerNorm (manual, using sqrt) mean = h.mean(-1, keepdim=True) var = h.var(-1, keepdim=True, correction=0) h = (h - mean) / torch.sqrt(var + 1e-5) energy = (h ** 2).sum() force = -torch.autograd.grad(energy, x, create_graph=True)[0] return energy, force def compute_loss(energy, force): return nn.functional.mse_loss(energy, target_energy) + nn.functional.mse_loss(force, target_force) # Eager weight.grad = None e_eager, f_eager = fn(x, weight) compute_loss(e_eager, f_eager).backward() grad_eager = weight.grad.clone() # Traced with make_fx weight.grad = None traced = make_fx(fn, tracing_mode="symbolic", _allow_non_fake_inputs=True)( x.clone(), weight.clone().requires_grad_(True) ) e_traced, f_traced = traced(x, weight) compute_loss(e_traced, f_traced).backward() grad_traced = weight.grad.clone() rel_err = (grad_eager - grad_traced).abs().max().item() / (grad_eager.abs().max().item() + 1e-10) print(f"Eager weight.grad (first 5): {grad_eager.flatten()[:5].tolist()}") print(f"Traced weight.grad (first 5): {grad_traced.flatten()[:5].tolist()}") print(f"Max absolute error: {(grad_eager - grad_traced).abs().max().item():.4e}") print(f"Max relative error: {rel_err:.4e}") assert rel_err < 0.01, f"BUG: sqrt decomposition in make_fx produces wrong 2nd-order gradients (rel_err={rel_err:.4e})" ``` ## Observed output ``` Eager weight.grad (first 5): [-0.0019134351750835776, 0.015697872266173363, 0.022656477987766266, -0.00547990994527936, 0.003433838952332735] Traced weight.grad (first 5): [0.6118248105049133, -0.7227284908294678, -1.1761434078216553, 0.006602346897125244, -0.14402151107788086] Max absolute error: 2.0306e+00 Max relative error: 4.0184e+01 AssertionError: BUG: sqrt decomposition in make_fx produces wrong 2nd-order gradients (rel_err=4.0184e+01) ``` ## Expected behavior The traced function should produce the same gradients as the eager execution. The relative error should be near zero (floating point tolerance), not ~40x. ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: [DTensor] ExportedProgram.run_decompositions() fails with AssertionError: out is not NotImplemented
labels: oncall: distributed, oncall: pt2, oncall: export, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Code to repro: ```python import torch import torch.distributed as dist import torch.nn as nn from torch.distributed.tensor.parallel import parallelize_module, ColwiseParallel, RowwiseParallel from torch.distributed._tensor import init_device_mesh from torch._decomp import get_decompositions # Initialize distributed if not dist.is_initialized(): dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl") device_mesh = init_device_mesh("cuda", (dist.get_world_size(),)) rank = dist.get_rank() print(f"[Rank {rank}] PyTorch version: {torch.__version__}") import torch.utils._pytree import torch.distributed.tensor._dtensor_spec torch.utils._pytree.register_constant( torch.distributed.tensor._dtensor_spec.DTensorSpec ) class ToyModel(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super().__init__() self.in_proj = nn.Linear(10, 320) self.out_proj = nn.Linear(320, 160) def forward(self, x): return self.out_proj(torch.relu(self.in_proj(x))) model = ToyModel().to("cuda") parallelize_module(model.in_proj, device_mesh, ColwiseParallel()) parallelize_module(model.out_proj, device_mesh, RowwiseParallel()) inp = torch.rand(2, 10, device="cuda") print(f"[Rank {rank}] Model parallelized successfully") # Export the model - this works now with DTensorSpec registration print(f"[Rank {rank}] Exporting model...") exported_program = torch.export.export(model, (inp,), strict=False) print(f"[Rank {rank}] Export succeeded") # Now try to run decompositions - THIS FAILS print(f"\\n[Rank {rank}] Attempting to run decompositions on exported DTensor model...") try: # Get standard decompositions decomp_table = get_decompositions([ torch.ops.aten.embedding_dense_backward, torch.ops.aten.native_layer_norm_backward, torch.ops.aten.slice_backward, torch.ops.aten.select_backward, torch.ops.aten.norm.ScalarOpt_dim, torch.ops.aten.native_group_norm_backward, torch.ops.aten.upsample_bilinear2d.vec, torch.ops.aten.split.Tensor, torch.ops.aten.split_with_sizes, ]) decomposed = exported_program.run_decompositions(decomp_table) except AssertionError as e: print(f"[Rank {rank}] Decomposition failed with AssertionError:") print(f" {e}") except Exception as e: print(f"[Rank {rank}] Decomposition failed: {type(e).__name__}: {e}") dist.destroy_process_group() ``` Looks like DTensor dispatch fails in decomposition context. ### Versions torch 2.11.0.dev20260216+cu130 cc @H-Huang @awgu @wanchaol @fegin @fduwjj @wz337 @wconstab @d4l3k @pragupta @msaroufim @dcci @aditvenk @engineer_23 @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4

title: torch.compile(..., name="flex_attention")
labels: triaged, function request, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: compile ux, dynamo-triage-dec2025, bot-triaged
body: I propose that we add a name kwarg to torch.compile. This gives a compile region a name, which then can be used in other contexts in a useful way. The motivating use case for this is for SAC over torch.compile regions. Because torch.compile regions are all-or-nothing from the perspective of activation checkpointing, when we run with `wrap_inductor_compiled_regions` we replace the torch.compile with an `inductor_compiled_code` HOP. We have no way of easily telling what kind of compile region this corresponds to from just the HOP. With a name, we can put this name on the HOP, and a policy function can match on it. There's other ways we can use it too. For example, the name can be used to give better names to tlparse compiled regions. We can expose it in stack traces. cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @chauhang @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: custom_op + TensorSubclass + torch.compile interaction : Better error message or doc
labels: triaged, module: custom-operators, tensor subclass, oncall: pt2, module: pt2-dispatcher
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Using TensorSubclass and custom_op with torch.compile leads to an error because the `custom_op` implementation is called instead of the fake implementation during Dynamo tracing. Idea here is to have a custom_op which can directly handle a TensorSubclass. Repro: ```python import torch _passthrough_ops: set = set() class ScaledTensor(torch.Tensor): @staticmethod def __new__(cls, data, scale): t = torch.Tensor._make_wrapper_subclass(cls, data.shape, dtype=data.dtype, device=data.device) t._data = data t._scale = scale return t def __repr__(self): return f"ScaledTensor(shape={self.shape}, scale={self._scale})" def __tensor_flatten__(self): return ["_data"], {"scale": self._scale} @staticmethod def __tensor_unflatten__(inner_tensors, metadata, outer_size, outer_stride): return ScaledTensor(inner_tensors["_data"], metadata["scale"]) @classmethod def __torch_dispatch__(cls, func, types, args=(), kwargs=None): if func in _passthrough_ops: return super().__torch_dispatch__(func, types, args, kwargs or {}) def unwrap(t): return t._data if isinstance(t, ScaledTensor) else t return func(*torch.utils._pytree.tree_map(unwrap, args), **torch.utils._pytree.tree_map(unwrap, kwargs or {})) @torch.library.custom_op("mylib::double_v2", mutates_args=[]) def double_v2(x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor: if isinstance(x, ScaledTensor): # print(f"double_v2: x._data: {x._data}") ptr = x._data.data_ptr() return ScaledTensor(x._data * 2, x._scale) else: # print(f"double_v2: x: {x}") ptr = x.data_ptr() return x * 2 @double_v2.register_fake def _(x): if isinstance(x, ScaledTensor): print(f"Fake Kernel : x._data: {x._data}") return ScaledTensor(x.new_empty(x.shape, dtype=x.dtype, device=x.device), x._scale) else: print(f"Fake Kernel : x: {x}") return x.new_empty(x.shape, dtype=x.dtype, device=x.device) _passthrough_ops.add(torch.ops.mylib.double_v2.default) def fn(x): return torch.ops.mylib.double_v2(x) # Plain tensors work t = torch.randn(4, device="cuda") print("Plain Tensor: Eager Execution...") fn(t) print("Plain Tensor: torch.compile(fullgraph=True)...") r = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True)(t) t = ScaledTensor(torch.randn(4, device="cuda"), scale=0.5) print("ScaledTensor: Eager Execution...") fn(t) print("ScaledTensor: torch.compile(fullgraph=True)...") r = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True)(t) ``` Error ``` File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/variables/builder.py", line 3068, in wrap_fx_proxy_cls out: VTTypeAlias = _wrap_fx_proxy( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/variables/builder.py", line 3192, in _wrap_fx_proxy example_value = get_fake_value(proxy.node, tx, allow_non_graph_fake=True) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/utils.py", line 3632, in get_fake_value return _get_fake_value_impl(node, tx, allow_non_graph_fake) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/utils.py", line 3823, in _get_fake_value_impl _wrap_graph_break_with_torch_runtime_err( File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/utils.py", line 3621, in _wrap_graph_break_with_torch_runtime_err raise exc.with_traceback(e.__traceback__) from None File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/utils.py", line 3618, in _wrap_graph_break_with_torch_runtime_err gb_fn() File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/utils.py", line 3824, in <lambda> lambda: unimplemented( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/opt/pytorch/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/exc.py", line 631, in unimplemented raise Unsupported( torch._dynamo.exc.TorchRuntimeError: RuntimeError when making fake tensor call Explanation: Dynamo failed to run FX node with fake tensors: call_function mylib.double_v2(*(ScaledTensor(shape=torch.Size([4]), scale=0.5),), **{}): got RuntimeError("Cannot access data pointer of Tensor (e.g. FakeTensor, FunctionalTensor). If you're using torch.compile/export/fx, it is likely that we are erroneously tracing into a custom kernel. To fix this, please wrap the custom kernel into an opaque custom op. Please see the following for details: https://pytorch.org/tutorials/advanced/custom_ops_landing_page.html") Hint: Your code may result in an error when running in eager. Please double check that your code doesn't contain a similar error when actually running eager/uncompiled. You can do this by removing the `torch.compile` call, or by using `torch.compiler.set_stance("force_eager")`. ``` ### Versions main branch cc @engineer_17 @albanD @chauhang @penguinwu @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: Choose rounding mode when casting to dtype
labels: module: cuda, triaged, module: type promotion, function request, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: floatx (formerly float8), bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch `tensor.to(dtype)` performs a round-nearest conversion. This makes sense in most cases, but there are situations where other rounding modes are useful. For example in MXFP8 (microscaled float8) on Blackwell GPUs, we need to convert the scales to their next power of 2 and convert them to a float8_e8m0fnu (i.e., a biased uint8). This could be done in one shot via `scales.to(torch.float8_e8m0fnu, round="up")`. Which then would allow PyTorch to match this exactly to the optimized PTX instruction `cvt.rp.satfinite.ue8m0x2.f32` (`.rp` means round-positive). ### Alternatives Several less-readable alternatives, which will also make it harder for PyTorch and Inductor to understand the intent and thus optimize the code. For example one could go through torch.log and torch.ceil, or through torch.frexp, or do the cast with round-nearest and the conditionally adjust the result. ### Additional context _No response_ cc @ptrblck @msaroufim @eqy @jerryzh168 @tinglvv @nWEIdia @nairbv @mruberry @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Incorrect storage offsets propagation in inductor with as_strided
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, pt2: ubn, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug On performing qkv fusion on linear layers which are followed by as_strided ops, it was observed that the storage offsets are not getting propagated accordingly by inductor. The problem occurs when the fused qkv linear node is split back into q, k and v and they are connected down the path to an as_strided node without any non-view op in between. The storage offsets aren't adjusted according to the fused ops and that causes accuracy failures. A temporary fix is to give contiguous input to as_strided in such cases. Here's a sample test to reproduce the bug. The test passes when contiguous are added before the as-strided ops in model. Test file: https://gist.github.com/Rakul-Chauhan/a79b4d6d7bc9c50ac03a8f95248548c8 Here's the full trace .log file: Trace log file: https://gist.github.com/Rakul-Chauhan/4200f004a7885491036e31c2a85a7ea1 ### Error logs Test file output (without .contiguous()): Fusing QKV parallel mm operations. === QKV Fusion Test Results === Attn match: False K match: False V match: False Attn max diff: 4.994934558868408 Attn mean diff: 0.37558531761169434 K max diff: 3.816382646560669 K mean diff: 0.6493327021598816 K storage offset (eager): 0 K storage offset (compiled): 768 V max diff: 4.59495735168457 V mean diff: 0.6489904522895813 V storage offset (eager): 0 V storage offset (compiled): 1536 Test file output (with .contiguous before as_strided): Fusing QKV parallel mm operations. === QKV Fusion Test Results === Attn match: True K match: True V match: True Attn max diff: 0.0 Attn mean diff: 0.0 K max diff: 0.0 K mean diff: 0.0 K storage offset (eager): 0 K storage offset (compiled): 0 V max diff: 0.0 V mean diff: 0.0 V storage offset (eager): 0 V storage offset (compiled): 0 ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: SAC should treat inductor_compiled_code and other HOPs specially (pull out a more unique name) to prevent mixups
labels: module: activation checkpointing, triaged, enhancement, oncall: pt2, module: higher order operators, module: pt2-dispatcher, module: compile ux, bot-triaged
body: SAC keeps track of what specific operators it saw in forwards and ensures you do the same operators in order, which helps catch when you mess up and your recompute doesn't do the same thing as the first time. However, for HOPs like inductor_compiled_code, just tracking the HOP name isn't enough granularity: different inductor_compiled_code regions can be quite different! If we assign a unique name to these HOPs (even an id is good enough), we can also detect mixups. This would give us a better error message in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/175258 cc @soulitzer @chauhang @penguinwu @ydwu4 @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: [RFC][Inductor] PyTorch Inductor cuTile Backend
labels: feature, module: cuda, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: helion, bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch ## Summary This RFC proposes adding a cuTile backend to PyTorch Inductor, providing a complementary CUDA code generation path using the cuTile library. This would enable the broader community to benefit from Tile IR's bytecode portability, performance portability across GPU generations, and access to an optimizing compiler specifically designed for tile-based GPU programming. We have a working implementation that can be contributed in phases to facilitate review and integration. ## Motivation PyTorch Inductor currently uses Triton as the primary backend for CUDA code generation. We propose adding a cuTile backend as a complementary path that leverages a hardware-aligned tile-based programming model. **What is cuTile?** [cuTile](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cutile-python/#) is a Python library targeting [Tile IR](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/tile-ir/latest/index.html), a virtual instruction set enabling native, portable tile-based GPU programming. **Why This Matters:** - **Hardware & Performance Portability**: cuTile targets Tile IR, a virtual ISA analogous to PTX. This abstraction decouples kernel logic from specific hardware, allowing kernels (e.g., GEMM, attention) to be "written once" and re-targeted to different architectures. It ensures optimized performance across multiple GPU generations via compiler-level mapping rather than manual kernel rewrites. - **Enabling Higher-Level DSLs**: [Helion](https://github.com/pytorch/helion) is a Python-embedded DSL for authoring ML kernels that currently compiles to Triton. We are planning to add a Tile IR/cuTile backend to Helion; implementing Inductor cuTile support is a necessary prerequisite for this path, as it provides the foundational infrastructure required for Helion to target Tile IR. - **Competitive Performance**: cuTile provides performance competitive to cuBLAS for GEMMs and cuDNN for attention at large problem sizes. ## Proposed Design ### Architecture Overview We propose integrating the cuTile backend into Inductor's existing architecture at the code generation layer, maintaining symmetry with the current Triton implementation: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ PyTorch FX Graph │ └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Inductor IR (Pointwise, Reduction) │ └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ▼ ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Scheduler & Fusion │ └─────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────┴────────────┐ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Triton Backend │ │ cuTile Backend │ │ (tl.* APIs) │ │ (ct.* APIs) │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Triton JIT │ │ cuTile JIT │ │ Compiler │ │ Compiler │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ │ └────────────┬────────────┘ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ │ CUDA Kernels │ └───────────────┘ ``` ### Key Components #### 1. Backend Selection (`torch/_inductor/config.py`) Users select cuTile via configuration: ```python torch._inductor.config.cuda_backend = "cutile" ``` To ensure a seamless and stable integration: - **Opt-in only**: Triton remains the default backend for all CUDA code generation. When `cuda_backend = "triton"` (the default), cuTile code paths are never imported or invoked, ensuring zero overhead for existing users. - **Automatic Fallback**: If the cuTile backend encounters an unsupported operation, it automatically falls back to the ATen path. This ensures that models always execute correctly even as native operator coverage in cuTile expands. **Usage Example:** ```python import torch torch._inductor.config.cuda_backend = "cutile" @torch.compile def my_fn(x, y): return torch.softmax(x + y, dim=-1) x = torch.randn(1024, 2048, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16) y = torch.randn(1024, 2048, device='cuda', dtype=torch.bfloat16) result = my_fn(x, y) ``` #### 2. Code Generation & Class Refactoring We introduce a **TileKernel abstraction layer** to generalize tile-based codegen, allowing logic sharing between Triton and cuTile. - **TileKernel / TileKernelScheduling (NEW)**: Base classes for all tile-aware backends - **TritonKernel / TritonScheduling (REFACTORED)**: Now inherit from the Tile base classes - **CutileKernel / CutileScheduling (NEW)**: Implementation of ct.* API generation - **TileTemplateMixin (NEW)**: Shared template logic used by both `TritonTemplateKernel` and `CutileTemplateKernel` **Hierarchy Diagrams:** ``` Kernel Classes: SIMDKernel └── TileKernel (NEW) ├── TritonKernel (REFACTORED) └── CutileKernel (NEW) Template Kernels: TileTemplateMixin (NEW) ├── TritonTemplateKernel = TileTemplateMixin + TritonKernel (REFACTORED) └── CutileTemplateKernel = TileTemplateMixin + CutileKernel (NEW) Scheduling: SIMDScheduling └── TileKernelScheduling (NEW) ├── TritonScheduling (REFACTORED) └── CutileScheduling (NEW) ``` #### 3. Runtime Helpers (`torch/_inductor/runtime/`) Following the same pattern as Triton's runtime helpers: **`cutile_heuristics.py`**: Autotuning heuristics for tile sizes, thread block configurations, and performance tuning **`cutile_helpers.py`**: Runtime helper utilities for cuTile, analogous to `triton_helpers` for Triton. Includes common operations used by generated kernels (e.g., Welford online algorithms, nan-propagating min/max reductions) as well as pointer-based memory operations (`load_offset`, `store_offset`, `atomic_add_offset`). The pointer operations bridge the gap between Inductor's offset-based IR and cuTile's array-based model -- cuTile's native load/store APIs operate on arrays, which are not a natural target for Inductor's codegen. These pointer operations are implemented using internal cuTile APIs that are not part of the public stable interface and may break across cuda-tile releases. In the short term, we mitigate this through version pinning. Longer term, we plan to work with the cuTile team to either stabilize these APIs or introduce public pointer-based alternatives that align with Inductor's IR model. ### Scope & Fallback Behavior The cuTile backend supports the following operation categories: - **Pointwise**: arithmetic, logical, bitwise, comparisons, type conversions, etc. - **Reductions**: sum, prod, mean, max, min, softmax, etc. - **Matrix multiplication**: mm with autotuning - **Scatter**: scatter_add, scatter_reduce, index_put - **Memory**: transpose, permute, expand, clone, cat, etc. Operations not yet supported fall back automatically to ATen eager mode. Current fallbacks include: - **Extern-dependent ops**: functions requiring libdevice or external math libraries (e.g., acos, erf, lgamma) - **Ops lacking native support**: randn, sort, scan Operator coverage will expand incrementally across Phase 1 PRs. ### System Requirements - **GPU**: Blackwell (SM 10.0+) currently; Future versions will add support for more architectures. - **Python Package**: `cuda-tile` (via `pip install cuda-tile`) ## Roadmap & Upstreaming We have developed an internal implementation and propose contributing it to PyTorch in phases. **Phase 1: Initial Integration** - Scope: Infrastructure (backend, config, helpers, autotuning, scheduling), Operations (pointwise, reductions, memory), Templates support, Tests & Documentation - Phase 1 will be delivered as a series of PRs to keep reviews manageable, starting with infrastructure and copy kernels, then expanding to pointwise, reductions, templates, and GEMM - Deliverables: Core operation support, fallback mechanism, Blackwell compatibility - Success criteria: All tests pass on Blackwell, no Triton regressions - Testing and CI Validation: We will leverage the existing Inductor test suite to validate the cuTile backend for functional parity with the ATen and Triton paths. Although cuda-tile is an optional dependency, we propose installing it in the PyTorch CI pipelines to ensure continuous verification of the codegen logic and to prevent regressions. While Blackwell hardware availability in CI is currently limited, we will perform internal validation on Blackwell nodes to ensure execution correctness. **Phase 2 & Beyond:** - **Short-term (1-2 releases)**: Expand operators (scan/sort, complex trig, random), fusion with templates, tuned heuristics - **Medium-term**: Flash Attention, support for more GPUs - **Long-term**: Post-Blackwell architectures, multi-backend autotuning ### Code Organization **New files:** ``` torch/_inductor/codegen/cutile.py torch/_inductor/codegen/cutile_utils.py torch/_inductor/runtime/cutile_helpers.py torch/_inductor/runtime/cutile_heuristics.py torch/_inductor/codegen/cutile_combined_scheduling.py test/inductor/test_cutile_backend.py ``` ## Risks and Mitigations | Risk | Mitigation | |------|-----------| | **cuTile API changes** | Version pinning; internal API usage isolated to a single module (`cutile_helpers.py`); working with cuTile team on stable alternatives | | **Performance regressions** | Keep Triton default | | **Incomplete operator coverage** | Automatic ATen fallback; incremental expansion | | **Maintenance burden** | Shared infrastructure with Triton; comprehensive tests | ## References - [Tile IR Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/tile-ir/latest/index.html) - [cuTile Documentation](https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cutile-python/#) - [Helion DSL](https://github.com/pytorch/helion) ### Alternatives _No response_ ### Additional context _No response_ cc @ptrblck @msaroufim @eqy @jerryzh168 @tinglvv @nWEIdia @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @oulgen @engineer_14 @yf225 @Sibylau @choijon5

title: Inductor CUTLASS backend uses `CollectiveEpilogue` for SM90 on SM100
labels: triaged, module: codegen, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: While `cuda_arch` is checked for EVT compatibility, `CollectiveEpilogue` is used in spite of the value of it. ref: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ca15d63e9368e4c5e2a617f83f3f63b673ffd419/torch/_inductor/codegen/cutlass/lib_extensions/evt_extensions.py#L116-L143 This mismatch leads to ~60 failures in `test/inductor/test_cutlass_backend.py`'s evt tests on Blackwell. cc @engineer_17 @bhosmer @bdhirsh @kadeng @engineer_15_gh @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Proposal: Native C++ Data Pipeline Engine for Python-independent Deployment (tf.data parity)
labels: feature, oncall: pt2, oncall: export, bot-triaged
body: One of the remaining structural gaps between PyTorch 2.x and TensorFlow is the lack of a fully serializable, C++-native data pipeline engine. Currently, even with the advancements in torch.compile and torch.export, the data loading logic (DataLoader) remains fundamentally tied to the Python runtime. When deploying models in high-performance C++ environments or restricted edge devices (ExecuTorch), developers are forced to manually reimplement preprocessing and augmentation logic in C++. I propose the development of a declarative data API that can be captured as part of the graph. This would allow a model to be exported not just as a computational kernel, but as a complete system including its data ingestion logic—effectively enabling a 'Python-free' end-to-end inference pipeline similar to what tf.data achieves within a SavedModel. While TorchData attempted to address modularity, it didn't solve the core issue of graph-level serialization for the entire ingestion-to-inference flow. This feature is critical for enterprise-grade, low-latency deployments where Python overhead is unacceptable. cc @chauhang @penguinwu @avikchaudhuri @engineer_08 @tugsbayasgalan @engineer_05 @suo @ydwu4

title: [dynamo] Audit `x is y` code path
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug While working on https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/175266, Dynamo was failing `test_getset_descriptor_objclass_identity` added test. The fix was ``` elif name in ("__objclass__", "__name__"): source = self.source and AttrSource(self.source, name) return VariableTracker.build(tx, getattr(self.desc, name), source) ``` but even if this fix was not there, I was not expecting it fail. After debugging, I found the issue is in `handle_is` call, where earlier we were doing After replacing it with the test started passing without even the special handling of `__objclass__`. This exposes a concern that `is` is not working properly in Dynamo. This issue is to audit and perhaps ask LLMs to write many complex `is` tests to expose issues. ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions NA cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: [dynamo] Refactor variables/builtin.py
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamo
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The mentioned file is full of spaghetti code. breakpoint is hard to reason with, code readability is bad. Suggested refactor 1) Take apart a few important builtins - starting list is - `getattr`, `iter`, `list`, `dict` - and make corresponding variable trackers - `GetAttrBuiltinVariable` etc and move the code from the `BuiltinVariable` to these new variable trackers 2) Add a check in BuiltinVariable tracker in the constructor to ensure that we use the newly created VT classes for the chosen builtins, so that we can just delete the code from BulitinVariableTracker. 3) Wherever possible, use method dispatch instead of `if` else calls to have a reasonable compile time. One of the reasons that file is hard to read is because of cache handler stuff - as you implement the changes, reason about compile time. ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions NA cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @kadeng @amjames @Lucaskabela @jataylo

title: Triton OutofMemoryError when fusing multiple ops
labels: high priority, triaged, module: regression, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I'm using Flexattention w/ torch.compile and running into the error below when calling the backward pass. This seems to be an issue with torch.compile creating too large of a fused op which the gpu then doesn't have sufficient shared memory for. ``` torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: RuntimeError: No valid triton configs. OutOfMemoryError: out of resource: triton_per_fused_add_div_expand_mul_pow_slice_split_sum_view_7 Required: 327768 Hardware limit:232448 Reducing block sizes or `num_stages` may help. ``` Repro of issue (disclaimer this was written by claude): https://gist.github.com/fynnsu/11a605c0f3ae9c08ffcfb3286b1741cb Notes: - In the error above the "Hardware limit:232448" is the amount of shared memory on each SM of the gpu I'm using (H100 80GB). - **This is new on `torch>=2.10`. The repro passes when using `torch==2.9`** - Adding a graph break in the fused region (see repro) forces compile to not create such a large op, which "fixes" the issues. Is there a `torch._dynamo.graph_break()` equivalent for preventing fusions in specific locations without actually graph breaking? Potentially related: #174066. ### Versions <details> <summary>Collect env output</summary> ``` PyTorch version: 2.10.0+cu128 Is debug build: False CUDA used to build PyTorch: 12.8 ROCM used to build
