title: Block mask caching for flex attention and SAC don't play nicely together (RuntimeError: Only Tensors of floating point and complex dtype can require gradients)
labels: high priority, module: crash, module: activation checkpointing, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dtensor, module: pt2-dispatcher, module: flex attention, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: Repro: ``` """Minimal self-contained repro for DTensor int tensor + SAC + compile bug. No sixlib dependencies. Triggers: RuntimeError: Only Tensors of floating point and complex dtype can require gradients in DTensor.__tensor_unflatten__ during SAC recomputation of a compiled region. Root cause: A compiled create_block_mask produces a BlockMask containing int tensors. When this BlockMask is cached and reused during SAC recomputation inside a compiled function that operates on DTensors, aot_autograd's wrap_tensor_subclasses incorrectly tries to reconstruct a DTensor from the int tensor with requires_grad=True. Essential ingredients (removing any one makes the bug disappear): 1. wrap_inductor_compiled_regions = True 2. Compiled create_block_mask (BlockMask cached across forward/recompute) 3. Compiled inner function wrapping DTensor -> to_local -> compiled flex_attention -> DTensor 4. SAC (selective activation checkpointing) around the outer function 5. SAC policy that saves both flex_attention and inductor_compiled_code outputs """ import os os.environ["RANK"] = "0" os.environ["WORLD_SIZE"] = "1" os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"] = "0" os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost" os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "29500" import torch import torch.distributed as dist import torch.utils.checkpoint from functools import partial from torch.distributed.device_mesh import init_device_mesh from torch.distributed.tensor import DTensor, Replicate from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import create_block_mask, flex_attention from torch.utils.checkpoint import CheckpointPolicy torch._inductor.config.wrap_inductor_compiled_regions = True dist.init_process_group(backend="fake", init_method="env://", world_size=1, rank=0) device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu" mesh = init_device_mesh(device, mesh_shape=(1,), mesh_dim_names=("fsdp",)) # SAC policy: save flex_attention and inductor_compiled_code outputs _SAVE = {torch.ops.higher_order.flex_attention.name(): None, "inductor_compiled_code": None} def ac_policy(ctx, op, *args, **kwargs): if (isinstance(op, torch._ops.HigherOrderOperator) and op.name() in _SAVE) or op in _SAVE: return CheckpointPolicy.MUST_SAVE return CheckpointPolicy.PREFER_RECOMPUTE L = 64 _compiled_create_block_mask = torch.compile(create_block_mask, dynamic=False, fullgraph=True) _compiled_flex_attention = torch.compile(flex_attention, mode="default", fullgraph=True, dynamic=False) def causal(b, h, q, k): return q >= k _mask_cache = {} def inner_fn(q, mask): """Compiled function: DTensor -> to_local -> flex_attention -> DTensor.""" ql = q.to_local() out = _compiled_flex_attention(query=ql, key=ql, value=ql, block_mask=mask) return DTensor.from_local(out, device_mesh=q.device_mesh, placements=q.placements) inner_fn = torch.compile(inner_fn, mode="default", fullgraph=True, dynamic=False) def outer_fn(x): """Builds/caches BlockMask, calls compiled inner.""" if "m" not in _mask_cache: _mask_cache["m"] = _compiled_create_block_mask( mask_mod=causal, B=None, H=None, Q_LEN=L, KV_LEN=L, BLOCK_SIZE=128, device=x.device, ) return inner_fn(x, _mask_cache["m"]) context_fn = partial(torch.utils.checkpoint.create_selective_checkpoint_contexts, ac_policy) # Input shape: [batch, num_heads, seq_len, head_dim] x = DTensor.from_local( torch.randn(1, 1, L, L, device=device, dtype=torch.bfloat16, requires_grad=True), mesh, (Replicate(),), ) try: out = torch.utils.checkpoint.checkpoint( outer_fn, x, use_reentrant=False, context_fn=context_fn, ) out.sum().backward() print("PASS - no error") except RuntimeError as e: import traceback traceback.print_exc() if "Only Tensors of floating point and complex dtype" in str(e): print("\\nFAIL - DTensor constructor error reproduced!") else: print(f"\\nDifferent error: {e}") finally: dist.destroy_process_group() ``` This fails with: ``` File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 1024, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/fbsource/genai/msl/sixlib/repro_dtensor_int_ckpt8_v2.py", line 65, in inner_fn def inner_fn(q, mask): File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 1263, in _fn return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py", line 1200, in forward return compiled_fn(full_args) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 561, in runtime_wrapper return _runtime_wrapper_impl(args) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 594, in _runtime_wrapper_impl all_outs = call_func_at_runtime_with_args( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/utils.py", line 138, in call_func_at_runtime_with_args out = normalize_as_list(f(args)) ^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/utils.py", line 105, in g return f(*args) ^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/autograd/function.py", line 596, in apply return super().apply(*args, **kwargs) # type: ignore[misc] ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 2535, in forward fw_outs = call_func_at_runtime_with_args( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/utils.py", line 138, in call_func_at_runtime_with_args out = normalize_as_list(f(args)) ^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 811, in wrapper return compiled_fn(runtime_args) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 1001, in inner_fn wrapped_outs = wrap_tensor_subclasses( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/subclass_utils.py", line 427, in wrap_tensor_subclasses subclass_meta.creation_fn(unwrapped_args, is_runtime=is_runtime) File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/schemas.py", line 329, in creation_fn rebuilt = original_subclass_type.__tensor_unflatten__( # type: ignore[attr-defined] ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/data/users/engineer_17/c/pytorch/torch/distributed/tensor/_api.py", line 362, in __tensor_unflatten__ return DTensor( ^^^^^^^^ RuntimeError: Only Tensors of floating point and complex dtype can require gradients (Occurred in compiled region with compile_id: 1/0, forward) (Occurred in compiled region with compile_id: 1/0, backward) ``` It seems very specifically related to block mask caching changing the forward on subsequent run, causing SAC to choke. cc @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @soulitzer @chauhang @penguinwu @wanchaol @tianyu-l @wz337 @XilunWu @d4l3k @pragupta @SherlockNoMad @ppwwyyxx @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03 @Chillee @engineer_22 @yanboliang @BoyuanFeng @liangel-02 @howardzhang-cv

title: Idiomatic way of matching against flex attention regions in SAC policy with wrap_inductor_compiled_regions
labels: feature, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: flex attention, module: compile ux, bot-triaged
body: It used to be that you couldn't use SAC over a torch.compile region. This was especially annoying for usage of flex attention. Now, it kind of works with: ``` torch._inductor.config.wrap_inductor_compiled_regions = True ``` However, when you write your policy function, something annoying happens. If you wrote your policy function to be matching on op names (pretty common), you end up having to match on "inductor_compiled_code" instead of something sensible like "flex_attention". This is bad. I'm not sure what I want the alternative to be. I think for flex attention, I would like the HOP to "live on" in the resulting compiled region. But this would be weirdly special-casey, because normally the flex attention HOP is /inside/ the compiled region and is completely vaporized by the act of compilation. It feels almost as if the compile region should be /inside/ the HOP somehow. Not sure if you can actually do that. There's also some more grungy ways of fixing it, like forcing people to stick their grubby hands into the HOP and read out the info they need, e.g., some extra metadata field or literally looking at the ops on the uncompiled graph to figure something out. cc @voznesenskym @penguinwu @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @chauhang @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @Chillee @engineer_22 @yanboliang @BoyuanFeng @soulitzer

title: torch.compile / scaled_mm: possible minor regression between 2.8 and 2.9.1
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug consider this code: ``` import torch m, k, n = 2058, 3072, 3072 device = 'cuda' x_8 = torch.ones (m,k, device=device, dtype=torch.float8_e4m3fn) w_8 = torch.ones (n,k, device=device, dtype=torch.float8_e4m3fn) one_1d = torch.ones(1, device=device) # [1] one_0d = torch.tensor(1.0, device=device) # [] def f(): return torch._scaled_mm(x_8, w_8.T, scale_a=one_1d, scale_b=one_0d, out_dtype=torch.float) f() compiled_f = torch.compile(f, fullgraph=True) compiled_f() ``` and note the shape difference between one_1d and one_0d. This code: - executes successfully on an RTX 4070 on all torch versions, compiled and non-compiled - executes successfully on a RTX 5090 on torch 2.8.0 compiled and non-compiled, and on all torch versions non-compiled - but fails on a RTX 5090 on torch 2.9.1 and torch 2.10 compiled - it triggers this assertion https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/e6e402dc069cb7ceb62917aef3571ba9bb2b65e1/torch/_inductor/template_heuristics/triton.py#L2199 feel free to close this, if it is just an unintended use of an unstable API (torch._scaled_mm). But it might also still exist in the now-stable API of scaled_mm. ### Error logs ``` assert len(scale_a.get_size()) == len(scale_b.get_size()) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: LoweringException: AssertionError: target: aten._scaled_mm.default args[0]: TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='arg1_1', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.float8_e4m3fn, size=[2058, 3072], stride=[3072, 1])) )) args[1]: TensorBox( ReinterpretView( StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='arg0_1', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.float8_e4m3fn, size=[3072, 3072], stride=[3072, 1])) ), FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.float8_e4m3fn, size=[3072, 3072], stride=[1, 3072]), origins=OrderedSet([permute]), stack_traces = {, File "/OneTrainer/modules/module/quantized/LinearW8A8.py", line 200, in f, return torch._scaled_mm(x_8, w_8.T, scale_a=one_1d, scale_b=one_0d, out_dtype=torch.float), , } ) ) args[2]: TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='arg2_1', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.float32, size=[1], stride=[1])) )) args[3]: TensorBox(StorageBox( InputBuffer(name='arg3_1', layout=FixedLayout('cuda:0', torch.float32, size=[], stride=[])) )) args[4]: None args[5]: None args[6]: torch.float32 kwargs: {'use_fast_accum': False} ``` ### Versions see above cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [inductor] Multiple randint calls cause inconsistent RNG results between eager and compiled mode after fixing the rng seed
labels: high priority, triaged, module: random, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug To reproduce: ```python import torch import torch._dynamo torch._inductor.config.fallback_random = True def fn(): torch.manual_seed(0) out = torch.randint(0, 100, (4,1), dtype=torch.int64) # Shape and integer range are not important. other = torch.randint(0, 100, (2,1), dtype=torch.int64) # No inconsistency if remove this line, although the value won't be returned. return out cfunc = torch.compile(fn, backend='inductor') torch.manual_seed(0) out1 = fn() torch.manual_seed(0) out2 = cfunc() print(f'{out1=}') ''' out1=tensor([[44], [39], [33], [60]]) ''' print(f'{out2=}') ''' out2=tensor([[33], [60], [63], [79]]) ''' torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` Problem: RNG state is inconsistent between eager execution and torch.compile(backend='inductor') execution, even after explicitly resetting the seed with torch.manual_seed(0) before each run. The issue occurs when two randint calls are called inside the compiled function:the output of the first randint already differs between eager and compiled mode, even though the second randint result is not returned. After a few debugging, I notice that the following version does NOT trigger the inconsistency: ```python def fn(): torch.manual_seed(0) out = torch.randint(0, 100, (4,1), dtype=torch.int64) torch.manual_seed(0) # Extra seed reset before second randint other = torch.randint(0, 100, (2,1), dtype=torch.int64) return out ``` The only difference is an extra torch.manual_seed(0) before the second randint. This behavior is bit counterintuitive: We already set the seed before the first `randint`, and the second `randint` does not modify the returned value. However, without setting the second `randint`'s rng seed still causes the first randint output to mismatch between eager and compiled mode. Note that this inconsistency only occurs for `inductor` backend, but does not occur for `eager/aot_eager`backend. ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @pbelevich @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: Backward under compile + autocast: misleading documentation/defaults
labels: module: docs, triaged, module: amp (automated mixed precision), oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, bot-triaged
body: ### 📚 The doc issue Hi, [Default `torch.compile` + backward behavior under autocast is documented as:](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/user_guide/torch_compiler/torch.compiler_backward.html#autocast-behavior): > "same_as_forward" (default). We assume that the backward of the torch.compile’ed region will be run under the same autocast context manager that the region was run under (if any). However, [the `torch.amp` docs themselves explicitly call out](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/amp.html): > [autocast](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/amp.html#torch.autocast) should wrap only the forward pass(es) of your network, including the loss computation(s). Backward passes under autocast are not recommended. Backward ops run in the same type that autocast used for corresponding forward ops. This is extremely misleading. If the recommended practice is *not* to perform backward under autocast, why is the default assumption that backward is performed under autocast if autocast is enabled in the forward? ### Suggest a potential alternative/fix Short of changing the default (which would be a breaking change, so obviously not a light thing to do), call out the discrepancy loudly around both docs. This deserves a serious warning IMO since it can have deep numerical correctness implications, and affects all backward-pass computations.

title: [inductor/aot_eager] interpolate under torch.compile produces incorrect result when mode is `nearest`
labels: high priority, triage review, triaged, module: dispatch, oncall: pt2, module: decompositions, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Here is the code to reproduce: ```python import torch def fn(x): x = torch.nn.functional.interpolate(x, scale_factor=1.3, mode='nearest') return x x1 = torch.tensor([[[[1.0, 2.0]]]], dtype=torch.float64) x2 = torch.tensor([[[[1.0, 2.0]]]], dtype=torch.float64) cfunc = torch.compile(fn, backend='aot_eager') out1 = fn(x1) print(out1) ''' tensor([[[[1., 2.]]]], dtype=torch.float64) ''' out2 = cfunc(x2) print(out2) ''' tensor([[[[1., 1.]]]], dtype=torch.float64) ''' torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` The expected result is `[[[[1,2]]]]`, but the actual result is `[[[[1,1]]]]`, I guess it is caused by some rounding issues inside torch.compile? This issue only happens when setting backend to 'aot_eager' or 'inductor', but does not happen when setting backend to 'eager' ### Error logs Mismatched elements: 1 / 2 (50.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 1.0 at index (0, 0, 0, 1) (up to 1e-07 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 1.0 at index (0, 0, 0, 1) (up to 1e-07 allowed) ### Versions [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @gchanan @kadeng @msaroufim @chauhang @penguinwu

title: torch.compile VRAM usage regression between 2.9.1 and 2.10.0
labels: high priority, needs reproduction, module: memory usage, triaged, oncall: pt2
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug There is a significant regression in torch 2.10 vram usage between torch 2.9.1 and torch 2.10.0, that depends on whether torch.compile is used: Torch 2.11 non-compiled 11.4 Torch 2.11 compiled **14.6** Torch 2.10 non-compiled 11.3 Torch 2.10 compiled **14.5** Torch 2.9.1 non-compiled 11.3 Torch 2.9.1 compiled 11 Torch 2.8 non-compiled 11.3 Torch 2.8 compiled 11 in GB, only the process according to `nvitop` (not total OS usage of vram) These tests were done using https://github.com/nerogar/OneTrainer, which under the hood is a `diffusers` model with regional compilation of the transformer blocks (if enabled). Using the Flux2 Klein 9b preset, but batch size 1 instead of 2, otherwise it OOMs using torch 2.10. If you have a suspicion what could be the root cause, I can run further tests or try to provide isolated reproduction code, but currently I do not know where to even start to isolate the root cause. ### Error logs _No response_ ### Versions These versions are with 2.9.1. title: torch.compile / Inductor generates invalid C++ (undeclared zuf0/zuf1) during compile (CppCompileError) on RTX 3090
labels: needs reproduction, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: dynamic shapes, module: inductor
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile (triggered here via Keras 3 torch backend with jit_compile=True), TorchInductor generates invalid C++ and compilation fails with CppCompileError. Baseline (jit_compile=False) runs successfully. With jit_compile=True, the same model.fit() crashes inside torch._inductor with a g++ error: ‘zuf0’ / ‘zuf1’ was not declared in this scope. This looks like an Inductor codegen bug (invalid C++ emitted). ### Error logs Repro outcome: ``` - jit_compile=False: OK - jit_compile=True: FAIL (InductorError -> CppCompileError) ``` Key error: `torch._inductor.exc.InductorError: CppCompileError: C++ compile error ` Command: `g++ /tmp/torchinductor_<user>/.../cgg3yxgs....cpp ... -o ...so ` Output: ``` /tmp/torchinductor_<user>/.../cgg3yxgs....cpp: In function ‘void kernel(double*, double*)’: ...cpp:9:29: error: ‘zuf0’ was not declared in this scope ...cpp:18:29: error: ‘zuf1’ was not declared in this scope ``` ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: Mega-Cache generate mismatched guard states when loading the compilation output
labels: triaged, module: amp (automated mixed precision), oncall: pt2, module: dynamo, module: guards, compile-cache, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug It appears that the serialization of the guard state is performed within the amp context. However, when reloading the product, during the construction of GLOBAL_STATE guard, the program has not yet entered the amp context. ``` import os os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR"] = "./cache_file" os.environ["TORCH_CACHING_PRECOMPILE"] = "1" import torch import logging logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO) torch._logging.set_logs(dynamo=logging.INFO, guards=True) import argparse parser = argparse.ArgumentParser() parser.add_argument( "--stage", type=str, choices=["save", "load"], default="save", ) args = parser.parse_args() def fn(x): return x + 1 * x inputs = ( torch.randn( 3, 2, device="cuda", ), ) fn = torch.compile(fn) def module_forward(): with torch.amp.autocast(device_type="cuda"): fn(*inputs) cache_dir = os.environ["TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR"] if __name__ == "__main__": if args.stage == "save": logging.info("Start to save cache artifacts") module_forward() artifact_bytes, cache_info = torch.compiler.save_cache_artifacts() torch.save(artifact_bytes, os.path.join(cache_dir, "cache.pt")) else: # Loading torch._dynamo.reset() logging.info("Start to load cache artifacts") artifact_bytes = torch.load(os.path.join(cache_dir, "cache.pt")) torch.compiler.load_cache_artifacts(artifact_bytes) with torch.compiler.set_stance("fail_on_recompile"): module_forward() ``` ``` RuntimeError: Detected recompile when torch.compile stance is 'fail_on_recompile'. filename: '/test.py', function name: 'fn', line number: 21 Failed on the following precompiled guards: TREE_GUARD_MANAGER: +- RootGuardManager | +- LAMBDA_GUARD: torch._functorch.aot_autograd.utils.top_saved_tensors_hooks ids == None # <unknown> | +- GLOBAL_STATE: ___check_global_state() against {"allow_bf16_reduce":0,"allow_fp16_reduce":0,"allow_tf32":false,"autocast_state":{"cached_enabled":true,"dtype":[15,5,5,15,5,5,15,15,5,5],"enabled":[false,true,false,false,false,false,false,false,false,false]},"default_dtype":6,"deterministic_algorithms":false,"deterministic_algorithms_warn_only":false,"grad_mode":true,"num_threads":96,"torch_function":true,"torch_function_all_disabled":false} | +- TORCH_FUNCTION_MODE_STACK: ___check_torch_function_mode_stack() | +- DEFAULT_DEVICE: utils_device.CURRENT_DEVICE == None # <unknown> | +- GuardManager: source=L['x'], accessed_by=FrameLocalsGuardAccessor(key='x', framelocals_idx=0), type=<class 'torch._subclasses.fake_tensor.FakeTensor'>, tag_safe=(True, False) | | +- TENSOR_MATCH: check_tensor(L['x'], Tensor, DispatchKeySet(CUDA, BackendSelect, ADInplaceOrView, AutogradCUDA), torch.float32, device=0, requires_grad=False, size=[3, 2], stride=[2, 1]) # return x + 1 * x # test.py:23 in fn | | +- NO_HASATTR: hasattr(L['x'], '_dynamo_dynamic_indices') == False # return x + 1 * x # test.py:23 in fn GuardDebugInfo( result=0, verbose_code_parts=["tensor 'L['x']' dispatch key set mismatch. expected DispatchKeySet(CUDA, BackendSelect, ADInplaceOrView, AutogradCUDA), actual DispatchKeySet(CUDA, BackendSelect, ADInplaceOrView, AutogradCUDA, AutocastCUDA)"], num_guards_executed=5, user_stack=None) ``` ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: torch.compile outputs incorrect results when applying `add_` on transposed tensor
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, topic: fuzzer, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile with the inductor backend to compile a model with `add_(xx).reshape(xx)` on a transposed tensor, the compile model outputs incorrect outputs compared to eager execution. Here is the code to reproduce: ```python import torch torch._inductor.config.fallback_random = True def fn(x, y): return x.add_(y).reshape(-1, 1, 3) torch.manual_seed(0) x1 = torch.ones([1, 2, 3]).transpose(1, 2) y1 = torch.randn([1, 3, 1]) print(x1, y1) ''' tensor([[[1., 1.], [1., 1.], [1., 1.]]]), tensor([[[ 1.5410], [-0.2934], [-2.1788]]]) ''' x2 = x1.clone() y2 = y1.clone() cfunc = torch.compile(fn, backend='inductor') out1 = fn(x1, y1) print(out1) ''' tensor([[[ 2.5410, 2.5410, 0.7066]], [[ 0.7066, -1.1788, -1.1788]]]) ''' out2 = cfunc(x2, y2) print(out2) ''' tensor([[[ 4.0820, 4.0820, 0.4131]], [[ 0.4131, -3.3576, -3.3576]]]) ''' torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` Notes: 1. Issue does not occur when setting backend to `eager` or `aot_eager`. 2. If we change `x1 = torch.ones([1, 2, 3]).transpose(1, 2)` to `x1=torch.ones(1, 3, 2])`. This issue does not occur. ### Error logs AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 6 / 6 (100.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 2.1787893772125244 at index (1, 0, 1) (up to 1e-05 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 0.7102370262145996 at index (0, 0, 2) (up to 1.3e-06 allowed) ### Versions [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [inductor] CPU test failures in test_cpu_select_algorithm.py due to insufficient tolerances for low-precision operations
labels: module: cpu, module: tests, triaged, module: bfloat16, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Three tests in test/inductor/test_cpu_select_algorithm.py are failing due to insufficient tolerance values when comparing low-precision (bfloat16/int4 quantized) operations against higher-precision reference implementations. The tests fail with numerical differences that are expected and acceptable for these reduced-precision data types, but the tolerance thresholds are set too strictly. **Failing Tests** test_bmm_amp_bs_1_Mdim_192_Kdim_196_Ndim_84_cpu_float32 test_int4_woq_mm_amx_batch_size_1_in_features_1024_out_features_1024_group_size_128_cpu_bfloat16 test_int4_concat_woq_mm_batch_size_4_in_features_256_out_features0_group_size_128_cpu_bfloat16 **Test 1: test_bmm_amp** AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 1 / 16128 (0.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 0.0625 at index (0, 12, 4) (up to 0.0001 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 0.00421142578125 at index (0, 12, 4) (up to 0.0001 allowed) **Test 2: test_int4_woq_mm_amx** AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 476 / 8192 (5.8%) Greatest absolute difference: 1.0 at index (0, 2, 851) (up to 0.01 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 5.40625 at index (0, 4, 219) (up to 0.01 allowed) **Test 3: test_int4_concat_woq_mm** AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 1754 / 16384 (10.7%) Greatest absolute difference: 0.75 at index (1, 7, 132) (up to 0.01 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 43.5 at index (2, 6, 87) (up to 0.01 allowed) **Steps to Reproduce** # Run the failing tests python test/inductor/test_cpu_select_algorithm.py TestSelectAlgorithmCPU.test_bmm_amp_bs_1_Mdim_192_Kdim_196_Ndim_84_cpu_float32 -v python test/inductor/test_cpu_select_algorithm.py TestSelectAlgorithmCPU.test_int4_woq_mm_amx_batch_size_1_in_features_1024_out_features_1024_group_size_128_cpu_bfloat16 -v python test/inductor/test_cpu_select_algorithm.py TestSelectAlgorithmCPU.test_int4_concat_woq_mm_batch_size_4_in_features_256_out_features0_group_size_128_cpu_bfloat16 -v ### Versions Collecting environment information... title: torch.cond() and cuda graph mega issue
labels: triaged, module: cuda graphs, oncall: pt2, module: higher order operators, module: pt2-dispatcher, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: While working on https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168912, I found that there are several issues remaining with torch.cond(). I would like to merge https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168912 and then follow up on these, to prevent that PR from becoming too large and possibly reverted for touching too many parts of the code base. Here they are: - [ ] Make sure it composes with Frank Lin's graph_capture_record_stream_reuse feature https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168912#discussion_r2789547713 - [ ] Make sure it works with https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/172836 - Right now, I unconditionally warn if the user uses any RNG inside a cudagraph and also uses torch.cond() in that cudagraph: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/168912/changes#diff-d7302d133bb5e0890fc94de9aeea4d9d442555a3b40772c9db10edb5cf36a35cR213 At the very least, we can make the warning more precise (we need to do it only when RNG happens inside the conditional node). But in the future, actually update the RNG seed appropriately. I'm sure we can do it. FYI @eee4017 - [ ] Add support for inductor for this. This is actually pretty easy. I'm just not familiar with inductor. - [ ] Use IF-ELSE node for torch.cond (as opposed to using two IF nodes) if CUDA version is >= 12.8. - FYI @BoyuanFeng this will reduce some of the overhead you are seeing in torch.cond(). We will have two fewer kernels. - [ ] Make sure that we can do forward pass, backward pass, gradient NaN check, and optimizer in a single cuda graph once https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/172836 merges. cc @mcarilli @engineer_17 @engineer_02 @penguinwu @BoyuanFeng @chauhang @ydwu4 @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: torch.compile yields incorrect results when as_strided is used on channels_last tensor
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, topic: fuzzer
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug When using torch.compile with the inductor backend on a model that includes torch.as_strided, the compiled output differs from the eager mode output if the input tensor is in channels_last memory format. The discrepancy does not occur when the input uses the default contiguous memory layout. Here is the code to reproduce: ```python import torch def func(x): x = torch.roll(x, shifts=-1, dims=0) return torch.as_strided(x, (5, 10), (4, 1), 0) x1 = torch.arange(100).reshape(2, 5, 2, 5).to(memory_format=torch.channels_last) # setting the memory_format to channels_last essential to reproduce the bug. x2 = torch.arange(100).reshape(2, 5, 2, 5).to(memory_format=torch.channels_last) cfunc = torch.compile(func, backend='inductor') out1 = func(x1) out2 = cfunc(x2) print(f'{out1=}') print(f'{out2=}') torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` Note: backend `aot_eager` and `eager` does not has this issue ### Error logs AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not equal! Mismatched elements: 49 / 50 (98.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 36 at index (0, 4) Greatest relative difference: 0.6666666865348816 at index (0, 4) ### Versions Versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: [RFC] Hooks to trigger recompilation
labels: feature, triaged, module: fx, oncall: pt2, module: aotdispatch, module: inductor, module: dynamo, module: pt2-dispatcher, bot-triaged
body: ### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch ## **Summary** This RFC proposes to define API to hook into cache systems, allowing downstream projects to trigger recompilation based on externally-defined conditions. ## **Motivation** Currently, TorchInductor allows various downstream projects/extensions to hook their logic into its existing infrastructure, e.g. register custom FX optimization passes for Inductor or register custom device backends. This is very helpful when implementing support or optimizations for specific devices. In the pursuit of providing maximum level of performance, the Inductor relies on its cache mechanism, reusing previously generated artifacts and not triggering re-compilation when it's not necessary. The framework relies on multiple levels of cache in the TorchInductor compilation path: there is FXGraphCache, AOTCache and DynamoCache, all of which playing its own role in deciding whether to issue a recompilation for the current launch of the model, or to re-use cached artifacts. This is very robust and mature system, however there is a limitation within the current implementation: the existing cache mechanism does not allow any external conditions to be included in the validity checks, which might affect correctness and/or performance of the artifacts generated by the downstream projects. In other words: custom backend might need to raise a re-compilation need in some externally defined circumstances, but currently there is no possibility to do that. ## **Solution proposal** For the purposes of further explanation, let's refer to the code belonging to a downstream project wanting to use this mechanism, as a *'user code'* (note that actual PyTorch's users might also use this mechanism for their purposes), in opposition to the current framework's code. The proposed solution is to introduce hooks in the existing caching systems and provide users with API, so that they could register their own set of predicates/conditions, evaluated/established based on some context generally available on the corresponding cache system level. The context provided to the users' callback must be reasonable broad, to allow them to construct valid and meaningful invariants. In order for this solution to work reliably, it must provide means for affecting all mentioned above cache levels - from DynamoCache, through AOTCache, to FXGraphCache. The AOTCache and FXGraphCache systems work very similar to each other (at least in the scope we care about in this RFC, so in terms of deciding about cache hit/miss), and we can reason about them together, while the DynamoCache is a different system and will need a different approach. ### A) DynamoCache #### Overview DynamoCache is driven by the TorchDynamo guards' mechanism. Affecting DynamoCache's hit/miss decision could be done by introducing a small, opt-in extension point in [torch/_dynamo/guards.py](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_dynamo/guards.py), that allows users to register a callback which produces an additional runtime lambda guard during guards' assembly. This callback will have access to the context generally available during the TorchDynamo guards' installation - an instance of [GuardBuilder](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_dynamo/guards.py#L1013), so when called, the user's code could provide necessary data to assemble a Dynamo guard just like it would if the code was included in the framework. #### Proposed API The data necessary for assembling such guard, might look like follow: ```python @dataclass class CustomGuardSpec: """ Struct describing data of a custom guard. """ # custom closure variables the guard function should capture closure_vars: Dict[str, Any] # a single Python expression string that returns True/False at guard time; # it can access `closure_vars` code: str # a human-readable, debug-oriented rendering of the guard logic. # If not provided, `code` will be used instead. verbose_code: Optional[str] # optional name for logs name: Optional[str] = None ``` Having that defined, we can draft the following callback registration API: ```python def register_custom_guard(custom_guard_producer_fn: Callable[[GuardBuilderBase], CustomGuardSpec]) -> RemovableHandle: # ... pass ``` This function will receive a user-provided callable, taking `GuardBuilder` object as an argument, and returning description of the guard to be installed. This function shall just store user's callable in some registry and return standard [`RemovableHandle`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/utils/hooks.py#L10), so that users can unregister their checks, when they need to. #### Usage example In order to demonstrate a usage pattern, let's write a test for the API we've drafted above. This is a test for artificial scenario, installing custom guard which asserts on the **value** of the input tensor. It's obviously a non-practical example, but it demonstrates the usage and makes sure we're testing this specific custom guard and no built-in guard is interfering. ```python # file: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/test/dynamo/test_guard_manager.py # ...standard import for test/dynamo/test_guard_manager.py... import torch._dynamo from torch._dynamo.testing import CompileCounter from torch._dynamo.source import LocalSource # 0) assume CustomGuardSpec and register_custom_guard are part of the torch._dynamo.guards: from torch._dynamo.guards import CustomGuardSpec, register_custom_guard class CustomGuardRegistrationTests(torch._dynamo.test_case.TestCase): def test_custom_guard_can_access_builder_and_closure_vars(self): """ Test whether custom guard works properly: can access `builder` and `closure_vars` during tracing and during subsequent runs. Test scenario is installing silly custom guard which will be guarding on value of the graph argument x[0][0] and will trigger recompilation if it changes. """ # 1) define some simple model to be compiled, note it has one input - `x` def fn(x): return x + 1 cnt = CompileCounter() compiled_fn = torch.compile(fn, backend=cnt, fullgraph=False) # 2) define user callback which will produce custom guard def custom_guard_producer(builder): # 2.1) capture the (0, 0) element value of the arg from the tracing run x_0_0 = builder.get(LocalSource("x"))[0][0] # 2.2) guard will be true for the tracing run and for any other subsequent run # when the value of L['x'] will match, and false when value changes return CustomGuardSpec( closure_vars={"x_0_0": x_0_0}, code="L['x'][0][0] == x_0_0", name="custom silly assert on x[0][0]", ) # 3) use the proposed API to register custom guard (and unregister in cleanup stage) handle = register_custom_guard(custom_guard_producer) self.addCleanup(handle.remove) # 4) define inputs with the same shape but different values for test x1 = torch.ones((4, 4)) x2 = torch.zeros((4, 4)) # 5) verify with counters if this guard works as expected # 5.1) 1st run compiles expected_compilations = 1 compiled_fn(x1) c1 = cnt.frame_count self.assertEqual(c1, expected_compilations) # 5.2) 2nd run with the same input shouldn't trigger compile expected_compilations += 0 compiled_fn(x1) c2 = cnt.frame_count self.assertEqual(c2, expected_compilations) # 5.3) run with different values should recompile expected_compilations += 1 compiled_fn(x2) c3 = cnt.frame_count self.assertEqual(c3, expected_compilations) # 5.4) subsequent run with the same values should not recompile expected_compilations += 0 compiled_fn(x2) c4 = cnt.frame_count self.assertEqual(c4, expected_compilations) ``` #### Hook implementation The only important thing missing is the actual invocation of the callbacks registered by users and guards' installation. This has to be done during the phase when the regular guards are installed, so that the user can access `GuardBuilder`, and we on the framework side can take the user-defined description to actually construct and install proper guard. [This place](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_dynamo/guards.py#L4355-L4356) in `CheckFunctionManager.compile_check_fn` looks like a sensible place to iterate through all the registered callbacks and process them. Assume we have some helper function `_get_registered_custom_guards()` that will retrieve from our registry an iterable of all the callbacks, then installing the user-defined guards could look like that: ```python class CheckFunctionManager: def compile_check_fn( # ... ) -> None: # ... # generate custom guards from user-registered specs: for custom_guard_producer in _get_registered_custom_guards(): try: # call user-defined code to produce the guard spec: custom_guard_spec = None custom_guard_spec = custom_guard_producer(builder) if not isinstance(custom_guard_spec, CustomGuardSpec): raise TypeError("Registered custom guard producer function should " "return `CustomGuardSpec`!") # append the custom guard's expression as a lambda guard verbose_code = custom_guard_spec.verbose_code if custom_guard_spec.verbose_code else custom_guard_spec.code builder.add_python_lambda_leaf_guard_to_root( [custom_guard_spec.code], [verbose_code], custom_guard_spec.closure_vars) # add this custom guard to logs add_code_part(custom_guard_spec.code, guard=None, log_only=True) except Exception as e: logging.getLogger(__name__).exception( "Failed to add registered custom guard '%s': %s", getattr(custom_guard_spec, "name", "--unknown custom guard--"), str(e)) # OK, all done generating guards # ... ``` These changes should be suficient to allow users' to influence the DynamoCache hit/miss decissions. #### Opens There are some questions to be answered yet, for example: A.1) what shall we do when the callback raises and error? Here I'm just logging it and functionally ignoring the error, but we might have some flag (in the config?) deciding what to do in such case. A.2) The other question is the type of guard we are installing. For the purpose of implementing a PoC backing this RFC, the lambda leaf guard seems reasonable and works well, but maybe there is some better option? ### B) AOTCache, FXGraphCache #### Overview Both AOTCache and FXGraphCache are using a simple hash key to decide about cache hit/miss, moreover both systems are taking - among other data - a [`_CompileFxKwargs`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_inductor/compile_fx.py#L764) instance to be pickled and hashed to produce proper cache key. For this reason we could treat them both the same way. In order to influence those cache systems, we can just provide the ability to include additional, user-defined data in the already pickled data producing compilation cache key used by AOTAutograd and TorchInductor. This data shouldn't be some static global config file, but rather provided in similar fashion as the solution for the DynamoCache, as a user-defined callbacks producing extra data based on some context available before performing the actual compilation, so it can vary per compilation without affecting a global context. For the purposes of this RFC, let's just allow one user callback that will produce the extra data for both cache systems at once. #### Proposed API The exact data structure may not be strictly defined, as this time we won't need it to construct specific objects based on it. The only requirements here is that the pickler should be able to handle it, and we must have the ability to stable sort the extra data coming from different producers, to produce stable hash. For this reason, we could assume the extra data format as a simple `Dict[str, Any]`. To register such callback, we can use API similar to previously introduced: ```python def register_extra_cache_key_data(extra_cache_key_data_producer_fn: Callable[[nn.Module, AOTConfig, List[Any], List[Any]], Dict[str, Any]]) -> RemovableHandle: """ Register a callback that emits a dict with extra values to include into the compilation cache key. It will influence both AotAutograd's and Inductor's cache key. The callback is invoked during aot_autograd compilation and provides the caller broad context via the following params: - gm: GraphModule, - aot_config: AOTConfig, - real_inputs: List[Any], - fake_inputs: List[Any]. It must return: - dictionary with extra keys used for generating cache key. Returned dict must be composed of deterministic, picklable primitives. """ # ... pass ``` This function will receive a user-provided callable. The callable shall take as arguments a broad context, including aot_config object, real and fake inputs, and return the dict with extra data. The purpose of this function shall just be to store user's callable in some registry and return standard `RemovableHandle`, so that users can unregister their extra data generation. The registration API is similar to the DynamoCache case, so the usage pattern will be also similar. The nature of this cache hit/miss decision is simpler, so the actual user's code might be a bit easier to understand. #### Usage example To demonstrate usage, let's define a simple test which will use some external data, to trigger [`FXGraphCache`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_inductor/codecache.py#L1210) miss. The test will look very similar for the AOTCache, just the counters' validation will differ, so let's omit that in this document. For the demonstation, the external data can even not be related in any way to the context we're getting in the callback, but of course the real user code will use them to define their own conditions. ```python # file: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/test/inductor/test_codecache.py# # ...standard imports for test/inductor/test_codecache.py... # 0) assume register_extra_cache_key_data is part of the torch._functorch.aot_autograd: from torch._functorch.aot_autograd import register_extra_cache_key_data class TestFxGraphCache(TestCase): @config.patch({"fx_graph_cache": True}) @config.patch({"fx_graph_remote_cache": False}) @torch._functorch.config.patch({"enable_autograd_cache": False}) def test_extra_cache_key_data_affects_fxgraph_cache(self): """ Changing extra_cache_key_data should invalidate the Inductor FxGraphCache key. We inject the extra_cache_key_data via the register_extra_cache_key_data() hook and use runtime-controlled cache_key_value as our extra data. """ # 1) define some simple model to be compiled def fn(x): return x.sin() * 2 + 1 compiled = torch.compile(fn, fullgraph=True) # 2) define user callback which will produce custom extra data to be included in the cache keys. cache_key_value = "A" def extra_key_cache_data_producer(gm, aot_config, real_inputs, fake_inputs): # Let's just use the simplest scenario possible - an external string value to be included in the extra data. # Real user's code will produce the extra data based on the available context. return {"cache_key_tag": cache_key_value} # 3) use the proposed API to include our externally defined data in the cache keys calculation (and unregister in cleanup stage) handle = register_extra_cache_key_data(extra_key_cache_data_producer) self.addCleanup(handle.remove) # 4) verify with counters if this mechnism will trigger cache misses when expected # 4.1) define some helpers for easier assetions def assert_fxgraph_cache_miss(): self.assertEqual(counters["inductor"]["fxgraph_cache_miss"], 1) self.assertEqual(counters["inductor"]["fxgraph_cache_hit"], 0) def assert_fxgraph_cache_hit(): self.assertEqual(counters["inductor"]["fxgraph_cache_miss"], 0) self.assertEqual(counters["inductor"]["fxgraph_cache_hit"], 1) # 4.2) define one and the same input tensor we will use for all runs x = torch.randn(16) with fresh_cache(): # 4.3) first compilation should miss regardless of our extra tag counters.clear() out1 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out1) assert_fxgraph_cache_miss() # 4.4) new run with same tag should hit self.reset() counters.clear() out2 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out2) assert_fxgraph_cache_hit() # 4.5) extra cache key data changed, different tag should trigger miss cache_key_value = "B" self.reset() counters.clear() out3 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out3) assert_fxgraph_cache_miss() # 4.6) going back to previous tag should hit cache_key_value = "A" self.reset() counters.clear() out4 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out4) assert_fxgraph_cache_hit() # 4.7) unregistering the extra data producer, no extra tag should trigger miss handle.remove() self.reset() counters.clear() out5 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out5) assert_fxgraph_cache_miss() # 4.8) changing tag with unregistered producer should NOT trigger miss cache_key_value = "C" self.reset() counters.clear() out6 = compiled(x) self.assertEqual(fn(x), out6) assert_fxgraph_cache_hit() ``` #### Hook implementation There are some bits of design left to be discussed. Let's talk about the place from which the user-registered callbacks will be called and where to store the data gathered from user's code, to be propagated to cache keys calculations. To store the extra data, we could extend the [`AOTConfig`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/schemas.py#L1066) with the extra field: ```python @dataclass class AOTConfig: """ Configuration for AOTDispatcher """ # ... extra_cache_key_data: Optional[OrderedDict[str, Any]] = None ``` Here we're already use the `OrderedDict`, as we should sort all the extra fields from all registered callbacks, to include them always in the same order. Place from where we shall call the users' callbacks, should offer access to the broad context we can pass to users, so that it would be generally useful in making decission about potential recompilation. Access to the real and fake inputs will be valuable, as well as access to the graph module and aot config object. Those are available at the beginning of AOTAutograd stage1 of compilation. The proper place seems to be [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py#L1057), inside the `prepare_aot_module_simplified` function from `torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py`: ```python def prepare_aot_module_simplified( # ... ) -> tuple[ # ... ]: # ... # if any extra cache key data producers are registered, append them to the aot_config # to be eventually passed to _CompileFxKwargs for inclusion in cache key generation for extra_cache_key_producer in _get_registered_extra_cache_key_data(): try: extra_data_dict = extra_cache_key_producer(mod, aot_config, args, fake_flat_args) if aot_config.extra_cache_key_data is None: aot_config.extra_cache_key_data = OrderedDict() logging.getLogger(__name__).debug("Updating extra_cache_key_data with the follwing keys: %s", extra_data_dict.keys()) aot_config.extra_cache_key_data.update(extra_data_dict) except Exception: logging.getLogger(__name__).exception("Failed to add extra cache key data.") # include the extra data always in the same order, regardless of registration order if aot_config.extra_cache_key_data is not None: extra_data_sorted_by_keys = OrderedDict(sorted(aot_config.extra_cache_key_data.items())) aot_config.extra_cache_key_data = extra_data_sorted_by_keys return ( # ... ``` Once we have the data collected and stored in a place we have access to it, it's time to wire it accordingly in the cache systems. In the [`AOTAutogradCache.try_load`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/autograd_cache.py#L744) method, the extra data can be appended to the `fx_config: _CompileFxKwargs` object, [just before](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/autograd_cache.py#L764-L767) creation of the AOTCache key: ```python class AOTAutogradCache(GuardedCache[GenericAOTAutogradResult[Any, Any]]): @staticmethod def try_load( # ... ) -> Callable[..., Any] | None: # ... fx_config: _CompileFxKwargs = { "cudagraphs": cudagraphs, "boxed_forward_device_index": boxed_forward_device_index, } # if there was provided extra_cache_key_data in aot_config, append it to the # _CompileFxKwargs, so it will be pickled when calculating cache key if aot_config.extra_cache_key_data is not None: fx_config["extra_cache_key_data"] = aot_config.extra_cache_key_data # ... ``` Now we also have to include in the Inductor's FxGraphCache. For the purposes of PoC for this RFC, I have passed the extra data to Inductor via `meta` of the graph_module under the `'extra_cache_key_data'` key, just before the [`aot_stage2_compile`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/15dbd457a8f7e15995e753e5d5ff3e04828711f4/torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py#L1161): ```python # ... # if any extra cache key data is included in aot_config, pass it to the meta # of the graph module, so it will be accessible when preparing _CompileFxKwargs # for key cache calculations if aot_config.extra_cache_key_data is not None: aot_graph_capture.graph_module.meta['extra_cache_key_data'] = aot_config.extra_cache_key_data compiled_fn, _ = aot_stage2_compile( # ... ``` Having this data in `meta`, the extra data can be appended to the `fx_kwargs: _CompileFxKwargs` object in the [`FxGraphCache.prepare_key`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c9fcc3ef74aebdd95635637a2bce5de98d4d9b66/torch/_inductor/codecache.py#L1618-L1620): ```python class FxGraphCache(GuardedCache[CompiledFxGraph]): @staticmethod def prepare_key( # ... ) -> tuple[tuple[str, list[str]] | None, dict[str, Any]]: # ... # if there was provided extra_cache_key_data in gm.meta, append it to the # _CompileFxKwargs, so it will be pickled when calculating cache key if "extra_cache_key_data" in gm.meta: fx_kwargs["extra_cache_key_data"] = gm.meta["extra_cache_key_data"] # ... ``` This, together with DynamoCache hook, should be enough to equip the users with the ability of embedding their own custom validity checks regarding cached artifacts. #### Opens There are still some questions left to answer: B.1) just like in the Dynamo case, we don't break the flow when the users' callback would throw, should we add some option to customize this behavior? B.2) Another question is: right now we're propagating the extra data to Inductor's cache from the AOTAutograd hook - should we add a separate, 3rd hook, just for the Inductor's cache? ## **Notes / limitations** By default, nothing changes in the framework's behavior, unless the proposed hooks are used. This is a low-level extension point and is intentionally minimal. Users providing their code to be used in those hooks, are responsible for ensuring the cache usage will be reasonable, for example that extra data provided for Inductor and AOTAutograd cache keys are deterministic and picklable. cc @engineer_17 @EikanWang @gujinghui @jgong5 @wenzhe-nrv @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @amjames @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo @Lucaskabela @bdhirsh @engineer_15_gh @engineer_03

title: Inductor BF16 training crashes with autograd INTERNAL ASSERT (opt_ready_stream && opt_parent_stream) during backward
labels: module: crash, module: autograd, triaged, module: bfloat16, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Training BertForMaskedLM with Inductor backend and bfloat16 precision consistently crashes during the backward pass with an internal autograd engine assertion failure. The failure occurs inside the CUDA autograd engine (engine.cpp:1084) and is reproducible outside of the determinism test harness. **This reproduces the issue reliably when run from the PyTorch repo root:** python benchmarks/dynamo/huggingface.py \\ --backend inductor \\ --bfloat16 \\ --accuracy \\ --only BertForMaskedLM \\ --training \\ --disable-cudagraphs **The run fails during the backward pass with an internal autograd assertion:** cuda train BertForMaskedLM Traceback (most recent call last): File "/pytorch/benchmarks/dynamo/common.py", line 2035, in validate_model self.model_iter_fn(model, example_inputs) File "/pytorch/benchmarks/dynamo/huggingface.py", line 565, in forward_and_backward_pass self.grad_scaler.scale(loss).backward() File ".../torch/_tensor.py", line 631, in backward torch.autograd.backward( File ".../torch/autograd/__init__.py", line 381, in backward _engine_run_backward( File ".../torch/autograd/graph.py", line 869, in _engine_run_backward return Variable._execution_engine.run_backward( RuntimeError: opt_ready_stream && opt_parent_stream INTERNAL ASSERT FAILED at "/pytorch/torch/csrc/autograd/engine.cpp":1084, please report a bug to PyTorch. ### Versions Collecting environment information... PyTorch version: N/A Is debug build: N/A CUDA used to build PyTorch: N/A ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A OS: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9.6 (Plow) (x86_64) GCC version: (GCC) 11.5.0 20240719 (Red Hat title: FlexAttention: CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered
labels: module: crash, module: cuda, triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: flex attention, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug # flex_attention + create_block_mask causes CUDA illegal memory access with pure causal mask (large KV_LEN, small Q_LEN) ## Description I am seeing **CUDA illegal memory access / crash** when using `torch.nn.attention.flex_attention` together with `create_block_mask`, **even with a pure causal mask** that does *not* access any external tensors. This reproduces reliably when: - `KV_LEN` is very large - `Q_LEN` is small (e.g. decode-like) - `create_block_mask` and `flex_attention` are both compiled with `torch.compile` Importantly: - The mask function only does `q_idx >= kv_idx` - No `doc_ids`, `seq_lens`, or any lookup tensors are used - Removing `torch.compile` avoids the crash - Reducing `KV_LEN` or setting `Q_LEN = 1` may avoid the crash This suggests a potential **out-of-bounds access or incorrect indexing** in the generated kernel, even for the simplest causal mask. ## Minimal Reproducible Example ```python import torch from torch.nn.attention.flex_attention import create_block_mask, flex_attention torch.set_default_device("cuda") torch.manual_seed(0) # ---- 1) Pure causal mask: no external tensor access ---- def causal_mask_mod(b, h, q_idx, kv_idx): return q_idx >= kv_idx # ---- 2) Compile ---- create_block_mask_compiled = torch.compile(create_block_mask) flex_attention_compiled = torch.compile(flex_attention) KERNEL_OPTIONS = { "FORCE_USE_FLEX_ATTENTION": True, "BLOCK_M": 16, "BLOCK_N": 16, } def run_once(Q_LEN: int, KV_LEN: int, enable_gqa: bool = True): HEADS = 1 DIM = 64 q = torch.randn(1, HEADS, Q_LEN, DIM, device="cuda") k = torch.randn(1, HEADS, KV_LEN, DIM, device="cuda") v = torch.randn(1, HEADS, KV_LEN, DIM, device="cuda") bm = create_block_mask_compiled( causal_mask_mod, B=None, H=None, Q_LEN=Q_LEN, KV_LEN=KV_LEN, device="cuda", BLOCK_SIZE=(16, 16), ) out = flex_attention_compiled( q, k, v, block_mask=bm, enable_gqa=enable_gqa, kernel_options=KERNEL_OPTIONS, ) torch.cuda.synchronize() print("OK, out.shape =", out.shape) if __name__ == "__main__": # Prefill-like run_once(Q_LEN=7896, KV_LEN=931920) # Decode-like (often crashes) run_once(Q_LEN=2, KV_LEN=931920) ``` ## Error Traceback The following CUDA illegal memory access occurs during `create_block_mask` execution when both `create_block_mask` and `flex_attention` are compiled with `torch.compile`. Paths have been anonymized for privacy. ```text File "/path/to/project/test.py", line 33, in run_once bm = create_block_mask_compiled( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 832, in compile_wrapper return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/nn/attention/flex_attention.py", line 1027, in create_block_mask def create_block_mask( File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_dynamo/eval_frame.py", line 1044, in _fn return fn(*args, **kwargs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_functorch/aot_autograd.py", line 1130, in forward return compiled_fn(full_args) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 353, in runtime_wrapper all_outs = call_func_at_runtime_with_args( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/utils.py", line 129, in call_func_at_runtime_with_args out = normalize_as_list(f(args)) ^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_functorch/_aot_autograd/runtime_wrappers.py", line 526, in wrapper return compiled_fn(runtime_args) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/path/to/python/site-packages/torch/_inductor/output_code.py", line 613, in __call__ return self.current_callable(inputs) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/tmp/torchinductor_generated/code.py", title: [export] while_loop raises GuardOnDataDependentSymNode if any input has data-dependent shape
labels: oncall: pt2, oncall: export, bot-triaged, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug # Problem If any of the inputs passed to `torch.while_loop` has a dimension whose size is data-dependent, `torch.export` fails with `GuardOnDataDependentSymNode`. # Reproduction ```python import torch from torch import Tensor, nn from torch.export import Dim, export class LoopModule(nn.Module): def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor: _, x = torch.while_loop( lambda idx, x: idx < 5, lambda idx, x: (idx + 1, 2 * x), (0, x) ) return x class LoopModuleExt(LoopModule): def forward(self, x: Tensor, mask: Tensor) -> Tensor: # Filter x with mask so that its size is data-dependent x[mask] = super().forward(x[mask]) return x bs = Dim("batch_size", min=1) # This works fine: graph = export(LoopModule(), (torch.rand(3, 4),), dynamic_shapes={"x": {0: bs}}) # This does not work: graph_ext = export( LoopModuleExt(), (torch.rand(3, 4), torch.randint(0, 2, (3,), dtype=torch.bool)), dynamic_shapes={"x": {0: bs}, "mask": {0: bs}} ) ``` # Error ``` GuardOnDataDependentSymNode: Could not extract specialized integer from data-dependent expression u0 (unhinted: u0). (Size-like symbols: u0) Caused by: return func(*args, **kwargs) # _export/non_strict_utils.py:1124 in __torch_function__ (_dynamo/variables/builder.py:3702 in <lambda>) For more information, run with TORCH_LOGS="dynamic" For extended logs when we create symbols, also add TORCHDYNAMO_EXTENDED_DEBUG_CREATE_SYMBOL="u0" If you suspect the guard was triggered from C++, add TORCHDYNAMO_EXTENDED_DEBUG_CPP=1 For more debugging help, see https://docs.google.com/document/d/1HSuTTVvYH1pTew89Rtpeu84Ht3nQEFTYhAX3Ypa_xJs/edit?usp=sharing User Stack (most recent call last): (snipped, see stack below for prefix) File "/home/user/software/anaconda3/envs/pl2/lib/python3.11/site-packages/torch/_higher_order_ops/while_loop.py", line 244, in _while_loop_op_wrapper return while_loop_op(*args, **kwargs) File "/home/user/software/anaconda3/envs/pl2/lib/python3.11/site-packages/torch/_export/non_strict_utils.py", line 1124, in __torch_function__ return func(*args, **kwargs) ``` The full traceback was too long to be included and is [attached as file](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25261513/traceback_while_loop.txt). Additional logs and outputs from `TORCH_LOGS=+dynamo` are [attached as file](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25261390/torch_logs_while_loop.txt) as well. ### Versions PyTorch version: title: [inductor] torch.compile produces incorrect result when conducting broadcast add on complex tensor
labels: triaged, module: complex, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, topic: fuzzer, PT2-Bug-Bash
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug Here is the code to reproduce: ```python import torch torch.manual_seed(0) class Model(torch.nn.Module): def forward(self, x): x_mask = torch.ones(2, 1, dtype=torch.complex64) x = x + x_mask return x mod = Model() x = torch.full((2, 2), 1., dtype=torch.complex64) ''' tensor([[1.+0.j, 1.+0.j], [1.+0.j, 1.+0.j]]) ''' out1 = mod(x) ''' tensor([[2.+0.j, 2.+0.j], [2.+0.j, 2.+0.j]]) ''' out2 = torch.compile(mod, backend='inductor')(x) ''' tensor([[2.+1.j, 2.+1.j], [2.+1.j, 2.+1.j]]) ''' print(out1) print(out2) torch.testing.assert_close(out1, out2, equal_nan=True) ``` Setting backend to `eager` or `aoteager` does not have this issue. ### Error logs AssertionError: Tensor-likes are not close! Mismatched elements: 4 / 4 (100.0%) Greatest absolute difference: 1.0 at index (0, 0) (up to 1e-05 allowed) Greatest relative difference: 0.4472135901451111 at index (0, 0) (up to 1.3e-06 allowed) ### Versions Versions of relevant libraries: [pip3] torch==2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu [pip3] triton==3.6.0 [conda] torch 2.11.0.dev20260209+cpu pypi_0 pypi [conda] triton 3.6.0 pypi_0 pypi cc @engineer_17 @anjali411 @dylanbespalko @mruberry @nikitaved @amjames @chauhang @penguinwu @voznesenskym @EikanWang @jgong5 @Guobing-Chen @XiaobingSuper @zhuhaozhe @blzheng @wenzhe-nrv @jiayisunx @ipiszy @kadeng @muchulee8 @aakhundov @coconutruben @jataylo

title: flex_attention fails for simple mask_mod
labels: triage review, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, module: flex attention, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug I am trying to use `flex_attention` to support KV caching when KV entries are reordered. The following simple code fails with the exception stated below. I am about to release a new library for KV caching and long context gradient computation. I'd love this to be supported by FlexAttention, which looks awesome. But I do not get past this issue here. I've attached code for reproducing the issue. [debug_flex_attention_simple.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25263754/debug_flex_attention_simple.py) The case `do_direct=True` is not so useful to me, since I'd like to use the kernel with different `token_positions` values. I just added it for reference. The code fails in both cases. Here is the output: ``` Computing prefill MHA (cache_length=4096) -13120.0 Computing chunk MHA (chunk_size=1024) Traceback (most recent call last): File "/home/ubuntu/sync/keys_values/keys_values/scripts/debug_flex_attention_simple.py", line 377, in <module> main( File "/home/ubuntu/sync/keys_values/keys_values/scripts/debug_flex_attention_simple.py", line 345, in main attn_outputs_chunk = scaled_dot_product_attention_flexatt( ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File title: [Inductor]Inconsistent suggest_memory_format results when convert_to_reinterpret_view introduces 0-stride
labels: triaged, oncall: pt2, module: inductor, bot-triaged
body: ### 🐛 Describe the bug The tensor slice_2 (size: [1, 64, 640, 959], strides: [78561280, 1, 122752, 128]) is lowered as an ir.SliceView. During the lowering of constant_pad_nd, this ir.SliceView is converted into a ReinterpretView via convert_to_reinterpret_view. In this process, the input for constant_pad_nd is inference as reinterpret_tensor(buf4, (1, 64, 640, 959), (0, 1, 122752, 128), 64) based on index and var_range, where (0, 1, 122752, 128) is tensor.stride it causes torch._prims_common.suggest_memory_format to return inconsistent results. fx_graph_runnable.py ``` import torch # Add by CAMBRICON # end Add by CAMBRICON from torch import tensor, device import torch.fx as fx from torch._dynamo.testing import rand_strided from math import inf import torch._inductor.inductor_prims device_str="cuda" import torch._dynamo.config import torch._inductor.config import torch._functorch.config import torch.fx.experimental._config torch._dynamo.config.suppress_errors = False torch._inductor.config.fx_graph_cache = True torch._inductor.config.size_asserts = True torch._inductor.config.alignment_asserts = False torch._inductor.config.inplace_buffers = False torch._inductor.config.allow_buffer_reuse = False torch._inductor.config.max_autotune_gemm_backends = 'ATEN' torch._inductor.config.autotune_fallback_to_aten = True torch._inductor.config.layout_optimization = True torch._inductor.config.fallback_random = True torch._inductor.config.max_fusion_size = 32 torch._inductor.config.unroll_reductions_threshold = 1 torch._inductor.config.split_reductions = True torch._inductor.config.combo_kernels_autotune = 2 torch._inductor.config.comprehensive_padding = False torch._inductor.config.triton.cudagraphs = False torch._inductor.config.triton.max_tiles = 3 torch._inductor.config.triton.prefer_nd_tiling = True torch._inductor.config.triton.unique_kernel_names = True torch._inductor.config.triton.persistent_reductions = True torch._inductor.config.triton.divisible_by_16 = True torch._inductor.config.triton.store_cubin = False torch._inductor.config.triton.use_block_ptr = True torch._inductor.config.triton.codegen_upcast_to_fp32 = True torch._inductor.config.trace.enabled = False torch._inductor.config.trace.save_real_tensors = False torch._functorch.config.functionalize_rng_ops = False torch._functorch.config.debug_partitioner = False torch._functorch.config.enable_autograd_cache = True torch._functorch.config.fake_tensor_allow_unsafe_data_ptr_access = True torch._functorch.config.unlift_effect_tokens = False torch._inductor.config.cpu_backend = "triton" isolate_fails_code_str = None # torch version: 2.9.1+mlu # torch cuda version: None # torch git version: 5811a8d7da873dd699ff6687092c225caffcf1bb # CUDA Info: # nvcc not found # GPU Hardware Info: # MLU590-M9DK : 1 from torch.nn convert_element_type_42 = convert_element_type_41 = None getitem_74 = convolution_backward_2[0] convert_element_type_51 = torch.ops.prims.convert_element_type.default(getitem_74, torch.float32); getitem_74 = None slice_2 = torch.ops.aten.slice.Tensor(convert_element_type_51, 1, 64, 128); convert_element_type_51 = None constant_pad_nd_4 = torch.ops.aten.constant_pad_nd.default(slice_2, [0, -1, 0, 0]); slice_2 = None upsample_bilinear2d_backward = torch.ops.aten.upsample_bilinear2d_backward.default(constant_pad_nd_4, [640, 958], [1, 64, 320, 479], True, 2.0, 2.0); constant_pad_nd_4 = None return (upsample_bilinear2d_backward,) def load_args(reader): buf142 = reader.storage(None, 147456, device=device(type=device_str, index=0), dtype_hint=torch.bfloat16) reader.tensor(buf142, (64, 128,
